zappy.name: Zappy (Zappy Software)
zappy.summary: Porto, Portugal-based all-in-one online booking and appointment-scheduling SaaS for local service businesses. It is the market leader in Portugal for the beauty/aesthetics sector. Closely adjacent to Guest Overflow's model (commission-free, embeddable booking on the venue's own site, Portugal-born, Portuguese-language support) but its core vertical is appointment-based services rather than restaurant table reservations.
zappy.region: Portugal (HQ in Porto); serves internationally. Portuguese-language product and support.
zappy.product: Cloud appointment scheduling + online booking platform. 24/7 online bookings via a Client App, Instagram, Facebook, Website, or QR code, with no redirect to a marketplace. Features: intelligent calendar (daily/weekly/per-employee views), recurring appointments, room/equipment/group-class management, client records with treatment files, session packs with auto-counting, automated SMS/email/push reminders with delivery reports, online payments (MB WAY, Multibanco, cards), marketing campaigns, multi-location/staff management, advanced reporting.
zappy.targetVerticals: Beauty & wellness (aesthetics, nails, eyelashes, spas, hair salons, barbershops), health (physiotherapy, mental health, nutrition, speech therapy, medical/dental clinics, telehealth), pet care, franchises/multi-location. NOT primarily restaurants.
zappy.commissionModel: Commission-free. Explicitly 'O Zappy nao cobra comissoes por marcacoes online' - the full amount the client pays is the venue's regardless of channel. Revenue is pure subscription.

pricing.model: Monthly subscription tiered by number of service providers; 14-day free trial, no commitment.
pricing.addOns: Premium branded customer app EUR29/mo; certified invoicing modules EUR6.50-12/mo; GDPR data-protection module EUR4/mo; multi-location EUR10/mo.

zappy.competitiveNotesVsGuestOverflow: Same commission-free + embed-on-your-own-site + Portugal/PT-language thesis, but priced far lower (entry EUR16-20/mo vs Guest Overflow Essential EUR99/mo) and sold as self-serve software, not concierge done-for-you. It is a beauty/wellness/health scheduler, not a restaurant table-reservation tool, so it overlaps most with Guest Overflow's spa/salon-adjacent prospects rather than restaurants. Watch-outs for Guest Overflow: Zappy's low price anchors PT buyers' expectations, and it already bundles SMS reminders, online payments and a branded client app. Guest Overflow's differentiation must lean on done-for-you concierge setup, custom website builds, restaurant/hotel/tour-specific flows, and data ownership/export.
zappy.alternativesIfWrongCandidate: Other entities named Zappy exist but are not booking competitors: 'Zappter' (no-code app builder with a reservation template), assorted 'Zappy Pizza' restaurants, and a 'zappy-saas.webflow.io' Webflow template demo. The Porto-based Zappy Software is by far the most likely intended company.

discoveredExtras: CoverManager (Sevilla, Spain; reservations/no-show/CRM; 6,000+ restaurants in 25 countries incl. 100+ Michelin; merged with Zenchef in July 2025)
Bouzon Bookings (Spain; 0% commission per cover with a restaurant website included, from EUR49/mo; positions as a CoverManager alternative)
Octotable (Italy; commission-free cloud table-reservation software + embeddable booking widget, no activation fee, 24/7 sync)
resOS (Denmark/EU-wide; commission-free booking system, free plan, flat paid plans from ~$47/mo, Google Reserve integration, customizable widget)
Tableo (Malta/EU; restaurant booking + table management, free up to 100 covers/month, no credit card)
Formitable (Netherlands; ~30% share of NL restaurants, own-website booking widget; now part of Zenchef group)

profiles.name: Zappy (Zappy Software)
profiles.positioning: "The best and most complete scheduling software" — the commission-free, no-marketplace appointment/booking platform built for Portuguese beauty, wellness and local service businesses ("the customer pays you, the client is yours").
profiles.businessModel: Flat monthly SaaS subscription priced by number of service providers (seats), with no per-booking commission. Revenue is augmented by a la carte paid add-on modules (branded client app, invoicing, GDPR module, extra locations, storage, DB access, consulting). No setup fee, no lock-in contracts, 14-day free trial. Adjacent monetization via electronic payments (MB WAY/Multibanco/cards) and an emerging API/MCP/AI layer.
profiles.pricing: All EUR, per month. Core seat-based tiers (annual price in parentheses): STAR (1 provider) EUR20 (EUR16/mo annual); DUO (2) EUR31 (EUR25); PRO (3-5) EUR42 (EUR33); ENTERPRISE (6-12) EUR75 (EUR60); UNLIMITED (12+) EUR119 (EUR99). No setup fee; no fidelity/lock-in contract; 14-day free trial. Paid add-ons/month: Premium branded Client App EUR29; Simple Invoicing EUR6.50; Advanced Invoicing EUR12; GDPR Data Protection EUR4; Citizen Card Reader cert EUR4; Additional Location EUR10; 50GB Storage EUR4; MySQL DB Access EUR30; Specialist Consulting (360 min/yr) EUR20. Zero commission per online booking, regardless of channel.
keyFeatures: Cloud appointment scheduling (daily/weekly/per-staff agenda views) with sync
Automatic reminders via SMS, email and push (anti no-show / late)
24/7 online booking from website iframe/button, Instagram, Facebook, QR code, or client app
Client records with treatment forms / medical history
Session/treatment packages with automatic counting
Certified invoicing module (optional) + inventory
Electronic payments: MB WAY, Multibanco, cards; full or partial pre-payment
White-label branded client mobile app (paid add-on) with name/logo/colors/photos
Marketing: discount campaigns, promotional messaging
Reports and analytics dashboard
Multi-location and franchise support
16+ industry vertical configurations (salon, barber, esthetics, wellness, health, therapy, pet, etc.)
Newer API, MCP and AI agent layer
GDPR module and Portuguese Citizen Card reader support (PT compliance)
whatWorks: Genuinely commission-free flat pricing — strong, repeated 'no commissions, the money is 100% yours' message that resonates vs marketplaces
Anti-marketplace 'your clients are yours, we never show competitors' data-ownership pitch that operators love
Very low entry price (EUR16-20/mo for a solo professional) — easy yes for small venues
Deeply localized for Portugal: PT-language support team, certified invoicing, Citizen Card, MB WAY/Multibanco — hard for foreign tools to match
Market leader in PT esthetics/beauty with 15,000+ professionals — strong local brand trust and word of mouth
All-in-one (agenda + clients + packs + invoicing + payments + marketing) replaces multiple tools in one subscription
No lock-in contracts + 14-day free trial + free 45-min demo/setup call lowers signup friction
Bookings embed on the venue's own site/Instagram/QR rather than forcing a marketplace listing
weaknesses: Client app rated only 3.3/5 (50 ratings) on the PT App Store — mediocre guest-facing experience
Recurring guest complaint: app no longer lets clients cancel or modify bookings — forces phone calls to the venue (friction the booking tool was meant to remove)
Guests struggle to find/add their salon in the shared 'My Zappy' app even with SMS links and correct data
Update/access bugs reported (app says update available but won't update or grant access)
True white-label branded app is a paid EUR29/mo add-on; the free 'My Zappy' is a shared multi-venue app, so out-of-the-box experience is NOT fully white-label
Nickel-and-diming: core capabilities (invoicing, GDPR, extra location, storage, consulting) are separate paid modules, so real cost rises well above headline tier price
No explicit deposit/card-guarantee no-show protection surfaced in the booking flow — relies on reminders and pre-payment only
Heavily PT-centric; limited evidence of strong EN/international or boutique-hotel/tours fit
Self-serve setup model — no deep done-for-you concierge build beyond a 45-min demo and help-center videos
Almost no third-party review presence (no Capterra/G2/Trustpilot footprint outside PT app stores), making external validation thin
profiles.dataOwnership: Venue owns the data. Zappy explicitly markets "Os seus clientes sao seus" (your clients are yours) and "all customer data belongs exclusively to your business," positioning against aggregator marketplaces that sit between venue and guest. Offers a GDPR data-protection module (paid EUR4/mo) and even MySQL database access as an add-on (EUR30/mo), implying export/portability. No marketplace intermediary on bookings.
profiles.whiteLabel: Partial / tiered white-label. Bookings are taken on the venue's own website (iframe/button), Instagram, Facebook and QR — guests are not redirected to a competitor marketplace, and the venue's brand/colors/logo can be applied. BUT the free consumer app is the shared, multi-venue "My Zappy" app where guests must find their venue (a source of friction and confusion in reviews); a fully branded, venue-named app is a paid EUR29/mo upsell. So it is more white-label than OpenTable-style marketplaces but less seamlessly invisible than a true embedded-on-own-domain widget by default.
profiles.targetSegment: Portuguese (and broader local-service) independent beauty/esthetics salons, hair salons, barbershops, spas/wellness, therapists and health practitioners, pet care, and small franchises — primarily solo operators up to multi-provider/multi-location businesses. Strong esthetics/beauty/wellness skew rather than restaurants, hotels or tours.
profiles.threatToGuestOverflow: High and direct on the spa/wellness segment in Portugal. Zappy occupies almost the exact positioning Guest Overflow uses (commission-free, flat monthly, white-label-ish, 'you own your clients', EU/PT, PT-language support) and is the entrenched market leader with 15,000+ professionals, deep local compliance (certified invoicing, Citizen Card, MB WAY) and very low entry pricing (EUR16-20/mo) that massively undercuts Guest Overflow's EUR99/249 tiers. For PT salons/spas it is the default incumbent. The threat is weaker for restaurants, boutique hotels and tours/activities (not Zappy's focus), and Guest Overflow can differentiate on done-for-you concierge, true on-domain embedding, no-show deposit/card protection, and EN/international + multi-vertical (restaurant/hotel) coverage.
lessonsForUs: COPY the relentless anti-marketplace, 'commission-free, your guests are yours, the money is 100% yours' messaging — it clearly converts PT operators and is core to our story too

DON'T match their low headline price head-on for spas; instead justify our EUR99+ with done-for-you concierge build + quarterly reviews + true on-our-client's-domain embedding + no-show deposit/card protection that Zappy lacks

WIN on guest UX: their client app is only 3.3 stars and guests complain they cannot self-cancel/modify and cannot even find their venue — make Guest Overflow's guest-side self-service (cancel, reschedule, no friction, no app download, no finding-your-venue) a headline differentiator
AVOID their add-on nickel-and-diming (invoicing, GDPR, extra location, storage all cost extra) — our flat tier should bundle the obvious essentials so the quoted price is the real price
DELIVER genuinely white-label by default (lives on the venue's own domain, never 'find us in the Zappy app'); contrast against their shared 'My Zappy' app and paid EUR29 branded-app upsell
MATCH local credibility they own: certified PT invoicing, MB WAY/Multibanco/card payments, Citizen Card, GDPR, and PT-language human support — these are table stakes to compete in Portugal

Lead with no-show protection (deposits + card guarantees) which Zappy's booking flow does not clearly offer beyond reminders — a concrete revenue-protection wedge for restaurants and high-value spa/hotel bookings
Target the segments Zappy under-serves — restaurants, boutique hotels, tours/activities, and EN/international venues — where they have little focus
Keep our no-lock-in + free-trial/founding-offer posture; Zappy proves PT buyers expect no fidelity contracts
profiles.confidence: High on identity, positioning, pricing and white-label/data-ownership (verified directly on zappysoftware.com pricing/feature pages, June 2026) and on the most direct competitor match. Medium on depth of negative-review sentiment: the strongest complaints come from the PT App Store (3.3/5, 50 ratings) and there is almost no Capterra/G2/Trustpilot footprint, so the operator-side (not guest-side) pain points are less independently corroborated. Note: a separate, unrelated 'Zappy Software' shipping/parcel product exists on Capterra (zero reviews) and was ruled out — this profile covers the Porto-based beauty/wellness scheduling Zappy.

profiles.name: TheFork (LaFourchette / a Tripadvisor company)
profiles.positioning: "The leading restaurant booking platform in Europe" — a consumer discovery marketplace (~55,000+ restaurants) that fills empty tables via diner-facing app/website, Tripadvisor distribution, discounts and the Yums loyalty program, bundled with TheFork Manager back-office software.
profiles.businessModel: Hybrid marketplace: a modest monthly SaaS subscription (TheFork Manager) PLUS a per-cover commission on every diner who books through TheFork's own channels (app, thefork.com, Tripadvisor, partners). Direct bookings made through the restaurant's own website/social widget are commission-free. Revenue is therefore weighted toward marketplace commission, not subscription. Additional revenue from prepayment/deposit payment-processing fees and promotional placement.
profiles.pricing: Subscription (varies by market, "ask for pricing"; figures from third-party 2026 sources): Visibility ~EUR29-39/mo; Performance (most popular) ~EUR89/mo; Enterprise ~EUR159/mo. Reported one-time setup/onboarding fee ~EUR400. PER-COVER COMMISSION is the real cost, indexed to average ticket: ~EUR1.50-2.50/cover at a EUR25 ticket; EUR2.50-4/cover at EUR45; up to ~EUR6/cover at EUR80 (commonly cited blended ~EUR2-3/cover, e.g. EUR2.30-2.60). Commission charged on EVERY marketplace cover including repeat guests. Prepayment processing reported around EUR0.95 + EUR0.50 + VAT per transaction. Illustrative total cost: ~EUR8,600/yr (small bistro ~200 covers/mo) to ~EUR35,000/yr (~800 covers/mo). TheFork does not publish exact pricing; rates negotiated per market.
keyFeatures: TheFork Manager: reservation/table management, smart floor plan, online booking widget
Consumer marketplace + app distribution and Tripadvisor integration for discovery
Yums loyalty program (cross-restaurant points) driving repeat traffic
Special Offers engine (20-50% diner discounts to boost visibility/occupancy)
No-show fighting tools: card guarantees, deposits/prepayment (Stripe, Apple Pay), SMS reconfirmation
Performance analytics and guest data collection / CRM (limited)
Dedicated account manager, free onboarding/training, 7-day support
Email/marketing campaign tools (higher tiers), integrations (Brevo, POS)
whatWorks: Massive built-in demand: large consumer audience + Tripadvisor funnel brings net-new diners independents can't reach alone
Strong brand trust in Europe (esp. France, Spain, Italy, Portugal) — being on TheFork is table-stakes for many venues
Pay-for-performance feels low-risk to owners: 'I only pay when I get a booking'
Discounts + Yums demonstrably spike reservations (cited 6.5x page visits / 11x reservations at 50% off)
Concierge-style support: dedicated account manager, free setup and training (a model GO also uses)
Effective no-show mitigation via deposits/card guarantees and SMS reconfirmation
Single platform combining discovery, booking, and back-office management
weaknesses: Per-cover commission compounds painfully at scale and is charged on REPEAT guests you already own — 'most valuable guests become most expensive acquisition channel' (40-60% of TheFork bookings cited as repeat clients)
Promotion/discount spiral: to stay visible you cut 20-50% AND still pay commission, gutting margin (e.g. EUR35 menu nets ~EUR24.50 then minus commission)
Opaque, market-by-market negotiated pricing; ~EUR400 setup fee; payment-processing fees on prepayments — hard for owners to forecast true cost
Diners experience TheFork's brand, not the venue's — restaurant loses control of the guest relationship/experience
Limited CRM / weak guest data ownership — relationship is mediated by the platform; reason cited for switching to ResDiary/Eat App
Algorithm/visibility dependency: rankings opaque; cut promos and your visibility drops
Yums loyalty steers diners to competitor restaurants, not back to yours
Real owner frustration with the commission bill driving churn to commission-free systems; some owners game/cancel bookings to dodge fees
Notably absent / unreviewed on G2 and Capterra (positions as consumer platform, not enterprise software) — limited independent B2B validation
profiles.dataOwnership: Platform-centric. The guest relationship is mediated by TheFork; marketplace diners are TheFork's audience first. CRM/guest-data export and control are limited compared to commission-free systems, and restaurants repeatedly cite weak guest-data ownership and shallow CRM as a reason to leave. Only bookings via the restaurant's own widget escape commission, but discovery customers effectively belong to the marketplace. This is the opposite of Guest Overflow's full data ownership/export promise.
profiles.whiteLabel: Primarily marketplace / platform-branded: diners book on thefork.com, the TheFork app, or via Tripadvisor and see TheFork branding throughout. TheFork does offer a free booking widget that can be embedded on the venue's own website/social (those direct bookings are commission-free), but the product's center of gravity is the marketplace listing, not a venue-branded experience. It is NOT a true white-label system — the platform brand is front-and-center for the high-volume marketplace channel.
profiles.targetSegment: Independent restaurants through to Michelin-starred and restaurant groups across Europe (France, Spain, Italy, Portugal, UK, etc.), with Enterprise tier for multi-location groups. Squarely overlaps Guest Overflow's independent-restaurant target in the same EU/Portugal geography.
profiles.threatToGuestOverflow: High for the restaurant segment, especially in Portugal/EU where TheFork is the dominant default and effectively table-stakes. It owns demand generation (something GO does not provide) and uses pay-per-cover to feel low-risk. BUT it is the textbook 'enemy' GO is built against: commission-driven, platform-branded, weak data ownership. GO does not compete on discovery — it competes on owning your existing/direct traffic commission-free. Realistic positioning: GO complements or replaces TheFork for a venue's OWN/repeat traffic, attacking the most-hated part (commission on repeat guests). Direct threat to GO mainly if TheFork pushes its commission-free direct widget harder; lower threat for GO's hotel/spa/tours segments where TheFork doesn't play.
lessonsForUs: Lead the sales pitch with the exact pain: 'stop paying EUR2-6 every time YOUR regular comes back.' Build a calculator showing TheFork annual commission (EUR8k-35k) vs GO's flat EUR99-249/mo — this is the single sharpest wedge.
Make full guest-data ownership + one-click export a headline feature and contractual promise — directly inverts TheFork's biggest weakness.
Double down on true white-label on the venue's own domain/brand; explicitly contrast with 'guests booking on TheFork's app see TheFork, not you.'
Copy what works: dedicated account manager, free done-for-you setup/training, and strong no-show tooling (deposits, card guarantees, SMS reconfirmation) — keep these in Premium.
Be radically transparent on price (publish EUR99/EUR249) since opaque negotiated pricing + EUR400 setup is a real TheFork friction point.

Acknowledge the gap honestly: GO does NOT generate net-new discovery demand. Position as 'own your direct + repeat traffic commission-free; layer marketplaces only for incremental discovery' rather than claiming to replace discovery.
Avoid the discount-spiral trap — sell margin protection, not race-to-the-bottom promos; frame GO as protecting revenue per cover.
Counter Yums: offer the venue its OWN loyalty/repeat-guest tooling that benefits only them, not a cross-venue program.
Offer an easy migration path / import of guest history for venues leaving TheFork (ResDiary and Eat App already win switchers this way).
profiles.confidence: Medium-high. Commission model, ~EUR2-6/cover indexed to ticket, the three subscription tiers (~EUR29/89/159), ~EUR400 setup, the repeat-guest/data-ownership/marketplace-branding weaknesses, and the discount-spiral dynamic are corroborated across multiple independent 2026 sources and TheFork's own pricing page (which confirms commission-on-marketplace, free-on-direct). Lower confidence on EXACT subscription euros and setup fee: TheFork does not publish them ('ask for pricing'), they vary by market, and the figures come from third-party comparison blogs, not TheFork directly. TheFork's near-absence from G2/Capterra limits independent B2B review depth, so complaint evidence leans on aggregators, switch case studies, and consumer Trustpilot.

profiles.name: OpenTable
profiles.positioning: The dominant reservation marketplace — "fill your tables with diners from the world's largest dining network" — discovery + booking management for restaurants, with OpenTable, not the restaurant, as the consumer-facing brand.
profiles.businessModel: Hybrid: flat monthly SaaS subscription PLUS per-cover commission on every seated diner. The commission is tiered by source — expensive "network" covers (diners who found you via the OpenTable marketplace/app) vs cheaper/free "website" covers (diners booking via your own site). Plus a 2% service fee on transactions (deposits, no-show penalties, prepaid experiences) and takeout. This makes OpenTable a marketplace/lead-gen toll booth, not a pure software vendor: you pay more the busier you get, and the marketplace owns the diner relationship.
profiles.pricing: US plans (USD, as of 2025/2026): Basic $149/mo + $1.50 per network cover + $0.25 per website cover (or $49/mo flat for website covers); Core $299/mo + ~$1.00 per network cover, website covers included; Pro $499/mo + ~$1.00 per network cover, website covers included. Plus a 2% service fee on transactions (deposits, no-show penalties, prepaid experiences) introduced early 2026, and 2% on takeout (Basic/Core). Historically also an installation/setup fee. Contracts are 1-year auto-renewing with a 30-day cancellation window. Real-world total cost balloons: an analysis of a 100-cover venue doing 1,500 network + 1,000 website covers/mo lands at roughly $1,799–$2,649/mo ($21.6k–$31.8k/yr) depending on plan; 1,500 network covers alone = $1,500–$2,250/mo in cover fees on top of subscription. UK no-show/cancellation fees seen at £20/person (<48h notice). EUR pricing for Portugal/EU not separately confirmed (OpenTable has weaker EU presence vs TheFork).
keyFeatures: Consumer marketplace + app driving discovery traffic (~60,000+ restaurants on the network)
Reservation/table management, floor plan, waitlist
Guest CRM / Guestbook with diner history and tags
Reporting and analytics on covers, no-shows, repeat guests
Booking widget for the restaurant's own website (separate, cheaper cover tier)
Deposits, prepaid experiences, and no-show penalty collection (2% service fee)
Takeout ordering (2% fee on Basic/Core)
CSV export of guestbook/reservation data
POS integrations
Loyalty/Dining Rewards points program for diners across the network
whatWorks: Massive demand-generation network — restaurants stay because OpenTable actually delivers incremental diners (especially fills off-peak and slow nights), which a self-hosted widget cannot do alone
Strong brand trust and habit with diners booking through one app across many restaurants
Solid B2B operator tooling and support (4.4-4.7 on Capterra/G2; 8.8/10 support on G2) — operators rate the product highly even while resenting the fees
You only pay network cover fees on seated diners, not on no-shows or cancellations — a fairness point they market hard
Mature feature set: floor plan, CRM, reporting, deposits, POS integrations all in one
Loyalty program (Dining Rewards) gives diners a reason to keep booking via OpenTable
weaknesses: Per-cover fees punish success: 'a busy Friday night could cost more than a slow Tuesday' — costs are unpredictable and climb as the venue grows, with operators having 'no say'
Widely reviewed as 'too expensive' by independents; high-volume venues pay tens of thousands/year, pushing them to Resy/SevenRooms/ResDiary

Data lock-in: OpenTable updated its client agreement (2019) to BLOCK restaurants from sharing diner data with competing platforms (e.g. SevenRooms) without a fee — restaurants compete with their own tech provider for the guest relationship
'System of record' requirement forces all inventory onto the marketplace and prevents full two-way integration with other booking tools, causing manual reconciliation and double-booking risk
Diner-facing reputation is poor: 1.5/5 on Trustpilot — phantom reservations, surprise no-show/cancellation charges ($200 / £120 disputes), multi-week support response, account history not showing
Restaurant's brand is buried — confirmation emails and the booking flow wear OpenTable branding, weakening the venue's direct relationship with the guest
Availability/sync conflicts: marketplace can show open tables that are actually full (and vice-versa)
1-year auto-renewing contract lock-in
profiles.dataOwnership: Contested and effectively platform-favoring. OpenTable's official line: restaurants 'always own their data' and can export the Guestbook to CSV; data the restaurant enters (phone/walk-in) belongs to the restaurant, and marketplace diners own their own data, shared with the restaurant only with consent. BUT in practice OpenTable asserts strong control: it requires being the 'system of record,' it shares marketplace-diner data only conditionally, and in 2019 it amended its agreement to prohibit restaurants from piping that data to competing services without paying — i.e. you can't freely move your guest relationships out. CEO framed restaurant data extraction by rivals as something they're 'morally and legally obligated' to block. Net: the venue gets a copy/export, but OpenTable owns the canonical guest graph and the demand channel, creating real lock-in.
profiles.whiteLabel: Not white-label. OpenTable is a platform/marketplace-first product: the primary booking surface is opentable.com and the OpenTable app, and even the on-site booking widget and confirmation emails carry OpenTable branding, not the restaurant's. The venue is a listing in OpenTable's marketplace, and is required to expose its inventory there. There is a website widget, but it is an OpenTable-branded embed (and a separate cheaper cover tier), not a venue-branded experience where the guest never sees OpenTable.
profiles.targetSegment: Mid-to-high-volume and full-service restaurants in major US/UK metros that value (and can monetize) marketplace discovery traffic; restaurant groups; venues willing to pay per-cover for incremental bookings. Weaker fit for tiny independents (too expensive) and for hotels/spas/tours/EU-Portugal independents (limited footprint; EU is more TheFork territory).
profiles.threatToGuestOverflow: Moderate but mostly INDIRECT, and a strong foil. OpenTable is restaurant-only, marketplace-centric, commission-based, and US/UK-weighted — the opposite of Guest Overflow's white-label, commission-free, venue-owns-data, EU/Portugal, multi-vertical (hotels/spas/tours) positioning. It does NOT compete on Guest Overflow's core promises. The real threat is gravitational: OpenTable's demand network is the one thing Guest Overflow can't replicate, so OpenTable will keep larger/metro restaurants that prioritize new-customer discovery over margin and brand ownership. Where they overlap (an independent restaurant's own-website bookings), Guest Overflow wins decisively on price, branding, and data. OpenTable is best used as the named villain in positioning, not feared as a feature-for-feature rival.
lessonsForUs: COPY the fairness framing but go further: OpenTable boasts 'you only pay for seated diners, not no-shows' — Guest Overflow's flat fee means you pay ZERO per cover, ever. Make the side-by-side cost curve explicit (show a busy venue's OpenTable bill at 1,500 covers ~$1,500-$2,250/mo in fees ALONE vs our flat EUR99-249).

WEAPONIZE the data lock-in: OpenTable literally amended its contract to stop restaurants exporting guest data to rivals. Our 'you own your data, full export, no marketplace in between' is the direct antidote — lead with it.

COPY the genuinely loved operator tooling: floor plan, guest CRM/history, no-show & repeat-guest reporting, deposits/card guarantees. Operators rate OpenTable's software 4.4-4.7 even while hating the fees, so the software bar is high — match it.

AVOID their diner-facing failure modes: phantom reservations, availability/sync mismatches, surprise no-show charges, and multi-week support (1.5/5 Trustpilot). Our concierge + quarterly review is a credibility wedge here.

EXPLOIT the branding gap: their widget and emails say OpenTable. Sell 'guests never leave your site, never see a third party, every email wears YOUR brand' as a tangible loyalty/repeat-business advantage.

BE HONEST about the one thing we can't match — marketplace discovery demand. Don't pretend to replace it. Position Guest Overflow for venues that already have demand (own audience, walk-ins, repeat guests) and want to stop paying a toll on it. Optionally pair with our Google Business Profile optimization as the 'discovery' answer for venues that need new traffic.

TARGET the abandoners: independents calling OpenTable 'too expensive,' venues running multiple channels frustrated by the no-integration 'system of record' rule, and EU/Portugal venues OpenTable underserves.
Mirror their 30-day cancellation but beat their 1-year auto-renew lock-in with our 'no-questions exit' founding offer — make switching risk-free.
profiles.confidence: High on the model, positioning, data-lock-in, and complaint themes (corroborated across official pages, trade press, SevenRooms, and review aggregators). Medium-high on exact US pricing (subscription tiers $149/$299/$499 and cover fees $1.50/$1.00/$0.25 consistent across multiple 2025/2026 sources, but OpenTable's official plans page timed out so figures rely on secondary sources; the 2% transaction service fee is recent). Low/uncertain on EUR pricing for Portugal/EU specifically — not separately published, and OpenTable's EU footprint is thinner than TheFork's.

profiles.name: Resy (American Express)
profiles.positioning: "The reservation network for the restaurants people love" - a flat-fee, no-per-cover reservation/table-management platform paired with a consumer discovery marketplace (resy.com + app) and Amex Global Dining Access for premium cardholders.
profiles.businessModel: Flat monthly SaaS subscription (tiered), explicitly NO per-cover/per-reservation commission - this is its headline differentiator vs OpenTable. Add-on transaction fees apply to ticketed events/prepayments (~2-3%) and POS integration is a paid add-on (~$100/mo). Strategically a customer-acquisition / data asset for American Express (acquired Resy 2019; acquired competitor Tock 2024 for ~$400M), driving Amex card spend and the Global Dining Access cardholder perk rather than pure SaaS profit.
profiles.pricing: USD. Tiered flat monthly: Platform ~$249/mo; Platform 360 ~$399/mo; Full Stack ~$899/mo (figures from third-party comparisons; Resy does not publish prices publicly - requires sales contact). No per-cover fee. Ticketing/events: up to ~3% transaction fee. POS integration add-on: ~$100/mo. Hardware (iPads/stands/networking) typically $400-$2,000 one-time; custom API integrations $500-$2,000. Example total: a 200-cover restaurant on Platform 360 ~$4,788/yr vs OpenTable ~$6,500+/yr. US-centric; limited/unclear EU and Portugal presence and no published EUR pricing.
keyFeatures: Flat-fee reservation + table/floor management (no per-cover charge)

profiles.name: SevenRooms
profiles.positioning: "The guest experience and retention platform" — a CRM-first reservations, waitlist, marketing and data platform for hospitality operators who want to own their guest relationship and drive repeat visits (not just take bookings).
profiles.businessModel: Flat-fee SaaS subscription, NOT per-cover commission. Modular/à-la-carte: you pay per feature module (reservations, waitlist, CRM, marketing automation, online ordering, etc.) layered on top, billed annually. No consumer marketplace and no per-reservation cover charge (a deliberate contrast to OpenTable's per-cover model). Heavy reliance on custom enterprise sales quotes rather than self-serve. As of June 2025 SevenRooms is owned by DoorDash (acquired for $1.2B), so it now feeds DoorDash's broader commerce/ads ecosystem.
profiles.pricing: No public price list — gated behind "contact sales" custom quotes. Directory-reported entry point is around USD $499/month per feature module; one annual single-user estimate cited at ~$5,988/yr (~$499/mo). GetApp lists a nominal "$1/month per feature" starting figure that is effectively a placeholder, with real cost driven by modules + venue size. Reviewers call it "more costly than anticipated." One-time setup/onboarding, floorplan digitization, data migration and training are extra. Independent 3-year total cost of ownership is estimated at roughly $20,000–$100,000+ including implementation. Currency USD. EUR pricing not published. Net: opaque, mid-market-to-enterprise pricing, materially more expensive than Guest Overflow's EUR99–249/mo.

MATCH their no-show toolkit: deposits, card guarantees and cancellation fees are table-stakes they prove customers value — Premium already covers this, keep it.
AVOID feature sprawl/modular upsell pricing that nickel-and-dimes; our flat all-in tier is a differentiator, not a weakness.

profiles.name: Zenchef (Zenchef | Formitable Group)
profiles.positioning: "The commission-free, all-in-one restaurant platform" — own your bookings and guest data instead of paying marketplace commissions to OpenTable/TheFork.
profiles.businessModel: Flat monthly SaaS subscription per location (tiered), explicitly commission-free on reservations. Revenue is augmented by per-transaction payment-processing fees (card 2-4%, iDEAL/Bancontact, AMEX 3.5-4%), per-SMS fees (~EUR0.05-0.11), and paid add-on modules (Marketing Suite, Website, Multi-Venue, AI Call, Meta/Instagram/Google Reserve). They also run a guest-facing discovery app (the Zenchef App) as a free distribution channel, not a commission marketplace. Scale built largely through M&A (merged with Formitable; acquired Resengo, Tablebooker).
profiles.pricing: Pricing is NOT published transparently — gated behind a demo/sales quote. Third-party listings report a flat starting rate around EUR109/month (Capterra/SoftwareWorld) and "from ~EUR49/location/month" for the most basic features (aleno/comparison sites). Three tiers: Reserve (entry, $0 commission, website widget + Google Reserve + app + 24/7 call assist), Manage (most popular; smart floor plans, waitlist, Instagram Reserve, review publishing; payment fees card 4%, AMEX 4%, SMS EUR0.06-0.11), Grow (enterprise; lower payment fees card 2%/AMEX 3.5%, email campaigns, advanced guest management). No setup fee publicly disclosed. Contracts are commonly ANNUAL with auto-renewal. Currency: EUR. Confidence on exact monthly numbers is medium because Zenchef hides them.

profiles.dataOwnership: Venue owns the guest data — this is Zenchef's central selling point. Caveat: Zenchef also operates its own consumer-facing discovery app (Zenchef App) where diners follow venues; bookings from that channel still feed the venue's CRM, but it does create a Zenchef-branded relationship layer. profiles.whiteLabel: Partially white-label. The core booking widget embeds on the venue's own website under the venue's brand (no commission, no forced redirect to a marketplace). HOWEVER Zenchef is NOT a hidden white-label: it also runs the Zenchef-branded consumer app/marketplace as a distribution channel, confirmation emails/widget carry Zenchef branding, and they actively build their own consumer brand. So it is venue-branded on the venue's site, but the Zenchef brand is still visible to guests in places — unlike Guest Overflow's promise that guests never see the platform name.
profiles.targetSegment: Independent restaurants, fine-dining establishments, and multi-location restaurant groups across Europe (France, Netherlands, Belgium, Germany, Spain, Italy, Portugal; offices incl. Lisbon). Heaviest in restaurants specifically — not hotels/spas/tours.
profiles.threatToGuestOverflow: HIGH overlap and a direct threat in the restaurant vertical. Zenchef shares Guest Overflow's two flagship messages almost verbatim — commission-free and venue owns guest data — and already has 15,000-20,000 restaurants, EU/PT presence, and deep funding (PSG Equity). They will be the incumbent Guest Overflow's restaurant prospects compare against. Mitigating factors: Zenchef is restaurants-only (no real hotel/spa/tours play), uses opaque sales-gated pricing, locks customers into annual auto-renew contracts, is self-serve (no concierge build), and is not a true invisible white-label. Guest Overflow can win on transparency, multi-vertical reach, done-for-you concierge, true invisibility, and flexible exit.
lessonsForUs: COPY the headline messaging that clearly works: 'commission-free' + 'you own your guest data' + a hard ROI number (Zenchef quotes 'save ~EUR600/mo in marketplace commissions' and 'no-shows under 1%'). Put a concrete savings/no-show stat on the landing page.

WIN on pricing transparency: Zenchef hides prices behind a demo and frustrates small operators. Guest Overflow already publishes EUR99/EUR249 — make that a loud differentiator ('see our price, no sales call required').
WIN on contract flexibility: their #1 review complaint is annual auto-renew + strict cancellation traps. Guest Overflow's 'no-questions exit' / no lock-in is a direct wedge — feature it prominently.
WIN on support: Zenchef is hammered for slow, bot-like, billing-aggressive support. Lean hard into founder-led concierge + responsive human support as the anti-Zenchef.

WIN on true white-label/invisibility: Zenchef still shows its brand (app, emails, widget). Guest Overflow's 'guests never see the platform name' is a genuine, defensible edge — say it explicitly vs Zenchef.

COPY the all-in-one breadth that justifies the subscription: deposits/no-show protection, floor plan, waitlist, reviews, CRM, marketing. Don't be just a booking form. Premium tier should match deposits + card guarantees + analytics (it does).

AVOID stacking surprise fees: Zenchef's 'commission-free' is undercut by 2-4% payment fees, per-SMS charges, and paid add-ons. Guest Overflow should keep flat pricing genuinely all-in (or be transparent about pass-through payment fees).
AVOID self-serve-only onboarding friction and clunky multi-step flows users complain about — the concierge done-for-you setup is exactly the antidote; market it as 'we build and install it for you'.

EXPAND where Zenchef can't: boutique hotels, spas, tours/activities + GBP optimization and full website builds. Zenchef is restaurant-only; Guest Overflow's multi-vertical + website/GBP services are open territory.

BILINGUAL EN/PT and EU/Portugal focus: Zenchef has a Lisbon office, so don't assume Portugal is uncontested — but Guest Overflow's local, founder-led, bilingual service can out-personalize a PE-backed pan-European platform.
profiles.confidence: Medium-high. High confidence on positioning, model, data-ownership stance, feature set, scale (15-20k restaurants, PE-backed, EU/PT presence), and the real complaint themes (annual auto-renew lock-in, support, widget bugs, opaque pricing) — all corroborated across Zenchef's own site, Capterra, Trustpilot summaries, and comparison sites. Medium confidence on EXACT monthly prices: Zenchef deliberately hides them behind a sales demo; reported EUR49-EUR109/mo figures come from third parties and likely vary by tier, location count, and add-ons. Trustpilot page returned 403 so those quotes are via search-summary, not direct read.

profiles.name: Tock
profiles.positioning: "Reservations, tables, events and experiences in one system" — the prepaid/ticketing reservation platform built for chef-driven fine dining and high-demand venues to eliminate no-shows.
profiles.businessModel: Hybrid SaaS: flat monthly subscription PLUS a percentage fee on prepaid (deposit/ticketed) reservations, layered on top of third-party card processing (Stripe ~2.9%+$0.30 or Braintree ~2.59%+$0.49). No traditional per-diner "cover" commission like OpenTable. Revenue is increasingly skewed toward the prepayment/transaction take-rate, which several owners call "a payment-processor play." Also operates the exploretock.com consumer marketplace for diner discovery. Now an American Express subsidiary (acquired June 2024 for ~$400M from Squarespace), so the longer-term model is tied to Amex's cardmember dining ecosystem.
profiles.pricing: All USD. Current/most-cited table-management tiers (2025-2026): Plus = $199/mo + 2% fee on prepaid reservations; Pro = $699/mo + 0% prepaid fee. Events/ticketing: free "Intro" tier + ~3% per booking, scaling to the Plus/Pro tiers that lower or zero the percentage. An earlier published breakdown listed a wider ladder (Base $99/mo +3%, Essential $269/mo +3%, Premium $339/mo +2%, Premium Unlimited $899/mo +0%), indicating Tock periodically restructures tiers — the through-line is "higher monthly = lower prepaid %." No per-cover fee. Card processing is separate (Stripe 2.9%+$0.30 / Braintree 2.59%+$0.49). Pricing is quote-gated behind a sales team ("Request Plan & Pricing Info"), and setup/onboarding fees are not published — consistent with reviewer complaints about a high-pressure sales process. Confidence on exact live numbers: medium (sales-gated, recently restructured).

profiles.whiteLabel: Not white-label.
