Tock also runs exploretock.com as a consumer-facing marketplace where the venue is a listing for discovery. So bookings are Tock-branded both on the venue's site (widget) and on Tock's marketplace — guests clearly see and transact with "Tock."
profiles.targetSegment: High-end, chef-driven and reservation-critical restaurants; wineries and tasting rooms; experience/event-led venues; high-volume or special-occasion concepts that need deposits, ticketing, and no-show protection. Primarily US-centric.
profiles.threatToGuestOverflow: Moderate-to-high on overlap of intent (commission-free framing, deposits, no-show protection) but LOW-to-moderate as a head-to-head threat to Guest Overflow's actual ICP. Tock targets US fine dining at $199-$699+/mo with a Tock-branded widget, a Tock marketplace, opaque sales-gated pricing, and POS lock-in (Stripe/Braintree) — the opposite of Guest Overflow's EU-focused, truly white-label, flat-fee, venue-owns-everything, concierge model for independent restaurants/boutique hotels/spas/tours. Tock does NOT serve hotels, spas, or tours/activities well and is not bilingual EU-localized. It validates the no-commission + deposits thesis without occupying Guest Overflow's positioning.
lessonsForUs: COPY the prepaid deposit / no-show protection as a headline benefit with hard proof (no-show reduction %, recovered covers) — it's Tock's most-praised, most-credible feature and aligns with our Premium tier card-guarantee.
WIN on white-label: lead with 'guests never see a third party / no logo, no marketplace' — Tock literally won't let venues remove its logo, a concrete, citable contrast.

WIN on transparent flat pricing: publish exact EUR numbers (EUR99/EUR249) vs Tock's sales-gated, restructured, percentage-creep pricing that 'forces you up a tier.' Make 'no per-cover AND no prepaid %' explicit.

WIN on data ownership: offer a written full-export guarantee and venue-owned database — Tock keeps data in-platform and is now under Amex; 'you own it, export anytime, no marketplace' is a sharp differentiator.

EXPLOIT the support gap: Tock's worst reviews are email-only/slow support and misleading sales — our concierge done-for-you setup + quarterly reviews is the direct antidote; market human, founder-led onboarding.
AVOID Tock's narrow ICP and POS lock-in: stay payment-processor-agnostic, serve hotels/spas/tours/restaurants, and localize EN/PT for EU independents Tock ignores.
AVOID opaque/high-pressure sales: keep our 3-months-half-price + no-questions exit as a trust signal explicitly countering Tock's 'high-pressure, misleading' reputation.

BE HONEST about discovery: Tock/OpenTable bring a diner network we don't — counter by pairing white-label bookings with our Google Business Profile optimization so venues get found without surrendering data to a marketplace.
profiles.confidence: Medium-high. Ownership (American Express, June 2024), the prepaid/ticketing model, the Tock-branded widget + marketplace (non-white-label), and the recurring review complaints (high-pressure/misleading sales, email-only support, POS misrepresentation, payment-processor framing) are corroborated across multiple independent sources. Confidence is only medium on the EXACT current monthly numbers because Tock gates pricing behind a sales request and has restructured tiers — sources agree on the Plus $199 +2% / Pro $699 +0% structure but an older ladder ($99-$899) also appears, so live figures should be re-confirmed with a sales quote.

profiles.name: ResDiary
profiles.positioning: "Zero commission, low monthly fee, more bookings" — the established flat-fee, commission-free table-management and reservation platform for restaurants (now owned by The Access Group), positioned as the predictable-cost alternative to OpenTable/TheFork.
profiles.businessModel: Flat monthly SaaS subscription tiered by booking volume (NOT per-cover), plus a long menu of paid add-on modules. Commission-free on all booking channels. Card guarantees/deposits run through Stripe (free unless a card is actually charged, then a small %). Cross-sells a free Dish Cult marketplace listing (ResDiary's own consumer-facing discovery site) on top of the on-site widget. Owned by The Access Group (acquired July 2023), so it's part of a larger hospitality software suite (POS, PMS, payroll cross-sell).
profiles.pricing: GBP, +20% VAT, billed in advance monthly or annually. Base plans named Express, Pro, Connect, Ultimate (also reported as Entry/Standard/Professional/Enterprise tiers), differentiated mainly by bookings-per-month volume. ResDiary does NOT publish full pricing publicly — quotes come after a sales call. Reported real-world figures: entry ~GBP99-109/mo, mid ~GBP149-199/mo, group/pro ~GBP200-400+/mo, enterprise custom; one reviewer reported being billed GBP154/mo vs a ~$69/mo headline. Mandatory 3-month minimum contract reported. Add-ons (each per month): Order & Pay GBP35 (+1% surcharge on payments, 3% on gift vouchers), Gift Vouchers GBP35, ResPhone GBP35, Event Manager GBP35, SMS GBP10, Group Bookings GBP10, POS/PMS/CRM integration GBP10 each; Order&Pay+Event bundle GBP50. No-show tokenization is free unless card charged. Setup/onboarding fee not publicly disclosed.
keyFeatures: Commission-free online booking widget embeddable on the venue's own website, social, Google Reserve
Customisable/brandable widget per venue or across a restaurant group (white-label widgets and emails for groups)
Table-management diary with pacing controls, capacity/yield management, group/multi-location management
No-show protection via Stripe tokenization: store card, charge customisable amount for no-shows/late cancellations, rules by day/time/party size
Deposits, prepaid bookings, Order & Pay, gift vouchers, event manager
CRM / guest database with marketing export, customer profiles, custom fields
Free Dish Cult marketplace listing + microsite for discovery
POS / PMS / CRM integrations (Access Group ecosystem)
ResPhone, SMS reminders, automated confirmations
whatWorks: Genuinely commission-free flat fee — the #1 reason venues switch from OpenTable/TheFork; predictable cost at high volume (flat ~GBP109-200 vs OpenTable ~GBP700+ at 500 covers)
No per-cover charge on any tier
Highly customisable widget that adapts to many venue types; praised by long-term power users
Strong automation and yield/pacing controls that 'think like a restaurant operates'
Reliable cloud platform, no reported outages
Scales to multi-site restaurant groups (9,000+ venues); backing of The Access Group adds an integration ecosystem and longevity
Card-guarantee/no-show tooling is effectively free until you actually charge a no-show
weaknesses: Opaque pricing — no public price list, quote-only after a sales call; reviewers report real bills far above headline (e.g. GBP154/mo vs ~$69 advertised)
Nickel-and-diming via paid add-ons: SMS, gift vouchers, Order&Pay, even POS/PMS/CRM integrations each carry a monthly fee on top of the base plan
Mandatory 3-month minimum contract; sales tactics around trial/availability called misleading
Steep, unintuitive UI — 'badly designed Excel document', deep nested menus, complex shortcut/parameter setup, hard for small venues
Overly complicated onboarding; training described as inadequate
Support drops off after setup; staff turnover hurts service quality; only 2.9/5 on Capterra (small sample, polarised)
Capacity bugs — 'maximum covers exceeded' errors despite available tables
Booking widget UI issues raised on their own support forums
It is a DIY/self-serve tool — no done-for-you build/install or proactive review of no-shows/repeat guests
profiles.dataOwnership: Venue-favourable on paper: ResDiary positions the venue as the GDPR data controller and itself as the processor; venues retain ownership of their guest/CRM database and can export profiles and marketing data (and import from OpenTable). However, ResDiary also operates its own consumer marketplace (Dish Cult) layered on top, which introduces a platform-side discovery surface even though core booking data stays with the venue. Net: strong data ownership claim, but the marketplace co-exists rather than being fully absent.
profiles.whiteLabel: Hybrid. The booking widget IS embeddable and brandable on the venue's own website/social with 0% commission, and groups get white-label widgets and emails — so guests can book on the venue's domain. BUT every customer also gets a Dish Cult microsite/marketplace listing (ResDiary's consumer brand), so it is not purely white-label end-to-end; the platform brand surfaces via Dish Cult. Not a concierge-installed widget — the venue self-configures it.
profiles.targetSegment: Independent restaurants, gastropubs, cafés through to mid-size restaurants and multi-location restaurant groups and hotel F&B outlets; strongest in UK/Europe and ANZ. Skews toward operators who want flat-fee table management and have the staff time to self-configure a fairly complex system.
profiles.threatToGuestOverflow: Moderate-to-high on overlap, lower on Guest Overflow's specific wedge. ResDiary already owns Guest Overflow's two biggest talking points — commission-free booking and a flat monthly fee on the venue's own site — and is a well-funded incumbent (Access Group, 9,000+ venues). It will be the obvious comparison restaurant prospects raise. BUT it is restaurant-only, self-serve/DIY with a notoriously complex UI, opaque quote-only pricing, add-on fees, a 3-month lock-in, and weak post-setup support — and it pushes its own Dish Cult marketplace. Guest Overflow's done-for-you concierge build/install, transparent flat pricing, true single-brand white-label (no competing marketplace), multi-vertical reach (hotels/spas/tours), and quarterly review directly attack ResDiary's worst-reviewed gaps.
lessonsForUs: COPY the commission-free + flat-fee message but go further: publish transparent prices on the site — ResDiary's quote-only, '$69 advertised vs GBP154 billed' opacity is its most-cited grievance and an easy contrast win
AVOID add-on nickel-and-diming: bundle SMS, deposits/card guarantees, integrations into the flat tiers (ResDiary charges monthly for each). Make 'no surprise fees' an explicit promise
AVOID lock-in: ResDiary's 3-month minimum + 'support vanishes after setup' is hated. Lean hard on the founding offer's no-questions exit and ongoing concierge contact

WIN on simplicity/onboarding: ResDiary's 'badly designed Excel' UI and inadequate training are top complaints — the done-for-you build/install and quarterly review is the antidote; market it as 'we set it up, you never touch a settings menu'
WIN on true white-label: do NOT layer a competing marketplace. ResDiary's Dish Cult dilutes its own-site story — Guest Overflow should promise guests never see a platform brand, full stop
MATCH must-have features so we're credible: Stripe-based no-show card guarantees/deposits (free until charged is a good model to copy), pacing/capacity controls, CRM export, group/multi-venue support
COPY the data-ownership framing (venue = controller, full export) and make it sharper — no marketplace intermediary at all
GO multi-vertical: ResDiary is restaurant-only; hotels/spas/tours are open ground for Guest Overflow
Use real review quotes ('maximum covers exceeded', support drop-off) in competitive comparison content
profiles.confidence: Medium-high. High confidence on positioning, commission-free model, add-on price list (from official pricing page), data-controller/processor stance, white-label-plus-Dish-Cult hybrid, Access Group ownership, and the review complaints (multiple independent sources). Medium confidence on exact base-tier monthly prices and the 3-month contract term, because ResDiary deliberately does not publish full pricing — figures are triangulated from third-party comparison sites and individual reviewer reports (in GBP, +VAT), so actual quotes vary by venue/volume/region.

profiles.name: Eat App
profiles.positioning: "Commission-free, all-in-one reservation, table management and guest CRM platform" - the affordable, data-ownership alternative to OpenTable/Resy for independent and multi-location restaurants.
profiles.businessModel: Flat monthly SaaS subscription tiered by cover volume + WhatsApp/SMS allowance, sold à-la-carte with paid add-on modules and per-integration fees. Explicitly commission-free / zero per-cover on its core reservation product (a deliberate anti-OpenTable wedge). The one exception is its optional "Booking Network" discovery add-on, which is the marketplace-style channel and DOES charge ~$2/cover. So: flat-fee core, with an opt-in commission marketplace bolt-on.
profiles.pricing: All USD. Monthly (pay-monthly): Free $0 (up to 100 covers/mo, no SMS/WhatsApp); Starter $99/mo (up to 300 covers/mo, 25 WhatsApp convos, customizable widget, email/WhatsApp marketing); Essential $199/mo (unlimited covers, 250 WhatsApp, Loyalty + CRM); Pro $389/mo (unlimited covers, 500 WhatsApp + 500 marketing msgs, dedicated success manager, POS & payment integrations). Annual billing ~30% off: Starter $69, Essential $159, Pro $299/mo. Enterprise = custom (quarterly business review, centralized multi-location guest data, call-center tools). NO setup fee advertised; 14-day free trial, no card. Add-ons stack on top monthly: Email Marketing $49, Automation $59, Customizable Widget $19, Online Waitlist $19, CRM & Smart Reports $39, Custom Messaging $39, Review Management $15, Chit Printer $179 one-time. Integrations monthly: POS $59, Phone $25, Payment/Deposits $19, Hotel/PMS $80. Marketplace "Booking Network" add-on: $2/cover. Note: their own comparison pages sometimes cite older "$0-$229" / Starter $69 / Pro $239 numbers - current live pricing page is higher (Pro $389/mo).
keyFeatures: Online reservation widget installable on the venue's own website (link, iframe, or popup)
Floor/table management with real-time table status and shift/daily reports
Guest CRM with auto-built profiles, tagging, segmentation, dining history and spend
Automated SMS / email / WhatsApp confirmations, reminders and two-way chat inbox
Email + WhatsApp marketing suites and a Loyalty suite
Waitlist (QR + online) management
Deposits / card capture / no-show protection via Payment integration add-on
POS, phone, and hotel/PMS integrations
Multi-location centralized guest data + enterprise admin panel
Optional Booking Network discovery marketplace (per-cover)
AI features marketed for reservations/guest management
whatWorks: Commission-free / zero per-cover core pricing is the headline reason restaurants switch from OpenTable - predictable flat cost
Genuinely cheaper than OpenTable/SevenRooms at comparable feature depth (Pro ~$2,868/yr framed as a fraction of OpenTable)
Strong, broad feature set (reservations + table mgmt + CRM + marketing + waitlist) in one platform - reduces tool sprawl
Reviewers consistently praise ease of use, intuitive setup and fast, responsive support (notable on Trustpilot/Capterra)
Explicit 'own your customer data' messaging with full export and direct marketing access - resonates with owners burned by marketplaces
Free tier + 14-day no-card trial lowers acquisition friction
Layered guest CRM/tagging enables personalized hospitality, praised by multi-venue groups
Deep regional moat in MENA (Bahrain/Dubai/Abu Dhabi/Doha), 800+ venues, Series B funded
weaknesses: Customer support 'almost non-existent' during initial setup per multiple reviewers - onboarding is self-serve/thin unless you pay up for Pro/Enterprise success manager
Back-end/admin interface described as complex and less user-friendly; learning curve
History of bugs/stability issues (reviewers note 'issues from the beginning' since improved, but reputational)
Widget customization is limited - users couldn't upload logos or rename terminology ('Restaurant' vs 'Cafe'); branding control is shallow
Cannot restrict bookings by table count (only by covers); no per-channel reservation source tracking (FB/IG/PPC)
Pricing perceived as high once you add the modules - the nickel-and-dime add-on/integration model means real cost is well above headline tier (widget, waitlist, CRM, payments all extra)
Weak marketing/diner-discovery presence outside MENA and in the US - thin Booking Network means little inbound demand vs OpenTable
DIY widget install (paste link/iframe) - no done-for-you build/design; IT/owner must implement
Low review volume on G2/Capterra (~9 reviews, 4.2/5) = limited social proof / unproven at scale in Western markets
profiles.dataOwnership: Venue owns its guest data. Eat App explicitly markets "Own your customer data," full unrestricted export, and direct marketing access (email/SMS/WhatsApp) - positioned squarely against OpenTable, which "controls the guest relationship and owns that data." This is a core selling point, not lock-in. The only marketplace-style intermediation is the optional, opt-in Booking Network discovery add-on; the default product keeps the relationship direct.
profiles.whiteLabel: Partially white-label / venue-branded. The reservation widget embeds on the venue's own website (link, iframe, or popup) so guests book on the venue's site rather than a marketplace listing. However, branding control is shallow - reviewers complained they could not upload their own logo or customize terminology in the widget, and the "Customizable Widget" is a paid $19/mo add-on (included only from Pro). So it is venue-facing and not a public marketplace by default, but it is NOT a deeply skinned, fully white-label experience - some Eat App scaffolding/limits remain, and customization costs extra.
profiles.targetSegment: Independent and small-to-mid restaurants (11-50 employees), fine-dining venues, and multi-location F&B groups; strongest in MENA (Bahrain, UAE, Qatar) but expanding globally. Restaurants specifically seeking to escape OpenTable/Resy commissions and own their guest data.
profiles.threatToGuestOverflow: Medium-high overlap on the core promise. Eat App attacks the SAME three pillars Guest Overflow sells: commission-free, flat monthly pricing, and venue-owned guest data with a website-embeddable widget. It is a credible, funded, feature-rich incumbent that already markets nearly identical messaging ("own your data," "zero per-cover"). It is the closest direct analog in this set. BUT it is restaurant-only (Guest Overflow also targets hotels/spas/tours), weakest in Europe/Portugal, self-serve on onboarding, nickel-and-dimes via add-ons, and has shallow widget branding - all gaps Guest Overflow can exploit. It is a threat as a feature/price benchmark more than as an EU-market competitor today.
lessonsForUs: COPY their winning wedge - 'commission-free + own your data' is proven to convert OpenTable refugees; keep hammering it, since Guest Overflow's flat-fee + data export story is identical and arguably purer (truly no marketplace at all, vs Eat App's $2/cover Booking Network)
BEAT them on onboarding - their #1 complaint is 'support almost non-existent at setup.' Guest Overflow's concierge done-for-you build/install is the exact antidote - make that the hero differentiator

BEAT them on white-label depth - reviewers can't even upload a logo and pay $19/mo extra for a 'customizable' widget. Guest Overflow's fully venue-branded, guests-never-see-the-vendor widget is a clean win; include logo/branding/custom flows as standard, not an add-on

AVOID the nickel-and-dime trap - Eat App's real cost balloons via per-module/per-integration add-ons (widget, waitlist, CRM, payments, POS all extra). Guest Overflow's all-inclusive flat tiers (Essential E99 / Premium E249) should be marketed as 'everything included, no surprise add-ons' - a direct contrast

Price-anchor against them: Eat App Pro is ~$389/mo (~E360) before add-ons; Guest Overflow Premium at E249 all-in is materially cheaper for deposits/no-show/analytics/concierge - lead with total-cost-of-ownership comparisons
Exploit segment gaps - Eat App is restaurant-only and MENA-centric. Guest Overflow's EU/Portugal base, bilingual EN/PT, and hotel/spa/tour coverage are uncontested ground here
Add what they lack: reservation-source tracking (FB/IG/PPC attribution) and table-count limits were explicit user requests Eat App hasn't met - cheap features that win deals
Keep the quarterly review - Eat App reserves its 'quarterly business review' for Enterprise/custom only; Guest Overflow offers it in standard concierge, a tangible upmarket-quality perk at SMB price
profiles.confidence: Medium-high. Pricing tiers, add-ons and integration fees come directly from the live pricing page (high confidence, current). Data-ownership and commission-free claims are from Eat App's own marketing plus corroborating third-party comparisons (high confidence on positioning, though "own your data" is their claim). Complaints are real but drawn from a thin public review base (~9 G2/Capterra reviews, 4.2/5) plus Trustpilot summaries - directional rather than statistically deep. Trustpilot and G2 pages returned 403 to direct fetch, so those quotes rely on search-engine summaries. White-label depth inferred from review complaints + install docs; the exact in-widget branding behavior wasn't fully documented on the public install guide.

profiles.name: Tablein
profiles.positioning: "Cheap restaurant reservation system" — an affordable, commission-free, easy-to-use online table booking widget for small and mid-sized independent restaurants, embedded on the venue's own website.
profiles.businessModel: Flat monthly SaaS subscription tiered by included reservation volume, with per-reservation overage fees once the tier cap is exceeded. No commission per cover and no commission on Google Reserve bookings. Add-on variable SMS costs (passed through per country/operator). This is a "flat-plus-overage" hybrid, not a true marketplace commission model. Multi-location discounts available.
profiles.pricing: From tablein.com/pricing (verified June 2026), billed in EUR/GBP/USD: Starter EUR67/mo (GBP60 / USD79) = 50 reservations included, EUR0.67 per extra reservation. Growth EUR117/mo (GBP105 / USD139) = 150 reservations included, EUR0.37 per extra. Success EUR177/mo (GBP157 / USD209) = unlimited reservations, no overage; annual = pay 10 months get 2 free (EUR1,570/yr). No setup/integration fee (installation assistance included). 14-day free trial of premium features, no card required. No contract, cancel anytime. Multi-location: -EUR20 for 2nd location, -EUR30/location for 7+. SMS billed separately (variable). Note: third-party listings cite a lower "from $49 / from EUR37" figure that no longer matches the current public pricing page — likely legacy/promo; current floor is EUR67/mo.
keyFeatures: Embedded, brand-customizable booking widget (logo, colors, fonts, button styles) on the venue's own site — guest never leaves the site
Integrations with WordPress, Wix, Squarespace, Webflow and other site builders
Reserve with Google integration (commission-free) on all plans
Full guest CRM with segmentation, guest categories (VIP, critics, special), and export/print of the guest database
Automated email/SMS confirmations and reminders to cut no-shows
Deposits / instant prepayments and saving card details to secure bookings
Floor plan / table management and digital reservation book accessible on any device
Waitlist when fully booked; custom fields for allergies/occasions/notes; reservation duration by party size
Reports/analytics and reservation source tracking (website, social, campaigns)
Facebook, Instagram, and Michelin Guide booking entry points
Installation assistance + settings optimization advisor included
POS integration listed as 'coming soon' (not yet available)
whatWorks: Excellent, fast, human customer support is the single most-praised attribute (chat replies in minutes, step-by-step tutorials, hands-on setup help)
Genuinely simple, intuitive UI that non-technical small-restaurant owners can run without training
Commission-free positioning resonates strongly with independents burned by OpenTable/marketplace per-cover fees
Low entry price relative to enterprise systems (SevenRooms, OpenTable) makes it accessible to tiny venues
5.0/5 on Capterra (21 reviews), 4.9/5 value-for-money, 100% recommend rate — small sample but very consistent
Automated reminders demonstrably reduce no-shows, a top owner pain point
White-label embed keeps the booking experience on the venue's brand/domain
Bugs from updates are fixed quickly per reviewers — responsive product team
weaknesses: No native mobile app — staff must log in via the browser; repeatedly requested by reviewers
Reservation data does not update live; users report needing to manually refresh and a 'slight time delay on customer history'
Speed/load issues on the custom booking web page reported by more than one user
Limited flexibility on reservation time slots and weak in-app search (e.g. cannot easily search by customer name)
POS integration still 'coming soon' — a real gap vs competitors for revenue/seating analytics
Overage fees on lower tiers can bite a growing venue (EUR0.67/reservation on Starter effectively reintroduces a per-cover-like cost until you upgrade)
Tiny review base (~21) — limited independent validation; almost no presence on G2/Trustpilot/Reddit to triangulate
Minor UX nits: cancellation instructions in emails too small, discount vs event signage unclear
SMS is an unpredictable pass-through cost on top of the flat plan
profiles.dataOwnership: Strong on venue ownership. The restaurant holds a full guest CRM and can export and print the guest database, and there is no marketplace sitting between venue and guest (no Tablein-branded discovery portal taking the customer relationship). Bookings flow through the venue's own site and Google Reserve. This is close to Guest Overflow's own promise — Tablein is NOT a data-hostage marketplace like OpenTable.
profiles.whiteLabel: White-labelled to the venue. The widget embeds directly on the restaurant's own website (WordPress/Wix/Squarespace/Webflow) and is customizable to the venue's brand (logo, colors, fonts, buttons); guests book without leaving the site. There is no consumer-facing Tablein marketplace/listing model — discovery comes via the venue's site, Google, Facebook/Instagram, and Michelin Guide. However, branding is "logo + colors" customization, not necessarily a fully invisible founder-installed experience; Tablein is still a recognizable vendor in the admin/back office.
profiles.targetSegment: Independent small-to-mid-sized restaurants (roughly 2–50 staff) that want commission-free online bookings on their own site without enterprise pricing or marketplace lock-in. Heavily European/UK-leaning (EUR/GBP/USD billing). Not aimed at hotels, spas, tours, or large hospitality groups.
profiles.threatToGuestOverflow: High and direct overlap. Tablein is arguably the closest archetype to Guest Overflow in the restaurant vertical: commission-free, white-label, embedded on the venue's own site, venue owns/exports guest data, flat-ish pricing, friendly hands-on onboarding. It already occupies much of Guest Overflow's intended positioning and at a LOWER list price (Starter EUR67 vs Essential EUR99). The main openings: Tablein is restaurant-only (no hotels/spas/tours), is self-serve SaaS rather than fully done-for-you concierge, has no quarterly performance review, no native app, no live-updating data, weak POS, and reintroduces per-reservation overage fees on cheap tiers.
lessonsForUs: COPY: lean hard into commission-free + venue-owns-and-exports-guest-data — Tablein proves this messaging wins independents, but pair it with a cleaner 'truly flat, no overage surprises' angle since Tablein's overage fees are a soft per-cover cost.

BEAT ON PRICE PERCEPTION: Tablein's real floor is EUR67/mo with overage; Guest Overflow Essential is EUR99 flat with no overage. Make the no-overage, unlimited-bookings story explicit so EUR99 reads as cheaper-at-scale, not pricier.

COPY: world-class fast human support is Tablein's #1 praised feature — Guest Overflow's concierge/done-for-you setup is a natural extension; market 'we build and install it for you' as a step beyond Tablein's self-serve embed.
EXPLOIT: build/advertise a proper staff mobile app and LIVE-updating reservation view — both are recurring Tablein complaints.
EXPLOIT: Tablein is restaurant-only. Guest Overflow's multi-vertical reach (hotels, spas, tours) is a clear differentiation — lead with it for non-restaurant prospects.
EXPLOIT: offer the quarterly review of no-shows/repeat guests — Tablein has analytics but no done-for-you advisory; that is a concierge moat.
AVOID: do not ship a slow-loading or non-live widget; Tablein lost goodwill on page speed and manual refresh.
AVOID: do not hide overage/SMS costs; transparent flat pricing is a wedge against Tablein's add-on SMS and per-reservation fees.
WATCH: Tablein lacks POS integration ('coming soon') — shipping POS/Google analytics integration could leapfrog them.
profiles.confidence: High on pricing, white-label model, data ownership, and feature set (verified directly against Tablein's live pricing and features pages, June 2026). Medium on the depth/representativeness of customer complaints: review evidence is consistent but the public sample is small (~21 Capterra reviews, 5.0/5) with almost no G2/Trustpilot/Reddit presence, so negatives may be under-reported. Note one pricing discrepancy: third-party aggregators still cite a "from EUR37/$49" entry price that the current pricing page contradicts (current floor EUR67/mo) — treated the live page as authoritative.

profiles.name: Quandoo
profiles.positioning: "Discover and book the best restaurants" — a consumer-facing restaurant reservation MARKETPLACE (Recruit Holdings-owned, like sister brand OpenTable) that brings diners to partner restaurants in exchange for per-cover fees. NOTE: As of 24 March 2026 Quandoo is in a planned global wind-down — new bookings phased out 30 Sept 2026, full shutdown 31 Dec 2026.
profiles.businessModel: Hybrid marketplace: monthly flat subscription PLUS per-cover commission on diners delivered through Quandoo's own portal/app and its partner network (e.g. it fed bookings to/from OpenTable). One-time setup fee. Crucially, bookings via the restaurant's own embedded Quandoo widget, "Reserve with Google", and social channels were sold as commission-free — the per-cover fee only applied to Quandoo's marketplace/partner-network demand. So it is a demand-generation marketplace first, software second.
profiles.pricing: Figures vary by country and are partly from third-party/review sources (Quandoo's official pricing page is now 404 due to the wind-down). Commonly cited: two plans ~Pro USD39/mo and Premium USD99/mo, plus a one-time setup fee ~USD99. Per-cover fees by market: UK GBP3.90 (also reported as GBP2.00 by a 2021 reviewer), Australia AUD3.50, Switzerland CHF3 (Pro). UK terms cap annual fee increases at "3.5% OR GBP10, whichever is lower". Bookings via own widget / Reserve with Google / social = free. WIND-DOWN: all service fees waived for the final ~6 months of operation (through end of Sept 2026). Confidence on exact tiers: medium — country-specific and now partly delisted.
keyFeatures: Consumer marketplace (web + app) driving diner demand to partner restaurants
Per-cover billing only on marketplace/partner-network covers
Embeddable booking widget for the restaurant's own website (commission-free covers)
Reserve with Google integration (commission-free)
Partner-network distribution incl. OpenTable integration
Reservation/table management calendar (Quandoo Bookings/merchant dashboard)
Diner reviews and cashback/loyalty points program
Marketing modules (paid add-ons, separately cancellable)
whatWorks: Stylish, easy-to-use consumer interface and fast booking flow consistently praised in diner reviews
Real incremental demand: brings NEW diners a venue would not otherwise reach (the core marketplace value)
Cashback/loyalty points drive repeat diner usage
Commission-free covers via own widget + Reserve with Google is a genuinely attractive hook for cost-sensitive venues
Broad EU footprint (strong in Germany/DACH, UK, Italy, Australia, Asia) gives wide distribution
weaknesses: EXISTENTIAL: shutting down. Recruit Holdings is winding it down after a strategic portfolio review with no buyer — restaurants must migrate off entirely by end of 2026
Hidden/undisclosed per-cover charges: repeated reviewer complaint that venues 'didn't know they'd pay per cover'
Auto-rolling yearly contracts with long cancellation notice (3 months reported); 'continues to direct debit after you quit'
Google widget / extra modules added to listings without clear notice
Billing disputes spanning years, delayed invoicing, unresponsive management on complaints
Diner-side trust issues: bookings confirmed but never reaching the restaurant; loyalty points silently expiring after 6 months
Low review scores: Capterra ~2.0/5 (Value 1.0/5, Support 1.0/5); Trustpilot TrustScore ~2/5
profiles.dataOwnership: PLATFORM-OWNED, not venue-owned. Per Quandoo's own UK terms, the diner is Quandoo's customer, the restaurant only gets a licensed/sublicensable right to use info Quandoo provides, and end users 'submit reviews exclusively to Quandoo.' Reviews and the diner relationship belong to the platform. This becomes catastrophically clear in the shutdown: when Quandoo dies, the restaurant does not walk away with a portable owned guest database from Quandoo's marketplace — it loses the channel and the data lives behind a marketplace that is being switched off.
profiles.whiteLabel: NOT white-label. Primary identity is the Quandoo-branded marketplace and app where diners discover and book; the venue is one listing among competitors. It does offer an embeddable booking widget on the venue's own site, but the brand/relationship/data flow back to Quandoo, and the marketplace listing (with competing restaurants alongside) is the main product. Guests very much see and transact with 'Quandoo,' the opposite of Guest Overflow's brand-invisible model.
profiles.targetSegment: Independent and mid-market restaurants/casual-dining venues across Europe (esp. Germany/DACH, UK, Italy), Australia and parts of Asia that want extra diner demand and table-management tooling. Restaurant-only — not hotels/spas/tours.
profiles.threatToGuestOverflow: LOW and shrinking — Quandoo is exiting the market by end of 2026, so it is not a forward competitor. But it is enormously valuable as (a) a live source of ~migrating, frustrated restaurants Guest Overflow can target NOW, and (b) the perfect cautionary tale validating Guest Overflow's entire thesis: a marketplace that owns your data and charges per cover can vanish overnight and take your channel with it. Different category anyway (marketplace demand-gen vs white-label owned booking).
lessonsForUs: COPY the one genuinely loved hook: commission-free covers via your own widget + Reserve with Google + social. Make 'zero per-cover commission, ever' a headline, since Quandoo only offered it as a carve-out

EXPLOIT the shutdown: run a 'Quandoo is closing — keep your bookings and your guest data' migration campaign with free done-for-you data export/import before Dec 2026; this is a time-boxed lead goldmine

Hammer DATA OWNERSHIP as the differentiator — Quandoo's terms literally make the diner THEIR customer and reviews THEIR property; show venues they truly own/export their guest list with Guest Overflow

AVOID every billing complaint that sank Quandoo: no auto-rolling annual lock-in, no long cancellation notice, no surprise add-on charges, no debiting after cancellation. Guest Overflow's 'no-questions exit' is the exact antidote — market it hard
AVOID hidden per-cover economics: keep flat pricing transparent and total-cost-clear up front
Match the praised UX: invest in a stylish, fast, mobile-first booking flow — that was Quandoo's one consistent positive

Be honest about the gap Quandoo leaves: Guest Overflow does NOT bring new diner demand (no marketplace). Pair the white-label widget with the Google Business Profile / website services to help venues generate their own demand, so ex-Quandoo restaurants don't feel they've lost their discovery channel
profiles.confidence: Medium-high. HIGH confidence on the strategic facts (Quandoo is a Recruit Holdings consumer marketplace, NOT white-label; platform owns diner data/reviews per its own terms; in confirmed global wind-down — new bookings end 30 Sep 2026, full shutdown 31 Dec 2026, fees waived for final 6 months; consistent review complaints on hidden per-cover fees, rolling contracts, billing and support). MEDIUM confidence on exact current pricing tiers and per-cover amounts — they vary by country, some figures come from third-party/review sources, and Quandoo's official pricing page now returns 404 due to the wind-down, so precise live numbers could not be confirmed from the vendor directly.

profiles.name: Formitable (now Zenchef / ZenchefOS) — Lightspeed is only a POS integration partner, NOT the owner
profiles.positioning: "The e-commerce platform for restaurants" — commission-free, white-label online reservations and prepayments embedded on the venue's own website (now consolidated into Zenchef/ZenchefOS as of Jan 2024).
profiles.businessModel: Flat monthly SaaS subscription (NOT per-cover commission on bookings). Reservations themselves are commission-free — this is their headline differentiator vs OpenTable/TheFork. They monetize the subscription PLUS per-transaction fees only on PAID actions (prepaid reservations, deposits, gift vouchers, takeaway orders) layered on top of the payment processor (Mollie) fees. Setup/onboarding fee on top. Important correction: "Lightspeed Reservations" is a misnomer — Lightspeed is a POS integration partner, not the owner. Formitable (Amsterdam, founded ~2015) merged with Zenchef + Resengo in January 2024 to form Zenchef; new development is on ZenchefOS and the legacy Formitable product is frozen (no new features).
profiles.pricing: Legacy Formitable: paid plans started around US$82.35/mo (~EUR79/mo); three tiers Basic / Standard / Pro. Per-transaction fee on paid bookings/vouchers/takeaway: EUR2.99 (Basic), EUR0.99 (Standard), EUR0.00 (Pro) — independent of party size or amount, and EXCLUSIVE of Mollie payment-processing fees. Regular (non-prepaid) reservations are always free, zero per-cover commission. Current Zenchef/ZenchefOS: quote-based, reportedly from ~EUR109/mo, plus a setup/onboarding fee. Reviewers call it "the most expensive reservation system on the market" and flag "odd unexplainable startup fees." Exact current tier numbers are not publicly listed (demo/sales-gated) — medium confidence on Zenchef figures, high confidence on legacy Formitable figures.
keyFeatures: White-label booking widget embedded on the venue's own website (venue branding, guest stays on-site)
Commission-free reservations — no per-cover fee
Integrated prepayments, deposits and credit-card guarantees to fight no-shows (yield management)
Reserve with Google + Instagram/Facebook 'Reserve' buttons (direct channels, still commission-free)
Visual table/seating plan and real-time availability management
Automated SMS + email confirmations and reminders
Guest CRM: profiles, history, preferences, segmentation for marketing/loyalty
Automated feedback/review request emails
Promotions engine: lunch deals, early-bird, group arrangements, gift vouchers
POS integrations (Lightspeed L-series, Trivec, etc.) with auto check-in and receipt linking
Takeaway/online ordering module
Analytics dashboard (occupancy, no-shows, repeat guests)
Multi-venue / restaurant-group support
whatWorks: Genuinely commission-free on bookings — strong, credible wedge against OpenTable/TheFork marketplace economics; venues stay to escape per-cover fees
Booking lives on the venue's own site under its own brand (true white-label), not a marketplace listing
Prepayment/deposit/card-guarantee tooling materially reduces no-shows — a top reason upscale venues pay
Rich guest CRM + automated review/feedback collection builds the venue's own database and reputation
Deep POS integration (Lightspeed/Trivec) auto-checks-in guests and links spend to guest profiles — sticky operational value
Polished, design-led, customizable widget praised for look and ease of use
Scale and credibility: 20,000-25,000+ restaurants, EU footprint (NL/FR/BE), reduces buyer risk
weaknesses: Repeatedly described in reviews as 'the most expensive reservation system on the market'
'Odd unexplainable startup/setup fees that add no value to packages' — opaque onboarding charges
Misleading/opaque pricing — actual tiers are demo/sales-gated, not on the site; per-transaction fees on prepaid bookings/vouchers/takeaway surprise buyers
Reports of promised features that don't exist or don't work properly
Aggressive/forced bundling: customers report being pressured onto the booking system with threats to cut off website-only services, and unexpected cancellation emails with no explanation
Useful features locked behind higher (paid) tiers — e.g. zero per-transaction fee only on the top Pro plan
Booking UX friction: when a date is full it silently offers alternative dates that are easy to miss → accidental wrong-date bookings/no-shows; no 'next available 8pm slot' filter
Disruption/uncertainty from the 2024 Formitable->Zenchef->Resengo merger; legacy Formitable frozen, forcing a migration to ZenchefOS
Payment fees stack on top of Mollie processor fees (double fee layer on prepaid)
Inconsistent satisfaction / low Trustpilot signal across regions
profiles.dataOwnership: Strongly pro-venue and marketed as such: Zenchef explicitly promises restaurants keep control of (A) guest relationships, (B) their data, and (C) their profits, via centralized guest profiles and CRM. Because bookings flow through the venue's own site/channels (not a marketplace), the venue captures guest emails/history directly. Guest/booking data was migrated when moving from Formitable to ZenchefOS. No marketplace middleman skimming the relationship — this mirrors Guest Overflow's promise closely. Caveat: it is still a hosted SaaS, so practical lock-in exists (export terms not prominently published), and reviews mention coercive contract behavior.
profiles.whiteLabel: Yes — white-label, branded to the venue. The booking widget embeds on the restaurant's own website with the venue's branding; guests book without leaving the site. It is NOT primarily a consumer marketplace (unlike OpenTable/TheFork). It also pushes direct channels (Reserve with Google, Instagram/Facebook buttons) that still point back to the venue, not a marketplace listing.
profiles.targetSegment: Independent mid-to-upscale and fine-dining restaurants, plus restaurant groups/chains, primarily in Europe (Netherlands, France, Belgium and broader EU). Venues that take prepayments/deposits and want to escape marketplace commissions while owning their guest data.
profiles.threatToGuestOverflow: HIGH and very direct. This is the closest model to Guest Overflow in the market: same core pitch (commission-free, white-label booking on the venue's own site, venue owns guest data, deposits/no-show protection, CRM). They are restaurant-only and EU-focused — exactly Guest Overflow's primary segment and geography. Their main exploitable weaknesses are price (most-expensive reputation), opaque/surprise fees, aggressive/coercive sales and bundling, frozen-product migration churn, and the lack of a done-for-you concierge model. Note they do NOT cover hotels/spas/tours/activities or generic appointments — leaving Guest Overflow room to differentiate by breadth and by service.
lessonsForUs: COPY: lead with credible, permanent commission-free messaging — it is proven to pull venues off OpenTable/TheFork. Make 'no per-cover fee, ever' a contractual promise, not a slogan.
COPY: prepayment/deposit/card-guarantee no-show protection is a top reason upscale venues pay — keep it central in Premium.
COPY: guest-data ownership + CRM + automated review/feedback requests; frame as 'your database, fully exportable.'

BEAT THEM ON PRICE TRANSPARENCY: publish flat EUR99/EUR249 tiers openly. Their #1 vulnerability is being 'most expensive' with 'unexplainable startup fees' — make Guest Overflow's no-setup-fee, no-surprise pricing a headline contrast.
AVOID: opaque demo-gated pricing and hidden onboarding charges — these are their loudest complaints.
AVOID: coercive bundling / forcing customers onto the booking system and threatening to cut website services — Guest Overflow's 'no-questions exit' is the exact antidote; market it hard.
DIFFERENTIATE WITH CONCIERGE: they are self-serve SaaS with onboarding friction; Guest Overflow's founder-led done-for-you setup + quarterly review is a clear wedge they cannot match at scale.
DIFFERENTIATE ON BREADTH: they are restaurant-only — Guest Overflow can win boutique hotels, spas, and tour/activity operators who have no equivalent commission-free white-label option.
AVOID their booking-UX pitfalls: build a 'next available slot' search and make full-date alternative suggestions explicit (opt-in), to prevent accidental wrong-date bookings/no-shows.
EXPLOIT migration churn: target restaurants unhappy about the Formitable->ZenchefOS forced migration with easy white-glove switching and full data import.
AVOID stacking transaction fees on top of the payment processor for prepaid bookings — keep deposits fee-clean to contrast with their EUR0.99-2.99 per-transaction surcharge.
profiles.confidence: Medium-high. HIGH confidence on the structural picture (commission-free, white-label on venue's own site, restaurant-only, EU focus, venue data ownership) and on legacy Formitable transaction-fee tiers (EUR0/0.99/2.99) and ~$82/mo starting point — these are corroborated across Formitable's own help center, Lightspeed integration page, and multiple review aggregators. MEDIUM on exact CURRENT Zenchef/ZenchefOS monthly tier numbers (from ~EUR109/mo, setup fee, quote-based) because live pricing is demo-gated and not published; figures come from review aggregators (selecthub, wmtips, softwaresuggest) not the vendor's own page. Trustpilot pages returned 403, so review complaints are sourced via search-result extracts and aggregators rather than read in full — directionally reliable but not exhaustively quoted. profiles.name: Superb (Superb Experience)
profiles.positioning: "Guest Experience Management (GXM)" — an all-in-one platform that unifies reservations, POS, payments, takeaway, gift cards and guest marketing so restaurants own the full guest relationship instead of renting it from a marketplace.
profiles.businessModel: Flat monthly SaaS subscription, explicitly commission-free / "never pay per customer" (no per-cover or per-booking fee) — this is identical to Guest Overflow's core pitch. Revenue is layered: (1) software subscription (Plus tier), plus (2) payment processing margin on card transactions (0.99%-1.29% per transaction via their own free card terminal / POS), plus (3) hardware sales (terminals, iPads, printers). The reservation system is NOT sold standalone — it is bundled into the paid "Plus" plan alongside POS/payments, so the real monetization engine is payment processing, not bookings. Effectively a hybrid: flat reservation subscription as the hook, payments take-rate as the profit center.
profiles.pricing: Currency EUR. Two public tiers + enterprise: (1) "Go" = EUR0/mo (free) — POS app + free card terminal only, NO reservation system, 1.29% transaction fee, daily payouts, no lock-in; aimed at shops/cafes/bars/takeaway. (2) "Plus" = price gated behind a "Customize" calculator (not transparently published); third-party comparison sites peg the reservation-bearing entry point at ~EUR79/mo and a higher "growth" tier around EUR149/mo. Plus includes restaurant POS, mobile POS, free card terminal, reservation system, gift cards, takeaway, email marketing, phone support; transaction fee drops to 0.99%. (3) "Enterprise" = custom pricing for venues processing >EUR500,000/yr. No setup fee advertised; 0-month contract / no lock-in claimed. KEY GAP: there is NO commission and NO published reservation-only price — you cannot buy just bookings, and you must adopt their POS+payments to get the full system. Confidence on exact Plus number is medium (gated behind a quote tool; EUR79 figure comes from comparison sites, not Superb's own page).
keyFeatures: Customisable/white-label booking flow embeddable on the venue's own website and channels
No-show protection at no extra cost: prepayments/deposits with custom rules, credit-card guarantee, automated SMS/email reconfirmation
Guest CRM: profiles with allergies, dietary notes, visit history, preferences, surfaced to staff during service
Reservation analytics: trends, returning-guest tracking, month-over-month comparisons
Waitlist management and upsell/add-on sales during the booking flow
Events, pop-ups and ticketing tied to the same guest database
Integrated POS (restaurant + mobile/pocket POS) with reservations auto-dropped onto tables, no separate integration
Built-in payments with free card terminal, flat per-transaction rate, daily payouts
Online takeaway (commission-free) and digital gift cards
Email/guest marketing built on owned guest data
Done-for-you onboarding: 30-min call, they set up the venue, migrate existing reservations + guest data, install the widget on the site, train staff
whatWorks: Commission-free flat pricing resonates strongly with restaurants burned by marketplace per-cover fees (TheFork/OpenTable) — same wedge Guest Overflow uses
Strong, credible 'you own your guest data' narrative — data ownership is front-and-center marketing
High Trustpilot score (~4.5/5 across 100+ reviews) with praise for fast, easy support and being 'different from other platforms'
Done-for-you onboarding (setup, data migration, website install, staff training) lowers switching friction — directly parallels Guest Overflow's concierge promise
All-in-one bundle (reservations + POS + payments + marketing + gift cards) gives operational lock-in and a single dashboard
No long-term contract / no lock-in reduces buyer risk and objection
Well-funded and credible: EUR12M Series A led by Kinnevik, with ex-PayPal/Zettle and ResDiary founder backing — strong European brand momentum
weaknesses: No free tier for the reservation product and a ~EUR79/mo+ entry point that comparison sites repeatedly call 'steep' for small/new independents (vs free-tier rivals like resOS)
Reservation pricing is opaque — gated behind a 'Customize' quote tool, no transparent published price; forces a sales conversation
Reservations are bundled into the POS/payments suite, not sold standalone — venues that just want bookings face 'feature overload' and must adopt Superb's POS and payment processing
Real lock-in via payments: the cheap subscription is subsidized by their payment take-rate (0.99%-1.29%), so the true cost depends on transaction volume and pushes venues onto Superb's processor
Reviews cite slow support response times on time-sensitive issues and some inexperienced/under-trained support staff causing resolution delays
Comparison pages flag missing capabilities vs broader rivals (e.g. review management, deeper marketing campaigns, 24/7 support)
Restaurant-only DNA — built for restaurants/bars/cafes; weak/no fit for hotels, spas, tours/activities (a segment Guest Overflow serves)
Self-serve, multi-product platform model — not a high-touch ongoing advisory relationship; onboarding is a one-time 30-min call, not quarterly strategic reviews
profiles.dataOwnership: Venue-owns-data is a headline claim: "you'll own the data and can target your ticketing to specific customers," with guest profiles, history and notes living in the venue's CRM rather than a marketplace. Practically, however, the data and the booking/payment flow live inside Superb's platform/dashboard — so while it is not a marketplace intermediary skimming the guest relationship, there is operational platform lock-in (especially through payments). It is not a third-party discovery marketplace like TheFork/OpenTable, so there is no marketplace lock-in of the demand side; the lock-in is product/ecosystem-based (POS + payments + CRM all in one vendor).
profiles.whiteLabel: White-label / venue-branded. Booking is a customisable flow embedded on the restaurant's own website and channels — guests reserve on the venue's site in the venue's brand, not on a Superb marketplace listing. There is a "Superb RES" mobile app, but the product is positioned as the venue's own branded booking experience, not a consumer discovery destination. This matches Guest Overflow's white-label positioning closely, though Superb's branding still surfaces in the dashboard/app and payment rails.
profiles.targetSegment: Restaurants, fine dining, cafes and bars (primarily independents and small groups) across Europe, with an Enterprise track for high-volume venues (>EUR500k/yr processing). Originated and strongest in the Nordics/Copenhagen, expanding across Europe. Notably restaurant/F&B-only — does not target hotels, spas, or tours/activities.
profiles.threatToGuestOverflow: High overlap on the core thesis (commission-free flat pricing + white-label booking on the venue's own site + venue owns guest data + done-for-you onboarding), so in the restaurant segment Superb is a direct, well-funded competitor that can out-resource a small agency on brand and product breadth. BUT the overlap is narrower than it looks: Superb monetizes through payments and only really sells you the booking system as part of a full POS/payments/marketing stack — a heavy, opaque-priced, restaurant-only commitment. Guest Overflow can win on (a) transparent flat pricing with no payment take-rate, (b) booking-only simplicity for venues that already have POS/payments and just want bookings, (c) multi-vertical reach (hotels, spas, tours) Superb ignores, and (d) ongoing concierge/advisory (quarterly reviews) vs Superb's one-time onboarding. Net threat: high in restaurants, low/none outside F&B.
lessonsForUs: COPY: lead hard with 'commission-free / never pay per cover' and 'you own your guest data' — Superb proves these messages convert and earn loyalty; make them louder than Superb does.

COPY: their done-for-you onboarding (setup + migrate existing reservations and guest data + install widget on the site + train staff) — but go further with Guest Overflow's recurring quarterly review, which Superb does NOT offer (one-time onboarding only).

BEAT THEM ON PRICE TRANSPARENCY: Superb hides reservation pricing behind a 'Customize' quote tool. Publish Guest Overflow's EUR99/EUR249 flat prices openly — transparency is a direct differentiator and removes a sales-friction objection competitors create.

BEAT THEM ON HIDDEN COST: expose that Superb's 'commission-free' subscription is subsidized by a 0.99%-1.29% payment take-rate and requires adopting their POS/payments. Guest Overflow's pure flat fee with no payment cut and no forced POS switch is a cleaner promise — message it as 'no commission AND no payment cut, keep your existing setup.'

WIN ON SIMPLICITY: many venues suffer 'feature overload' and don't want a full POS migration just to take bookings. Position Guest Overflow as drop-in bookings on your existing site/POS — no rip-and-replace.
WIN ON VERTICAL BREADTH: Superb is restaurant-only. Aggressively own boutique hotels, spas/wellness, and tours/activities where Superb has zero presence.
EXPLOIT SUPPORT WEAKNESS: reviews cite slow/under-trained support. Guest Overflow's founder-led concierge and responsiveness is a concrete wedge — promise and prove fast, expert human support.
AVOID: don't try to out-bundle a EUR12M-funded all-in-one POS platform. Stay focused, transparent, multi-vertical and high-touch rather than competing on feature breadth.
NOTE the no-lock-in playbook: Superb advertises 0-month contracts; Guest Overflow's 'no-questions exit' founding offer matches and should be kept prominent.
profiles.confidence: Medium-high. High confidence on positioning, model (commission-free flat sub + payments take-rate), white-label embed, data-ownership claim, restaurant-only focus, funding (EUR12M Series A, Kinnevik), and the free 'Go' tier with 1.29%/0.99% transaction fees — all verified on Superb's own pricing/product pages and reputable press. MEDIUM confidence on the exact reservation/Plus monthly price: Superb gates it behind a 'Customize' quote tool, so the ~EUR79/mo entry and ~EUR149 growth figures come from third-party comparison sites, not Superb's own page. LOWER confidence on the depth/volume of complaints: Trustpilot is ~4.5/5 and the page was 403-blocked to direct fetch, so negative themes (slow support, under-trained staff) are drawn from search summaries and comparison articles rather than a full first-hand review read; not listed on G2/Capterra with substantial volume.

profiles.name: Cloudbeds
profiles.positioning: "The hospitality management platform" — an all-in-one unified system (PMS + channel manager + commission-free booking engine + payments + guest experience) for independent hotels, hostels, B&Bs and groups worldwide.
profiles.businessModel: Flat/tiered SaaS subscription (priced per property, scaling by number of rooms/units and feature tier) PLUS a payments-processing take rate (Cloudbeds Payments via Adyen) which is where a large share of revenue comes from. The booking engine itself is commission-free; Cloudbeds monetizes the subscription, the payment processing markup, optional paid onboarding, and a post-stay commission only on metasearch-sourced bookings. Hybrid: subscription + payments interchange margin + optional services.
profiles.pricing: No public list price — entirely quote-based ("Request a quote"). Four tiers: Flex (PMS + Payments + marketplace integrations), One (adds Channel Manager + Booking Engine), Experience (adds Guest Experience + Reputation), Enterprise (custom). Third-party estimates: from ~USD 99-108/mo for a small property (up to ~9-10 rooms, 1 user), scaling roughly USD 100-600/mo for ~10-room properties and up to ~USD 1,000/mo at 100 rooms. Optional onboarding/implementation fee ~USD 200 to 5,000+ depending on complexity. Booking engine = commission-free (0% per booking). Payments: advertised as low as 1.40% + USD 0.20 per transaction via default Adyen agreement but real effective rates are higher (interchange-plus or blended); plus ~1% currency-conversion fee on non-USD. Metasearch bookings carry a post-stay commission. Contract length/auto-renewal/early-termination not publicly disclosed — set per individual sales agreement. Currency: USD.
keyFeatures: Cloud PMS (front desk, calendar, folios, housekeeping)
Channel Manager (OTA distribution: Booking.com, Expedia, Airbnb etc.)
Commission-free Booking Engine (Immersive Experience 2.0) embedded on the venue's own site via code snippet
Cloudbeds Payments (integrated PCI-compliant processing via Adyen)
Upsells/add-ons, promo codes, packages, group bookings
Guest experience / messaging tools and Reputation Management (higher tiers)
Analytics and reporting incl. Google Analytics / Facebook Pixel pass-through on own domain
Cloudbeds Websites (optional hosted website product)
Large marketplace of third-party integrations and open API
whatWorks: True all-in-one: PMS + channel manager + booking engine + payments + messaging under one roof, eliminating multi-vendor stitching
Genuinely commission-free direct booking engine — markets ~25% lift in direct bookings and ~30% reduction in OTA commissions
Strong, well-regarded onboarding coaches and guided setup (praised repeatedly in reviews)
High aggregate ratings: ~4.3-4.4/5 across thousands of reviews (Capterra/HotelMinder)
White-labelled booking flow keeps guest on the venue's own domain and brand
Broad integration ecosystem and open API give larger/tech-savvy properties flexibility
Global scale and brand trust — safe default choice for independent hotels
weaknesses: Customer support widely criticized post-sale: 'attentive during setup, then shouting into a void', tickets 'sucked into a black hole', no phone number to call
Opaque, quote-only pricing — no public prices makes comparison hard and erodes trust
Reported aggressive price increases mid-relationship (e.g. '+30%' or 'doubled the price') with pressure tactics and refused refunds on early cancellation
Total cost balloons once payment processing + add-ons stack on top of subscription ('significant monthly expense')
Reliability incidents reported: booking calendar offline for 8+ weeks, lost revenue (~USD 2,000), broken Google/RemoteLock integrations
Reporting bugs (e.g. commission shown as 890%), clunky workflows ('7 clicks' to add an item)
Data-hygiene error reported: imported unrelated hotel's contacts, misrouting guest requests
Outdated knowledge-base documentation; limited customization complaints
Sales team described by some as 'rude and condescending'
~1% currency conversion fee painful for properties serving international guests
profiles.dataOwnership: Venue-favorable: Cloudbeds states the customer always owns their domain/URL and the guest data, with full-funnel analytics on the venue's own domain and no marketplace intermediary on direct bookings. However the data lives inside Cloudbeds' platform (not a venue-owned database), and at least one review reported a data-integrity incident (importing an unrelated hotel's contacts). Export exists but lock-in concern is the breadth of the all-in-one stack — leaving means re-platforming PMS, channel manager, booking engine and payments at once.
profiles.whiteLabel: White-labelled. The Booking Engine is a fully branded widget embedded into the venue's own website via a code snippet; guests stay on the venue's domain and brand throughout — it is NOT a marketplace/aggregator listing. (Cloudbeds also offers an optional hosted website product.)
profiles.targetSegment: Independent and boutique hotels, hostels, B&Bs, vacation rentals, and small hotel groups/multi-property operators globally — generally properties from a few rooms up to ~100+ rooms that want a single unified platform rather than separate point tools.
profiles.threatToGuestOverflow: Moderate and mostly adjacent rather than head-on. Cloudbeds is hotel/room-night-centric (PMS-led) and is overkill for restaurants, spas, tours and simple appointment bookings — Guest Overflow's core segments. Direct overlap exists only for boutique hotels: there Cloudbeds is the incumbent default with commission-free direct booking and a far bigger feature set. But Cloudbeds' weaknesses — opaque quote-only pricing, stacked payment fees, mid-contract price hikes, poor post-sale support, no concierge done-for-you ethos, and complexity — are exactly the gaps Guest Overflow's flat-price, commission-free, founder-installed, concierge model is built to exploit. It validates the 'commission-free direct booking + you own your data + on your own domain' thesis rather than blocking it.
lessonsForUs: COPY their proven core thesis and say it louder: commission-free direct bookings on your own branded domain + you own your guest data — Cloudbeds proves venues want this (25% more direct bookings, 30% less OTA commission).

WIN on pricing transparency: Cloudbeds hides all prices behind quotes and is hated for surprise increases. Publish flat EUR99/EUR249 prices openly and contractually promise no per-cover commission and no mid-term hikes — this is a sharp wedge.

WIN on the hidden payments take: Cloudbeds' real money is the ~1.4%+ processing markup + 1% FX fee stacked on top of subscription. Keep Guest Overflow truly flat and be explicit that there is no payment-processing margin (or pass-through at cost).

WIN on support: their #1 complaint is post-sale support vanishing, no phone number, ticket black holes. Guest Overflow's founder-led concierge + quarterly reviews + a real human is a direct, defensible differentiator — market it as 'a named human who answers, not a ticket queue'.

AVOID their lock-in/complexity feel: keep the widget lightweight, easy to export from, and easy to leave (Guest Overflow's no-questions exit already mirrors this) — contrast with re-platforming an entire all-in-one stack.

AVOID over-scoping: Cloudbeds tries to be everything (PMS, channel manager, payments). Stay focused on the embedded booking widget + concierge for restaurants/spas/tours where Cloudbeds is a poor fit, rather than competing on hotel PMS depth.
AVOID their reliability/trust mistakes: publicized 8-week outages and data-mixing incidents are brand-killers — emphasize reliability, clean data handling, and full export as trust selling points.

Note the gap for non-hotel verticals: Cloudbeds barely serves restaurants/spas/tours — Guest Overflow can own 'commission-free direct booking' for those segments where the hotel incumbents don't reach.
profiles.confidence: medium — High confidence on positioning, commission-free white-label booking engine, data/domain ownership claims, and the nature of complaints (support, opaque pricing, price hikes, payment-fee stacking), all corroborated across official pages and multiple independent review/pricing sources. Lower confidence on exact subscription numbers because Cloudbeds moved to quote-only pricing; the USD 99-1,000/mo figures are third-party estimates, not confirmed list prices, and contract terms are not publicly disclosed.

profiles.name: Mews
profiles.positioning: "The hospitality cloud" — an all-in-one cloud PMS, payments and booking platform built by ex-hoteliers to automate operations for hotels, hostels and serviced apartments.
profiles.businessModel: SaaS subscription (per-room/per-month + base platform fee, tiered) PLUS a heavily monetized payments layer. The real margin engine is Mews Payments: a payment-processing cut on every guest transaction (rate set in contract, on top of interchange/scheme fees) and an embedded-finance marketplace. Booking engine and add-ons are modular up-sells, not bundled. So: hybrid — subscription + take-rate on payments + marketplace app fees. Notably NOT an OTA commission model on direct bookings (no per-cover/per-booking commission on the booking engine itself).
profiles.pricing: No public self-serve pricing; quote-only after a sales demo, on typical 2-year contracts. Corroborated figures: PMS from ~USD 17/room/mo entry tier, with "Mews Commander" cited starting ~USD 30/room/mo; standalone PMS effectively ~EUR 300/mo minimum (without channel manager). Three tiers commonly referenced: Essentials / Advanced / Enterprise (a.k.a. Pro). Mews POS: one-time setup EUR 899/property + EUR 399 for digital ordering, then ~EUR 69/device/mo (or EUR 645/yr). Payments: Mews Payments commission per transaction (undisclosed %, in contract) + interchange + scheme fees; chargeback reserve withheld from payouts. Add-on RMS (Atomize), marketplace apps (1,000+) priced separately. HotelMinder rates it "expensive" vs alternatives. Currency: EUR/USD depending on region.
keyFeatures: Cloud PMS (front desk, housekeeping, rates, multi-property)
Booking engine — embeddable JS widget on own site, Mews-hosted standalone page, or API
Mews Payments — automated/embedded payment processing, deposits, card capture
Guest portal / online check-in & check-out
Channel manager (separate module)
Mews POS for F&B (hotel restaurant/bar)
AI-driven rate recommendations / RMS (Atomize)
Open API + marketplace of 1,000+ integrations/plug-ins
Hostel & serviced-apartment / long-stay management
whatWorks: Strong overall ratings (~4.6+ on Capterra/G2) and brand credibility as a modern cloud platform
Genuinely broad, deep feature set + huge integration marketplace — venues can build a full tech stack on it
Built by ex-hoteliers; resonates with operators who want automation and operational depth
Booking engine can be brand-tailored and embedded, capturing direct bookings and avoiding ~20% OTA commissions
Integrated payments remove manual reconciliation — payment, charge and bill stay in sync
Scales across multi-property groups and segments (hotels, hostels, apartments)
weaknesses: 2-year contract lock-in repeatedly cited as a top reason hoteliers seek alternatives
Payment-processing lock-in — Mews Payments take-rate plus reserves/payouts that bundle fees, refunds and chargebacks confusingly; slow payouts (reports of ~6 days) and held reserves
Steep learning curve / 'clunky, unrefined, not intuitive' UI; constantly opens new browser tabs
Demo-vs-reality gap: features shown in demo (guest-portal cancellations, custom booking add-ons) turn out unavailable in setup
'Marketplace cost creep' — core capabilities gated behind paid add-ons; add-ons restricted enough to cost upsell revenue
Support friction: scheduled calls days out, fragmented email threads, no strong support portal, accountability complaints after failed implementations
Quote-only opaque pricing; expensive; not viable for very small owner-operated venues
Weak for non-hotel verticals — no real product for independent restaurants (only hotel F&B POS), spas, or tours/activities
profiles.dataOwnership: The venue captures and retains guest data via direct bookings/PMS, and Mews markets this as a benefit (capture guest data for marketing/loyalty). There is no OTA marketplace skimming the relationship. BUT lock-in is via the platform and especially Mews Payments, not via owning the contacts — switching away from the PMS and payment processor is the friction point. No marketplace intermediary owns the guest, but practical portability is constrained by the 2-year contract and payments dependency.
profiles.whiteLabel: Partially white-label. The booking engine can be styled to the venue's brand and embedded as a JS widget on the venue's own site (or run as a Mews-hosted standalone page, or via API). It is NOT a public marketplace listing — guests book on/through the venue's brand. However the broader guest portal/check-in experience carries Mews platform conventions, and Mews is positioned as a visible vendor brand to the operator, not invisible. So: brandable booking widget, but a platform-branded operational layer behind it.
profiles.targetSegment: Midscale-to-upscale independent hotels, multi-property hotel groups, hostels and serviced-apartment / long-stay operators in Europe and North America. Medium-to-large properties; explicitly a poor fit for tiny owner-operated venues and for non-accommodation verticals (standalone restaurants, spas, tours).
profiles.threatToGuestOverflow: Low-to-moderate, and mostly indirect. Different weight class and category: Mews is a full hotel PMS/payments platform for medium/large accommodation businesses; Guest Overflow is a lightweight, commission-free, white-label booking widget for small independents across restaurants, hotels, spas and tours. Overlap is narrow — only a small boutique hotel evaluating both — and even there Mews loses on price, contract terms and simplicity. Mews does NOT compete in restaurants/spas/tours where Guest Overflow plays. Main risk: Mews' brand gravity and "all-in-one" pitch could pull a boutique-hotel prospect upmarket. Mews is more a source of lessons than a head-to-head rival.
lessonsForUs: COPY: keep pricing public, flat and self-serve — Mews' quote-only opacity and 'expensive' reputation is a wedge for our transparent EUR 99/249 flat pricing.
COPY/AMPLIFY: make 'no lock-in' a headline — Mews' 2-year contract is its #1 churn driver; our no-questions exit and month-to-month is a direct counter-message.
AVOID: payment-processing lock-in, held reserves and confusing bundled payouts — these generate real anger. Stay commission-free and let venues keep their own processor / get clean payouts.
AVOID: the demo-vs-reality gap — only promise what the widget actually does at install; our concierge done-for-you setup is the antidote (we build it, so there's no overselling).
COPY: lean into 'venue owns its guest data' but go further than Mews — full export, no marketplace, and frame it as portability/no-lock-in, which Mews can't honestly claim.
AVOID: clunky multi-tab UI and steep learning curve — our edge is zero learning curve for the venue (we run setup) and a guest-facing widget that never leaves their site.

EXPLOIT WHITE-SPACE: Mews ignores standalone restaurants, spas and tours/activities — that is exactly Guest Overflow's multi-vertical territory; position there rather than fighting Mews on hotel PMS depth.
AVOID: add-on/marketplace 'cost creep' — bundle the core promises (deposits, no-show protection, analytics, concierge) into clear tiers instead of nickel-and-diming.
COPY: ex-hotelier credibility — Mews' founder-operator story builds trust; emphasize MoggingLabs' hands-on founder-led concierge as the human, EU/local alternative to a big platform.
profiles.confidence: Medium-high. Strong, multi-source corroboration on category, positioning, business model, complaints (2-year lock-in, payments friction, support, demo-vs-reality, add-on creep), and the booking-engine embed/white-label model. Lower confidence on EXACT pricing numbers: Mews is quote-only with no public per-room rates, so figures (~USD 17-30/room/mo, ~EUR 300/mo minimum, POS EUR 899 setup / EUR 69/device) come from third-party aggregators and may vary by region/property; the Mews Payments take-rate % is contractual and not disclosed publicly. The official mews.com pricing page returned 429 (rate-limited) on direct fetch.

profiles.name: Little Hotelier (by SiteMinder)
profiles.positioning: "The easy all-in-one software for small properties" — a cloud PMS + channel manager + commission-free direct booking engine purpose-built for B&Bs, guesthouses and boutiques of roughly 1-25 rooms, backed by SiteMinder's OTA distribution network.
profiles.businessModel: Flat monthly SaaS subscription tiered by number of rooms/properties (not per-booking commission for direct bookings). Revenue is layered: base subscription + optional add-ons (Website Builder, Hotel Metasearch, Channels Plus, Guest Engagement, Dynamic Revenue Plus) + payment-processing transaction fees via "Little Hotelier Pay" (a % of each card transaction) + variable per-booking fees on Metasearch/Channels Plus. So it is subscription-led but with usage-based upsell layers.
profiles.pricing: Subscription billed in USD, tiered by bookable rooms (month-to-month, ~5% off annual). Reported tiers: 1-5 rooms ~USD 109/mo (USD 104 annual); 6-10 rooms ~USD 129/mo (USD 123 annual); 11-15 ~USD 139/mo; 16-20 ~USD 179/mo; 21-30 ~USD 189/mo. Two plan lines: Pro (channel manager, direct booking engine, front desk, mobile app, insights, payments) and a premium tier (variously "Premium" / "Revenue Optimiser") that adds guest-experience and revenue tools, quoted via sales. 30-day free trial, no card required. Direct bookings are commission-free. Payment processing ("Little Hotelier Pay") charges an undisclosed % per transaction (no setup/monthly/refund/cancellation fee claimed) — exact rate not published. Metasearch/Channels Plus carry variable per-booking fees. Prices vary by region/currency; only quote-based exact figures for larger setups.
keyFeatures: Cloud PMS / front desk (calendar, reservations, check-in/out)
Channel manager with SiteMinder OTA connectivity (Booking.com, Expedia, Airbnb, etc.)
Commission-free direct booking engine embeddable on the venue's own website, customizable to brand colours/images, 80+ currencies and multi-language
Booking widget also for Facebook and mobile
Little Hotelier Pay integrated card processing (PCI compliant)
Mobile app for on-the-go management
Insights/reporting dashboard
Add-ons: Website Builder, Hotel Metasearch, Guest Engagement, Dynamic Revenue Plus (revenue management)
Automated pre/post-arrival guest emails, 'Hot Deals' promotions
whatWorks: Genuinely commission-free direct bookings on the venue's own site — the same core promise Guest Overflow makes, validated as a selling point
Purpose-built for tiny properties (1-25 rooms) rather than a stripped enterprise tool — strong product-market fit with owner-operators
All-in-one: PMS + channel manager + booking engine + payments in one subscription removes integration headaches
SiteMinder's mature OTA distribution is a real moat for hotels that depend on Booking.com/Expedia
Flat monthly, no per-cover/per-booking commission, month-to-month option, 30-day free trial lowers entry friction
Ease of use praised once set up (Capterra ~4.1/5); responsive live chat agents often named positively
Cost-effective relative to enterprise PMS for small operators
weaknesses: Customer support is the dominant complaint: slow/inconsistent, chat staff 'not reading messages before responding', complex issues take weeks, onboarding leaves users to figure it out alone
Cancellation is hard: must phone in, 'phone lines do not work half the time'; reports of multiple ignored cancellation emails

Contract/billing horror stories: one user reported being billed 14 months (~USD 1,900) after trying to terminate early; complaints of 'strict contract conditions, no flexibility for early cancellation' despite marketing 'no lock-in'
Double-booking bugs reported, slow to be escalated/acknowledged as system faults
Platform described by some as 'slow, laggy, full of bugs'; app reliability doubts vs desktop
Slow to release/settle funds while 'quick to take commissions'; invoicing overly complex (multi-page invoices), no automatic invoicing, cannot merge duplicate guest records
Hard ceiling: properties outgrow it past ~25-30 rooms and must migrate to Cloudbeds/Mews
Transaction-fee % for Little Hotelier Pay is undisclosed publicly — opacity is itself a friction point
Setup is self-serve/assisted, NOT done-for-you concierge
profiles.dataOwnership: Guest data lives inside the Little Hotelier/SiteMinder platform; the venue accesses its guest records through its account but the platform is the system of record. Direct bookings are commission-free and not intermediated by a marketplace, so unlike OTAs the venue does capture guest contact details directly. However, public materials do not advertise a clean, full-export-and-leave guarantee, and complaints about hard cancellation, surprise billing and migration to other PMSs on growth signal practical lock-in. Net: better than an OTA (no marketplace skim, direct guest contact), but ownership/portability is not an explicit, emphasized promise — closer to "you can see your data in our system" than "your data is yours to take and leave freely."
profiles.whiteLabel: The direct booking engine is white-labelled and embedded on the venue's OWN website, customizable with the venue's brand colours, images and descriptions — guests book on the hotel's site, not a Little Hotelier marketplace. This matches Guest Overflow's model. Caveat: the booking flow is a configurable widget/template (grid-style room/rate display), not a bespoke designed-in experience, and the underlying admin/PMS is clearly SiteMinder-branded to the operator.
profiles.targetSegment: Independent small accommodation providers: B&Bs, guesthouses, small boutique hotels, serviced apartments, typically 1-25 rooms (sweet spot 5-15), owner-operated. Global, multi-currency/multi-language. Not restaurants/spas/tours — purely lodging.
profiles.threatToGuestOverflow: Moderate-to-high overlap on the hotel segment specifically. Little Hotelier already owns Guest Overflow's two headline promises for hotels — commission-free direct bookings + white-label widget on the venue's own site — at a comparable flat monthly price (~EUR/USD 100-190/mo), and adds PMS + OTA channel management that Guest Overflow does not. For a boutique hotel that needs OTA distribution, Little Hotelier is the more complete answer.
