BUT it is hotel-only, has weak/self-serve support, painful cancellation, billing-trust issues, no concierge done-for-you setup, no EU/Portugal local-language hand-holding, and bolts payment % fees and add-on per-booking fees on top. Guest Overflow does NOT compete on channel management, but can win on service, transparency, data-portability guarantee, restaurants/spas/tours coverage, and EU bilingual concierge.
lessonsForUs: COPY the proven core: commission-free direct bookings + brand-skinned widget on the venue's own domain is validated demand — lead with it, but differentiate on how it's delivered.

ATTACK their #1 weakness — support. Their reviews are dominated by slow, scripted, hard-to-reach support and self-serve onboarding. Guest Overflow's founder-led concierge setup + quarterly review is a direct counter; market it as 'a human builds it for you, no ticket queue.'

ATTACK cancellation/billing trust. Their worst stories are 14-month surprise bills and 'easy to join, impossible to leave.' Guest Overflow's no-questions exit and month-to-month should be made loud and contractual — turn their pain into our trust badge.
MAKE DATA OWNERSHIP EXPLICIT. Little Hotelier never clearly promises full export + walk-away. Guarantee one-click full guest-data export and 'your data, leave anytime' in writing — a clean wedge.

BE TRANSPARENT ON PRICE. They hide the payment-processing % and gate the premium tier behind sales. Publish flat numbers (Guest Overflow already does EUR99/EUR249) and state the payment % up front — 'no hidden per-booking fees, no surprise add-on charges.'
AVOID feature bloat that creates bugs/lag. Reviews cite 'slow, laggy, buggy' and double-bookings. Keep the widget lightweight and rock-solid; reliability is a marketing asset.

DON'T try to out-PMS them. Their moat is SiteMinder OTA distribution + full PMS. Guest Overflow shouldn't chase that; instead position as the better-supported, owns-your-data, multi-vertical (restaurants/spas/tours too) commission-free layer for venues that primarily want direct bookings, optionally pairing with their existing tools.

EXPLOIT the >25-room cliff differently — and the segment gap: they are hotel-only and abandon growing properties. Guest Overflow's restaurant/spa/tour coverage + EU/PT bilingual local presence is whitespace they don't touch.
profiles.confidence: medium-high. High confidence on positioning, model, white-label/commission-free direct booking, target segment, and the nature of complaints (support, cancellation, billing) — these are consistent across the official site and multiple independent review/comparison sources. Medium confidence on exact per-room USD tiers (sourced from a third-party pricing breakdown and search aggregation; the official calculator is quote-gated and region-variable) and LOW confidence on the Little Hotelier Pay transaction-fee percentage, which is deliberately undisclosed publicly. Data-ownership/export specifics are inferred from absence of an explicit promise plus lock-in complaints rather than a stated policy.

profiles.name: Fresha
profiles.positioning: "The #1 booking platform for salons and spas" — a marketplace + management suite that promises to fill chairs with new clients via the Fresha consumer marketplace and app, monetized through commissions and payments rather than (originally) subscription.
profiles.businessModel: Hybrid, marketplace-led. Three stacked revenue layers: (1) marketplace commission of 20% on every "new client" booking (min ~$6/£6 per client); (2) payment processing take-rate on card transactions; (3) since 2025, a per-team-member SaaS subscription. Plus paid add-ons (e.g., Data Connector, marketing/SMS). The core economic engine is the marketplace + payments, not the seat fee — the venue is a supply-side listing in Fresha's consumer marketplace.
profiles.pricing: Subscription (introduced/expanded 2025, ending the formerly "free forever" model): Individual ~$19.95/mo; Team ~$14.95 per bookable team member/mo (UK ~£7.99/member); live chat + phone support gated behind a paid premium tier (~$14.95). Marketplace new-client commission: 20%, minimum ~$6/£6 per new client. Payment processing (US): in-person 2.29% + $0.20; online 2.79% + $0.20; manual card 3.30% + $0.20; Tap-to-Pay $0.10/auth. UK processing ~1.2% + 20p. Data Connector (real data export/BI feed) reported at ~$295/mo per location. Net effect: total cost is hard to predict because it layers subscription + per-client commission + transaction fees, and scales up (not down) with success.
keyFeatures: Consumer-facing Fresha marketplace + mobile app that distributes new bookings to listed venues
Online booking (but link-out to a Fresha-hosted page, not embeddable on the venue's own site)
Integrated card payments / POS with Tap-to-Pay
No-show protection: stored cards, deposits, cancellation fees
Calendar, staff scheduling, multi-location management
Automated reminders, marketing blasts, SMS, reviews
Client list import/export to CSV/Excel (but encrypted card-on-file data cannot be exported)
Reporting/analytics; advanced data export via paid Data Connector add-on
Multi-country, multi-language support
whatWorks: Genuine new-customer demand: the marketplace + app actually send first-time bookers, which is the headline reason venues join
Low/zero entry cost historically (was free) made it frictionless to adopt and built a huge installed base
Easy to learn, clean UX — repeatedly praised as user-friendly
All-in-one: bookings + payments + POS + marketing in one place reduces tool sprawl
Broad international/language coverage
Performance-style commission feels 'free until it works' to a new venue (pay only when a new client books)
weaknesses: 20% new-client fee is widely disputed: owners report being charged for people who actually came from their own Google/Instagram/word-of-mouth or were already regulars

Fee mechanics are punitive: it still applies if the client cancels and staff re-book them, and to 'unmanaged'/rescheduled appointments — driving surprise charges (e.g., a £28.80 new-client fee complaint)
2025 end of the free plan angered the long-term base who built their business on 'free forever'
Costs are unpredictable and scale UP with volume — commission + subscription + processing stack on top of each other
Support gated: chat/phone require a paid tier; email support reported slow
Cannot embed booking on the venue's own website — you link out to a Fresha page, weakening the venue's brand
Clients are pushed to create a Fresha account to book — Fresha owns the relationship
Encrypted card-on-file data cannot be exported/migrated, creating switching friction; perception that the system is built for Fresha's revenue, not the salon
profiles.dataOwnership: Mixed and a real pain point. Venues can export the basic client list (name/contact) to CSV/Excel, so it is not total lock-in — but stored card-on-file/payment credentials are encrypted and explicitly cannot be exported or transferred, and the booking/transaction relationship runs through Fresha. Marketplace clients book via a Fresha account, so Fresha effectively co-owns (and remarkets to) the guest. Owners report being told they must surface existing client data (including emails) to the marketplace to stay visible. Net: the venue does NOT cleanly own the end-to-end guest relationship the way a self-hosted system would.
profiles.whiteLabel: Not white-label. The opposite of Guest Overflow. Booking is NOT embeddable on the venue's own domain — the venue links out to a Fresha-hosted booking page, and discovery happens on the Fresha-branded marketplace/app where the guest sees "Fresha," browses competitors, and creates a Fresha account. The venue is a listing inside Fresha's brand, not a branded experience on its own site.
profiles.targetSegment: Independent and chain salons, spas, barbershops, nail/beauty, wellness and personal-care businesses globally — solo practitioners up to multi-location teams. Primarily beauty/wellness rather than restaurants or hotels.
profiles.threatToGuestOverflow: Indirect but instructive overlap. Fresha competes for the same spa/wellness buyers and is the 800-lb gorilla in that vertical, so Guest Overflow will be compared to it. However, Fresha is a marketplace/commission model — the exact thing Guest Overflow positions against. It does NOT do white-label embeds on the venue's own site, does NOT offer flat commission-free pricing, and does NOT give the venue clean data ownership or done-for-you concierge setup. So it is more of a foil to differentiate against than a head-to-head feature competitor. Threat is real only insofar as Fresha's 'free + new clients' pitch is seductive to cash-strapped independents.
lessonsForUs: COPY the proven hook: Fresha wins because it brings NEW customers. Guest Overflow's flat/commission-free angle must still answer 'where do new guests come from' — pair the white-label widget with the Google Business Profile optimization upsell and message it loudly.

WEAPONIZE the 20% new-client-fee outrage: lead with 'never pay a commission on a guest you already own — including your own Google, Instagram and regulars.' This is Fresha's single biggest review complaint.

WIN on predictable pricing: Fresha's stacked subscription + commission + processing is unpredictable and scales up with success. Guest Overflow's flat EUR99/249 is a clean, defensible contrast — show a side-by-side cost-at-volume calculator.
WIN on data ownership: hammer 'full export, you own your guest emails/history, no marketplace in between.' Fresha's non-exportable card data and account-gated guests are a credible attack surface.

WIN on brand/white-label: Fresha can't embed on your own site and forces a Fresha account; Guest Overflow keeps guests on the venue's domain and brand. This is core differentiation — make it the hero message.

AVOID Fresha's bait-and-switch reputation: do NOT ever introduce a surprise commission or kill a 'free' tier later. The 2025 free-plan removal permanently damaged trust — Guest Overflow's 'no per-cover commission' and 'no-questions exit' must be ironclad and never walked back.
COPY no-show protection done well (deposits, card guarantees) — already in Premium; but make the rules transparent so we never replicate Fresha's 'charged even on cancel/re-book' resentment.

Match Fresha's ease-of-use and multi-language (EN/PT already aligned), but differentiate hard with concierge done-for-you setup — Fresha is self-serve and gates support behind paid tiers; our human setup + quarterly review is a moat against the gorilla.
profiles.confidence: high — pricing structure (20% new-client fee w/ min $6/£6, ~$14.95–$19.95 subscription, processing rates), the no-embed/marketplace model, the 2025 end-of-free shift, and the data-export limitation are each corroborated across 3+ independent sources plus Fresha's own help center. Slight uncertainty on exact current regional subscription numbers and the $295 Data Connector figure (single-source), which Fresha varies by market and updates frequently.

profiles.name: Treatwell
profiles.positioning: "Europe's best-loved salon platform" — a hair & beauty marketplace (Treatwell.com) bundled with Treatwell Connect salon-management software; half a million daily searchers and ~150,000 partner salons across Europe used as the customer-acquisition channel.
profiles.businessModel: Hybrid marketplace + SaaS. Low flat monthly software subscription (Treatwell Connect) plus a 35% "finder's fee" commission on the FIRST booking of any NEW client introduced via the Treatwell marketplace (0% on all repeat bookings of that client thereafter), plus a payment-processing fee on online prepayments. Direct/website-widget/social bookings are commission-free aside from the prepayment fee. Revenue is concentrated on new-client acquisition, not ongoing per-cover commission.
profiles.pricing: Software subscription: NL Starter EUR 35/month; UK has tiered plans (Freelancer / Advanced) but GBP monthly figures are not published on the pricing page. Marketplace commission: 35% (+ VAT, i.e. 20% VAT applied to the commission itself, so ~42% all-in on the commission amount — NOT 55%; some bloggers misstate it as "35%+20%=55%") on the first booking of a new marketplace-sourced client; 0% on that client's repeat bookings (365-day rebooking window). Online-prepayment processing fee: 2% (NL) / 2.5% (UK), +VAT. Treatwell Pay card fee cited elsewhere ~1.1% + 20p. Optional professional photoshoot EUR 99. No setup fee advertised. Contract length and free trial not clearly published. Currency: EUR / GBP depending on market.
keyFeatures: Consumer marketplace (treatwell.com) with ~500k daily searchers for discovery
Treatwell Connect salon-management software: online calendar, staff rota, client records
Bookable widget for the salon's own website + Facebook/Instagram booking buttons (commission-free)
Online prepayments / deposits and no-show protection via Treatwell Pay
Daily payouts
Automated booking confirmations and reminders
Business insights / reporting dashboard
Mobile app (Treatwell Connect for salons)
Integrations with third-party salon systems (e.g. Salonized)
whatWorks: Massive built-in demand: half a million daily searchers and ~150k partner salons give genuine new-client discovery that an independent venue cannot generate alone
0% commission on repeat bookings makes the cost feel fair once a client is 'yours' — well-liked positioning ('fair, transparent, 0% on repeat clients')
Strong overall ratings (Capterra 4.4/5, ~285 reviews, ~88% positive) for an intuitive, easy online-booking experience
Low monthly software fee (EUR 35) lowers the barrier to adopt the software
Commission only on the FIRST booking reframes spend as a one-time acquisition cost, not a perpetual tax
Daily payouts and prepayments reduce cash-flow and no-show risk for venues
weaknesses: 35% (+VAT) first-booking commission is widely felt as punishing; owners say they must inflate prices to net a viable margin (e.g. charge ~£97 to keep ~£63 on a massage)

No-show / cancellation policy seen as protecting Treatwell's margin, not the salon — Capterra cancellation-process feedback ~92% negative; reported case of a client cancelling 27 min before with a full refund despite a 48h policy
Customer service is hard to reach after signing — 'only chat, 48-72h response,' no weekend support
Platform reliability complaints — outages during Black Friday 2024 and Feb 2025 with no refunds/compensation
Clients are acquired as 'Treatwell clients' first; the marketplace mediates the relationship and is criticized for not driving repeat/loyal customers back to the salon
Owners report being 'trapped' by functionality despite disliking fees
Reports that Treatwell deactivates in-salon payment so customers must pay online, letting Treatwell hold the funds; payment delays noted
Calendar sync occasionally breaks; can't fully sync with other salon systems
profiles.dataOwnership: Terms state the salon's data 'is and remains the property of your establishment,' and owners can download data before termination (excluding customer reviews); on termination access is lost immediately and export must be arranged via support if not done in advance. BUT in practice the marketplace intermediates the guest relationship — new clients are surfaced and converted on Treatwell's platform, Treatwell retains administrator access to all parts of the software, and reviews/funds flow through Treatwell. So nominal data ownership exists, but real relationship and discovery control sit with the platform (soft lock-in).
profiles.whiteLabel: NOT white-label. The dominant experience is Treatwell-branded: customers 'search for appointments on Treatwell,' salons get a 'digital shopfront' WITHIN treatwell.com. There is a bookable widget for the venue's own site (and IG/FB buttons) that is commission-free, but the platform leads with its marketplace brand and discovery reach, not with the venue's brand. Guests see and transact under Treatwell.
profiles.targetSegment: Independent and chain hair & beauty salons, spas and wellness/treatment providers (incl. freelancers) across Europe (strong UK, NL, ES, IT, FR, DE presence) that want incremental new-client demand from a marketplace.
profiles.threatToGuestOverflow: Moderate but adjacent rather than head-on. Treatwell competes for the same spa/wellness venues and its low EUR 35/mo software fee undercuts Guest Overflow's flat fee on the surface. HOWEVER it is the philosophical OPPOSITE of Guest Overflow: marketplace-branded vs white-label, commission-on-new-clients vs commission-free, platform-mediated relationship vs venue-owns-everything. Venues that resent the 35% finder's fee, the marketplace owning discovery, and poor support are exactly Guest Overflow's switching targets. Treatwell's strength (demand generation) is something Guest Overflow does NOT offer, so it's a competitor for venues that need leads, not for venues that already have their own traffic/brand.
lessonsForUs: COPY the 0%-on-repeat framing as a contrast weapon, but go further: Guest Overflow is 0% on ALL bookings, including the first — make 'never pay a finder's fee on your own guests' the headline

ATTACK the 35% + VAT first-booking commission directly in marketing with the real math (a EUR 100 booking nets the venue ~EUR 58 after commission+VAT) — quantify what a venue loses per year vs flat pricing

LEAN INTO white-label + data ownership: Treatwell's biggest structural weakness is that guests are 'Treatwell clients' on a Treatwell-branded marketplace; Guest Overflow keeps the guest on the venue's domain and brand — make this explicit
FIX the support gap: Treatwell is hated for 'only chat, 48-72h, no weekends' — Guest Overflow's concierge / founder-led setup and quarterly reviews is a direct, ownable differentiator

BUILD salon-FRIENDLY no-show/deposit controls the venue actually controls (real card guarantees, venue keeps the policy) — Treatwell's cancellation handling is its single most-complained-about feature (~92% negative)
BE the anti-lock-in option: offer one-click full guest-data export and the 'no-questions exit' as core trust signals, since Treatwell makes export awkward post-termination
AVOID Treatwell's reliability optics — outages with no compensation erode trust; promise/communicate uptime

DON'T try to out-marketplace Treatwell on demand generation; instead win the segment that already has traffic/brand and resents paying a tax on it. For venues with NO website, route them through Guest Overflow's Google Business Profile / website-build offers rather than a marketplace
profiles.confidence: high — Pricing, commission structure (35% new / 0% repeat + VAT + ~2-2.5% prepayment fee), marketplace-vs-widget model, and review complaints were verified against Treatwell's own NL/UK pricing pages, their terms, and Capterra/competitor sources. Two caveats lowering it from 'very high': (1) UK GBP monthly tier figures are not publicly posted (only NL EUR 35 confirmed), and (2) the all-in commission rate is sometimes misquoted online as '55%' — the accurate figure is 35% + 20% VAT on the commission (~42% effective on the commission, i.e. venue nets ~58% of a new-client booking), which I corrected.

profiles.name: Mindbody
profiles.positioning: "The #1 software for fitness, wellness, and beauty businesses" — an all-in-one management + consumer-marketplace platform that runs scheduling, payments, payroll, marketing and a discovery app for spas, salons, gyms and studios.
profiles.businessModel: Hybrid and the key contrast with Guest Overflow: (1) a per-LOCATION monthly SaaS subscription (4 tiers), PLUS (2) payment processing taken in-house at ~2.75-3.6%, PLUS (3) a 20% MARKETPLACE COMMISSION on the first purchase of any client Mindbody's app/marketplace sends you (capped at ~$30), PLUS (4) paid add-ons (branded app, marketing suite). So Mindbody is simultaneously your software vendor, your payment processor, and a commission-taking marketplace that also routes your clients to competitors. Revenue compounds: more locations = more base fees; more bookings = more processing %; marketplace = a per-acquisition cut.
profiles.pricing: USD, per location, billed best on annual contracts (12-month lock-in). Tiers (reported ranges; Mindbody hides exact pricing behind sales calls): Starter ~$99-$159/mo; Accelerate ~$259-$279/mo; Ultimate ~$499/mo; Ultimate Plus ~$699+/mo. EU storefront lists "from EUR99/month per location." Payment processing 2.75%-3.6% per transaction. Marketplace commission: 20% of a marketplace-acquired client's first purchase, capped at ~$30. Branded mobile app add-on ~$75-$250+/mo. All-in real cost: a single small studio commonly reports $300-$500+/mo; a 2-location boutique $1,000-$1,700/mo. Onboarding can take up to 8 weeks. Note: figures are cross-referenced reported numbers; Mindbody does not publish a transparent price list.
keyFeatures: Class/appointment scheduling and calendar management
Integrated payment processing (in-house, not BYO processor)
Branded web widgets (embed booking/calendar/purchase on the venue's own Squarespace/Wix/WordPress site, color/font matched, transactions on-page)
Consumer Mindbody app + marketplace (discovery channel that lists the venue to a wide audience)
Optional branded native mobile app (paid add-on)
Email/SMS marketing, promos, lead-management dashboard (higher tiers)
Staff/payroll, room & resource management
Reporting and analytics (basic to advanced by tier)
Memberships, packages, retail/POS
whatWorks: Massive consumer marketplace + app drives genuine new-client discovery — the single biggest reason wellness businesses tolerate Mindbody
True all-in-one: scheduling, payments, payroll, retail, marketing in one system reduces tool sprawl
Branded web widgets keep booking and payment on the venue's own site (no leaving to a third-party page for the transaction)
Deep feature breadth and many third-party integrations; de facto industry standard, so staff/clients already know it
Strong for multi-location and complex class/membership operations once configured
weaknesses: Expensive and opaque — no public pricing, costs creep (users report jumping from ~$220 to ~$480/mo, ~$1k all-in with processing)
20% marketplace commission AND in-house payment fees stack on top of subscription — effective ~23.5% take on marketplace-acquired clients
12-month lock-in contracts; numerous Trustpilot/BBB complaints about being refused cancellation, charged after cancel requests, billed during/after botched onboarding
Slow, low-quality support (3+ business days; reps often can't resolve; work orders open 8+ months)
Software feels dated/clunky; broken booking links, lost client data on import, login loops reported
Capterra value-for-money rating only ~3.6 — its weakest score

Data-ownership concern: marketplace routes YOUR clients to competing businesses when you're full; privacy policy allows sharing client data for cross-contextual/targeted advertising (arguably a CCPA 'sale')
Per-location pricing punishes growth — every new location pays full base rate
profiles.dataOwnership: Mixed/platform-leaning, and a real vulnerability. Operationally the venue can access and export client lists, but the marketplace model means Mindbody actively uses the client relationship for its own ends: it surfaces YOUR clients to competing businesses when you are fully booked, and its privacy policy permits sharing personal data for cross-contextual/targeted advertising (which under CCPA can count as 'selling'). Competitors (WellnessLiving, Zenoti) explicitly attack Mindbody on this, marketing 'your data is yours, no consumer marketplace, no sharing with competitors.' Net: clients acquired via the marketplace feel like Mindbody's clients, not the venue's.
profiles.whiteLabel: Partial / dual-mode. Mindbody offers BRANDED WEB WIDGETS that embed on the venue's own website with the venue's colors/fonts and on-page transactions — genuinely white-label at the point of booking. BUT the strategic pull is the Mindbody-branded consumer app and marketplace, where the venue is one listing among many competitors and the Mindbody brand is dominant. So it is white-label on the venue's own site, marketplace-branded everywhere else — and the marketplace is where the 20% commission and competitor-routing live. There is also a paid branded native mobile app, but base branding leans toward Mindbody.
profiles.targetSegment: Fitness, wellness and beauty businesses: yoga/pilates/boutique fitness studios, gyms, spas, salons, med-spas — from single-location independents up to multi-location chains. Skews toward established/multi-location operators who value the marketplace and can absorb the cost and complexity.
profiles.threatToGuestOverflow: Moderate, mostly adjacent. Mindbody dominates spa-wellness, one of Guest Overflow's target verticals, so a spa evaluating booking software will likely hear of Mindbody. BUT it is the philosophical opposite of Guest Overflow on every core promise: commission-taking marketplace vs commission-free; opaque per-location pricing vs flat monthly; platform/marketplace ownership of clients vs venue owns its data; self-serve 8-week onboarding vs concierge done-for-you. It does not serve restaurants or tours, and its pain points (cost, lock-in, support, data) are exactly Guest Overflow's selling points. Threat is more 'incumbent to position against' than 'feature-for-feature rival.'
lessonsForUs: COPY: branded web widgets that keep booking AND payment on the venue's own domain with on-page transactions — this is the part of Mindbody people actually like; make it the default, not an add-on.

AVOID the 20% marketplace commission and competitor-routing entirely — make 'we never take a cut and never send your guests to a competitor' an explicit headline; it directly answers Mindbody's loudest complaint.
COPY the breadth-of-value perception but deliver it via concierge setup instead of an 8-week self-serve onboarding that users hate.

AVOID 12-month lock-in and exit friction — Guest Overflow's 'no-questions exit' and month-to-month flat fee is a direct wedge; advertise it next to Mindbody's BBB/Trustpilot cancellation horror stories.
COPY transparent, all-in pricing: publish exact EUR numbers (Essential 99 / Premium 249) — Mindbody's hidden, creeping pricing is a top grievance, so transparency is a differentiator.
AVOID monetizing client data / targeted ads — lead with 'venue owns all guest data, full export, nothing shared with advertisers or competitors' as a GDPR-friendly EU selling point.
AVOID per-location penalty pricing — flat monthly that doesn't punish growth, and a clear Custom tier for multi-location, beats Mindbody's per-location stacking.
COPY/INVEST in responsive human support — Mindbody's 3+ day, low-competence support is its other top complaint; concierge + fast support is Guest Overflow's natural advantage.
profiles.confidence: Medium-high. High on the model (per-location SaaS + in-house payments + 20% capped marketplace commission + 12-month lock-in), on the dominant complaints (cost/opacity, support, cancellation, data/marketplace), and on the branded-widget vs marketplace duality — all corroborated across multiple independent sources. Medium on exact tier prices and processing %: Mindbody deliberately hides pricing behind sales calls, so the dollar figures are well-corroborated REPORTED ranges, not an official published price list, and may vary by region/negotiation/year.

profiles.name: Booksy
profiles.positioning: "All your features in one app, no hidden fees" — a marketplace-first booking and business-management app for beauty, barber, spa and wellness solo operators and small teams, where the platform also acts as a consumer discovery channel.
profiles.businessModel: Hybrid: flat per-seat SaaS subscription (low base + per-staff add-on) layered on top of (1) payment-processing margin on every card transaction and (2) an optional marketplace commission ("Boost") that takes a one-time 30% cut of a new client's first visit. The marketplace/consumer app is the real flywheel — the subscription is cheap to drive density, and Booksy monetizes the transaction and client-acquisition layers.
profiles.pricing: USD. Base Booksy Biz: $29.99/mo + tax, no contract, 14-day free trial (no card). Additional team member: $20/mo each. Payment processing: 2.49% + $0.10 (Booksy card reader), 2.49% + $0.20 (Tap to Pay), 2.69% + $0.30 (mobile/keyed entry); Fast Payouts 1.5%, next-business-day payout free. Boost marketplace marketing: free to switch on, but 30% of the new client's first-visit total as a one-time fee (min $10, max $100); repeat visits are commission-free. Hardware: BBPOS WisePOS E $219.85, Stripe Reader M2 $53.10. All software features included in the base price; no per-booking commission on non-Boost bookings. No setup fee.
keyFeatures: Consumer marketplace + 'Booksy for Customers' app (discovery channel for new clients)
Calendar/scheduling, client management (CRM), staff management
Online booking widget (copy-paste snippet for WordPress/Wix/Squarespace) plus a hosted Booksy profile page
Integrated payments via Stripe, Tap to Pay, card readers, fast payouts
No-show protection: deposits and card-on-file guarantees
Boost paid-acquisition feature (marketplace visibility for 30% of first visit)
Marketing tools: message blasts, automated SMS/email reminders, gift cards, loyalty cards, waitlists
Reviews collected and displayed on the marketplace profile
Mobile-first apps for both providers and consumers
whatWorks: Very low entry price ($29.99/mo, no contract, 14-day no-card trial) removes friction for solo operators
Marketplace genuinely sends new bookings — solos who can't market themselves value the discovery traffic and the 'only pay when it works' Boost framing
Everything bundled into one price (no feature-gated tiers) is praised as simple and transparent vs competitors that nickel-and-dime
Strong mobile/consumer app UX; clients find rebooking easy
Integrated Stripe payments + deposits/no-show protection are seen as competitive and convenient
Large installed base and brand recognition in barber/beauty creates network effects and consumer trust
weaknesses: Marketplace lock-in / 'golden handcuffs' — clients are surfaced competitors during booking and business owners feel client relationships are 'rented back' to them
BBB complaints that clients get forwarded to other stylists and that funds don't reach the original business
Customer support widely described as slow, unresponsive, and dismissive ('asks for screenshots, never follows up')
Per-staff pricing scales painfully: a 5-chair shop is ~$29.99 + 4x$20 = $109.99/mo before processing fees
Reliability complaints: app crashes, OTP/SMS reminders/mobile payments breaking while billing continues
Double-booking and phantom-availability complaints (slots shown bookable that the provider had blocked)
Refund/deposit disputes — consumers charged after cancelling, stylist absorbs Booksy's cut so client loses money
Difficulty getting a business listing/data removed after leaving; export policies unclear
30% first-visit Boost commission is steep, and the marketplace can commoditize a premium brand
profiles.dataOwnership: Platform-leaning. Booksy is structured as a marketplace that acquires the consumer first via its own app/directory and then connects them to the venue, so the booking relationship and the consumer account live with Booksy. Third-party migration guides describe Booksy-type platforms as 'walled gardens' with 'golden handcuffs' where data is locked in and export policies are unclear/restrictive. Business owners report difficulty getting their listing and data removed even after repeated requests. The venue gets a CRM view of its clients inside the app, but does not own the acquisition relationship or the consumer's Booksy account, and clients booking through the marketplace are actively shown competing nearby venues.
profiles.whiteLabel: Not white-label. Bookings are Booksy-branded: the primary channels are the Booksy consumer app and the Booksy.com marketplace profile, both of which prominently surface other (often competing) venues. Booksy does offer an embeddable booking widget (copy-paste code snippet) that sits on the venue's own site, but it launches the Booksy booking flow/brand rather than a white-labelled, venue-only experience — the guest still ends up in Booksy's system and sees Booksy branding.
profiles.targetSegment: Solo and small-team personal-services providers — barbers, hair/nail salons, beauty technicians, tattoo artists, and spa/wellness practitioners — primarily in the US, UK and Poland, who want cheap scheduling plus a marketplace that brings them walk-up demand. Skews micro-business and appointment-services rather than restaurants or hotels.
profiles.threatToGuestOverflow: Moderate and mostly indirect. Booksy dominates a different vertical (barber/beauty/spa solos) and a different model (marketplace + per-seat app). For Guest Overflow's core targets (independent restaurants, boutique hotels, tour operators) Booksy is rarely the chosen tool, so direct head-to-head overlap is low. The real overlap is the spa/wellness segment Guest Overflow also names — there Booksy is the incumbent. But Booksy is the philosophical opposite of Guest Overflow (marketplace, platform-owned data, Booksy branding, per-seat + commission) which makes it an easy contrast to sell against rather than a direct substitute. Threat: medium for spa/wellness leads, low elsewhere.
lessonsForUs: Lead the pitch on the exact pains Booksy creates: 'your guests are yours, not rented back to you,' 'no marketplace showing your clients your competitors,' 'full data export, delete-on-exit.' This is Guest Overflow's clearest wedge.
Copy the simplicity Booksy is praised for: one transparent price with everything included; avoid feature-gated nickel-and-diming.

Avoid per-seat pricing — it punishes growing venues (a 5-staff Booksy bill is ~$110/mo before fees). Guest Overflow's flat per-venue monthly is a concrete advantage to spell out with a side-by-side cost example.

Booksy's worst reviews are about support being slow and dismissive — Guest Overflow's concierge / founder-led setup + quarterly reviews directly counter this; make human, responsive support a headline differentiator, not a footnote.

Address reliability explicitly (SMS reminders, payments, no phantom availability/double-booking) since these are Booksy's most damaging operational complaints — reliability + done-for-you install is a trust message.
Match Booksy's no-show protection (deposits, card guarantees) — table stakes; Guest Overflow's Premium already has this, keep it front and centre for spa/wellness prospects.

Counter Booksy's one real strength — it brings new demand. Guest Overflow doesn't have a marketplace, so pair the 'commission-free, you own everything' message with the Google Business Profile optimization upsell as the legitimate new-customer-acquisition story.

Borrow the framing 'only pay when it works' that makes Boost palatable — Guest Overflow's founding offer (3 months half price + no-questions exit) is a stronger, commission-free version of the same risk-reversal.
profiles.confidence: High on pricing and model (confirmed on Booksy's own pricing page plus multiple corroborating third parties, June 2026). High on the marketplace/Booksy-branded nature and the support/reliability/refund complaints (consistent across Trustpilot, BBB, app-store reviews). Medium on precise data-export/deletion policy (Booksy doesn't publish exact export terms; complaints and migration guides imply lock-in but exact contractual specifics are inferred, not quoted from Booksy).

profiles.name: FareHarbor
profiles.positioning: "The world's most powerful reservation platform for tours, activities and attractions" — $0/month, concierge-built booking that the customer (not the operator) pays a fee on, embedded on the operator's own site via Lightframe overlay. Owned by Booking Holdings.
profiles.businessModel: Commission per booking, but inverted vs a restaurant model: the per-booking "booking fee" (~6%, range 6-8%) is added to the guest's total at checkout and paid by the GUEST, so FareHarbor markets itself as "free" / $0 monthly to the operator. Additional take: ~2% on API/affiliate bookings and ~2% from the operator's share on OTA-channel bookings (Viator/GetYourGuide/Google Things to Do, all conveniently in the Booking Holdings orbit). Card processing (~2.9% + $0.30 US) is separate and borne by the operator. Monetization is volume-based and embedded in the checkout, plus an optional paid website-build service. Revenue model relies on the operator never feeling the cost directly while the guest absorbs it.
profiles.pricing: $0/month subscription, no setup fee (concierge onboarding included free). Core take: ~6% per-booking fee (commonly cited 6-8%, region-dependent; US/Canada/Mexico/Caribbean ~6%) added to the GUEST's total at checkout — operators are NOT allowed to absorb it. ~2% on API/affiliate bookings; ~2% taken from the operator's share on OTA channel bookings (those OTAs separately charge 20-30%). Credit-card processing ~2.9% + $0.30/transaction (US; varies by region), paid by operator. Optional website-build service reported at ~$499/mo or ~$5,000/yr. No public rate card — rates are negotiated per operator at onboarding. Effective all-in platform cost cited at ~10.3% of booking value (~€10,260 on €100k bookings). Some reviewers report total guest-facing add-ons (fees + taxes) reaching 12-15% of cart. Currency: USD primary; EUR/regional equivalents apply.
keyFeatures: Lightframe embeddable booking overlay — guests book on the operator's own site without redirect
WordPress plugin + embed generator (book buttons, embedded grid widgets)
Free concierge onboarding: dedicated account manager builds/configures the booking flow
24/7 customer support (widely praised)
Dashboard for inventory/availability, resource & manifest management, dispatch
Deposits, waivers, gift cards, add-ons, custom booking flows
Distribution to OTA channels (Viator, GetYourGuide, Google Things to Do) and affiliate/reseller network
Data export to XML/CSV/Excel/PDF; reporting suite
Optional full website-build service for operators lacking a site
Payment processing built in
whatWorks: $0 monthly + 'operator pays nothing' framing removes adoption friction — huge for cash-strapped small operators
Free, hands-on concierge setup with a real account manager — removes the build burden entirely
24/7 support is the single most praised attribute across Capterra/G2 (4.7/5, ~1,100+ reviews)
Lightframe keeps guests on the operator's own domain (white-label feel, not a marketplace redirect)
Built-in OTA/affiliate distribution leverages Booking Holdings scale to fill capacity
Operator is the GDPR data controller and CAN export historical data (XML/CSV) — genuine portability exists
Deep feature depth for complex tour logistics (manifests, dispatch, resources, add-ons)
weaknesses: Per-booking fee is forced onto the GUEST at checkout and cannot be absorbed by the operator — drives cart abandonment and price-shock complaints
Reviewers report total guest-facing add-ons (fee + tax/service) reaching 12-15% of cart; e.g. a $900 booking becoming $1,023
Slow checkout: a March 2025 review reports a '14-second spinning wheel of death' and sales dropping ~25%; add-ons further slow the flow
Complex, unintuitive operator dashboard and reporting — steep setup/learning curve
Post-acquisition by Booking Holdings, reviewers say 'company values shifted… only out to make money'
Unilateral changes: operators report FareHarbor changing their prices, titles and T&Cs without asking
Payout/refund friction: 'they are holding my money to give my customers'; unclear disbursement timelines
Opaque, negotiated pricing — no public rate card, hard to compare or predict total cost
Effective ~10%+ all-in cost makes it expensive at scale despite the '$0/month' headline
profiles.dataOwnership: Reasonably operator-favorable on paper: the activity provider is the GDPR data controller of its guest data, and FareHarbor supports export/portability (XML, CSV, Excel, PDF) — third-party tools (e.g. TRYTN, Bokun) even offer FareHarbor data-import migration. So the venue does retain access to and ownership of its guest records. The catch is soft lock-in via the Booking Holdings ecosystem: guest acquisition flows through FareHarbor-controlled OTA/affiliate channels (Viator etc.), the payment rail and checkout are FareHarbor's, and operators report the platform unilaterally altering prices/titles/T&Cs — so operational control over the customer relationship is weaker than the formal data-controller status implies.
profiles.whiteLabel: Primarily white-label on the operator's OWN website: the Lightframe overlay and embed widgets/WordPress plugin let guests browse and complete a booking without leaving the operator's domain, and the operator's brand is front-and-center (guests are not sent to a fareharbor.com marketplace listing for the core direct-booking flow). However it is NOT purely white-label: it doubles as a distribution network, pushing inventory into OTA marketplaces (Viator, GetYourGuide, Google Things to Do) and an affiliate/reseller program, and the guest-facing "booking fee" line item exposes the platform's monetization at checkout. So: white-label storefront, marketplace-style distribution and a visible platform fee.
profiles.targetSegment: Tours, activities, attractions and experience operators of all sizes — from single-guide operators up to large multi-location attractions; strongest in North America, with global reach. Skews toward operators who want zero upfront/monthly cost and done-for-you setup and are willing to pass fees to guests.
profiles.threatToGuestOverflow: Moderate and mostly adjacent. FareHarbor dominates tours-activities, which is one of Guest Overflow's target verticals, and it shares two of our core plays: white-label embedding on the venue's own site and concierge done-for-you setup. That makes it a credible reference point and a direct competitor for any tour/activity prospect. BUT its business model is the opposite of ours on the dimension we lead with: it monetizes via a per-booking fee (just shifted onto the guest) rather than flat commission-free pricing, and the guest-facing fee is its single biggest source of complaints. It is also restaurant/hotel/spa-agnostic (built for tours), US-centric, and English-first — leaving Guest Overflow's EU/Portugal, bilingual, multi-vertical positioning open. Net: a strong incumbent in one vertical, but its weaknesses are precisely our differentiators.
lessonsForUs: COPY: the '$0 monthly + concierge done-for-you setup' adoption hook — but beat it honestly with truly commission-free flat pricing instead of a hidden guest-facing fee
ATTACK their core weakness in our messaging: 'No surprise fee at your guest's checkout — your price is your price.' This is FareHarbor's #1 complaint and our structural advantage
COPY 24/7 / highly responsive support — it's the most-praised thing about FareHarbor and a real moat for small operators
AVOID slow checkout at all costs — their '14-second spinner = -25% sales' is a cautionary tale; make widget load speed and add-on handling a measurable selling point
AVOID unilateral changes to operator prices/T&Cs and avoid holding operator payouts — these breed deep distrust; promise transparency and direct/fast settlement
COPY genuine data export/portability and go further: make 'you own and can export all guest data anytime, no marketplace in between' an explicit, contractual promise
DIFFERENTIATE on no marketplace/OTA dependency: FareHarbor funnels guests through Booking Holdings channels; we keep the guest relationship 100% the venue's
OFFER transparent published flat pricing (EUR99/EUR249) vs their opaque negotiated rates — predictability is a selling point
AVOID their UI complexity — lean on concierge + simplicity for non-technical independent operators
WIN on EU/Portugal + bilingual EN/PT + multi-vertical (restaurants, hotels, spas) where FareHarbor is weak/absent
profiles.confidence: Medium-high. Pricing direction (>$0/month, ~6% guest-paid booking fee, 6-8% range, +2% API/OTA, ~2.9%+$0.30 card processing, ~$499/mo website service) is corroborated across multiple independent 2026 sources and review aggregators, and the white-label/Lightframe + Booking Holdings ownership + GDPR data-controller/export facts are well-established. Lower confidence on exact numbers because FareHarbor publishes NO public rate card — all rates are negotiated per operator, so percentages vary by region/volume and reviewer-reported totals (12-15% all-in) mix in taxes. The official pricing page failed to load directly, so pricing is sourced from third-party analyses and reviews rather than FareHarbor's own page.

profiles.name: Rezdy
profiles.positioning: "The world's leading independent B2B booking and distribution platform for tours, activities & attractions" — direct online bookings PLUS a 25,000+ reseller/OTA distribution marketplace (Channel Manager) in one tool.
profiles.businessModel: Hybrid SaaS: flat monthly subscription tier PLUS a per-booking fee. ~3% on every online booking and a flat per-booking fee on offline/agent bookings (USD 1.00 down to USD 0.70 by tier). Additional revenue from RezdyPay payment processing (~1.6%+ $0.30) and from the Marketplace/Channel Manager that distributes operators' inventory to OTAs (Viator, GetYourGuide, TripAdvisor) where reseller commissions also apply. So Rezdy monetizes subscription + transaction % + payments + distribution.
profiles.pricing:
Three published plans (USD on the US site; AUD ex-GST on the AU site — verify by region):
- Foundation: $49/mo, 3% per online booking, $1.00 per offline/agent booking. Core booking engine, mobile manifest, resource management.
- Accelerate: $99/mo, 3% per online booking, $0.85 per offline booking. Adds packages, gift cards/vouchers, automated notifications, advanced plugins.
- Expansion: $249/mo, 3% per online booking, $0.70 per offline booking. Adds advanced reporting, bulk tools, full API access, webhooks.
Custom/Enterprise plans for high volume. 21-day free trial, no credit card, no contract lock-in. 15% nonprofit discount. RezdyPay processing fees from ~1.60% + $0.30. Note: the 3% per-cover/per-booking fee is the key cost — it compounds on top of the monthly fee and on top of payment processing, so true cost scales with revenue. Operators can absorb it or pass it to guests.
keyFeatures: Online booking engine with real-time availability + embeddable Book Now widgets (iframe stays on-site, or new-tab/lightbox)
Channel Manager / Marketplace distribution to 25,000+ resellers and OTAs (Viator, GetYourGuide, TripAdvisor, Expedia)
Agent/reseller tools — operators can also resell other operators' products
RezdyPay integrated payments + Stripe, Apple Pay, Google Pay gateways
Gift cards, vouchers, packages, promo codes/codewords
Automated guest notifications and confirmations
Guest manifest, resource management, scheduling
Reporting/analytics with data export (CSV), customer list import/export
API access + webhooks (Expansion tier), Shopify bridge
One-click website builder for operators without a site
WordPress/Wix/Squarespace/Weebly/Joomla/Drupal compatibility
whatWorks: Strong 4.5/5 on Capterra (236+ reviews) — broadly liked despite price
Stable, reliable API praised by integrators ('never create issues')
The distribution/Channel Manager is the real moat — instant access to global OTAs from one inventory is a genuine growth lever operators can't easily replicate
Vouchers, codewords and promo tooling praised for running campaigns
No contract lock-in, unlimited products/users/agents on all tiers, 21-day free trial lowers entry friction
Explicit 'you own your data, branding, website and customer relationships' messaging resonates with operators wary of OTA dependency
Flexible booking-fee handling (absorb or pass to guest)
weaknesses: Pricing perceived as expensive for small operators — 'hard to justify the price tag'
Bait-and-switch complaints: users report being pushed to upgrade with ~10x cost jumps and surprise percentage fees on bookings they thought were flat-rate
The 3% per-booking fee stacks on top of subscription AND payment processing — total cost grows with success and is a recurring grievance
Weak support: 'no phone line, no live chat, just slow careless email,' reps refuse to escalate, multi-week response times
Dated/clunky UI — one reviewer: 'like something Fred Flintstone would use'
Onboarding overruns — promised 3-day setup taking weeks/months; setup is heavy and self-serve
Mobile app limited (only shows current week; hard to modify bookings)
Functional gaps: no minimum party size on OTA connections, add-ons lack tiered pricing, currency conversion errors, availability auto-disable glitches forcing manual reopening of tours
Self-service model — no done-for-you setup
profiles.dataOwnership: Operator-favorable on paper: Rezdy explicitly states the operator owns their data, branding, website and customer relationships, with CSV export and customer list import/export. The operator captures guest data on direct bookings. BUT: bookings that come through the Marketplace/OTA channels (Viator, GetYourGuide) carry the standard OTA caveat — the reseller/OTA mediates that customer relationship and data, so distribution-sourced guests are not fully 'owned' the same way. There's also practical lock-in: once inventory, availability and OTA connections live in Rezdy, migrating off is painful (a source of the 'bait-and-switch' resentment).
profiles.whiteLabel: Partial. The Book Now widget can be embedded via iframe so guests stay on the operator's own site, and the button is style-customizable (color/label/size). It is branded to the operator's site visually, but the underlying booking/checkout flow is Rezdy-hosted and Rezdy does NOT clearly advertise a fully white-label, Rezdy-invisible checkout — branding during the payment step is unconfirmed and would need sales confirmation. Crucially, Rezdy is ALSO a marketplace: a large part of its value is listing operators on third-party OTA marketplaces, which is the opposite of white-label (the guest books on Viator/GetYourGuide, not the venue's site).
profiles.targetSegment: Tour, activity and attraction operators (day tours, adventure, experiences, attractions) — from small independents up to multi-product/enterprise operators. Strong in AU/NZ, US, UK, EU. B2B-distribution-oriented operators who want OTA reach, not just a direct booking button.
profiles.threatToGuestOverflow: Moderate and mostly adjacent. Rezdy targets tours/activities specifically, overlapping with one of Guest Overflow's segments (tour/activity operators), but it is not focused on restaurants, hotels or spas. The biggest philosophical clash: Rezdy is fundamentally a commission + marketplace model (3% per booking, OTA distribution), which is exactly what Guest Overflow positions against (commission-free, white-label, no marketplace in between). For a small independent operator who just wants direct bookings on their own site without per-cover fees, Guest Overflow is differentiated. Rezdy only out-competes when the operator actively wants OTA distribution reach — a feature Guest Overflow does not offer. Threat rises if Guest Overflow tries to serve high-volume operators who need channel management.
lessonsForUs: COPY the data-ownership message but go further: Guest Overflow's 'no marketplace in between, full export, you own every guest' is a cleaner, more credible version of Rezdy's claim — lead with it against Rezdy's OTA dependency.

ATTACK the 3% per-booking fee directly in marketing: show a cost calculator where Rezdy's subscription + 3% + payment fees + OTA commission balloons with volume, vs Guest Overflow's flat EUR99/249 that never grows. This is Rezdy's #1 complaint.

EXPLOIT support gaps: Rezdy has no phone, slow email, no escalation. Guest Overflow's concierge done-for-you setup + quarterly review is the exact opposite — make 'a real founder builds and supports it' a headline differentiator.
EXPLOIT onboarding pain: Rezdy's '3-day setup' taking weeks is a recurring gripe. Guest Overflow's done-for-you install directly neutralizes this — promise and prove fast, hands-off launch.

AVOID hybrid pricing surprises: Rezdy's 'bait-and-switch' reputation comes from flat fees turning into percentage fees and forced upgrades. Keep Guest Overflow pricing genuinely flat and transparent; make the 'no per-cover commission, ever' a written guarantee.

CONSIDER (don't necessarily copy) distribution: Rezdy's marketplace is its real moat for growth-hungry operators. Guest Overflow can't match OTA reach, so don't try — instead reframe: position direct-bookings + owned data as higher-margin than OTA-dependent revenue, and target operators burned by OTA commissions.
MODERNIZE UX: Rezdy is criticized as dated/clunky with a weak mobile app. A clean, modern, mobile-first widget and dashboard is an easy win.

Match Rezdy's low-friction entry: 21-day no-CC trial and no lock-in. Guest Overflow's founding offer (3 months half price + no-questions exit) is competitive — keep emphasizing the easy exit, which Rezdy lacks (lock-in resentment).
profiles.confidence: high — pricing tiers, the 3% per-booking fee, per-tier offline fees, 21-day trial and no lock-in were confirmed directly on rezdy.com/pricing; complaints (cost stacking, bait-and-switch, poor support, dated UI, onboarding overruns, weak mobile app) were corroborated across Capterra reviews and comparison sites. Medium confidence on two points: (1) exact currency varies by region (USD on US site, AUD ex-GST on AU site) so absolute numbers shift by market; (2) whether the checkout is FULLY white-label/Rezdy-invisible at the payment step is unconfirmed — Rezdy's page does not state it explicitly and would require sales confirmation.

profiles.name: Bokun (a TripAdvisor / Viator company)
profiles.positioning: "Your brand. Our platform." — an all-in-one reservation, channel-management and distribution system for tours and activities, tightly wired into the Viator/TripAdvisor OTA marketplace.
profiles.businessModel: Hybrid: monthly SaaS subscription PLUS a per-booking "booking fee" (1–1.5%) on applicable direct/OTA bookings. Bokun is owned by Viator (TripAdvisor); the deeper monetisation is distribution — operators are funnelled into Viator, where Viator takes its own ~20–30% OTA commission. The platform fee is effectively a customer-acquisition loss-leader for the Viator marketplace.
profiles.pricing: Official tiers (USD/month, billed monthly, no setup fee, 14-day free trial, no card): FREE $0 (1 user, manual/offline bookings only, NO OTA/API connectivity); START $49 (up to 5 users) with 1.5% booking fee on applicable bookings; PLUS $149 (up to 10 users) with 1.25% fee; PREMIUM $499 (unlimited users) with 1% fee. "0% Bokun fees on Viator and offline bookings" on paid plans; operators can choose to pass the booking fee to the customer or absorb it. Separate Channel Manager plan (price on request) charges 0.5–1.5% on Viator bookings. Viator's own OTA commission (~20–30%) is on top and separate. Historically Bokun was free; it added the mandatory $49/mo + 1.5% fee on 2 Nov 2020 and has changed pricing roughly three times in two years.
keyFeatures: Embeddable booking widgets (button, product list, product page, calendar) customisable to operator brand/colours
Channel manager / OTA connectivity to Viator, GetYourGuide, Expedia, Airbnb Experiences, etc.
Viator Marketplace + reseller network (B2B distribution to thousands of agents)
Inventory, resource and allocation management; POS; gift cards
Custom agent/reseller booking areas (higher tiers)
Zapier integrations, API
White-label 'branded booking hub' for DMCs/DMOs/agencies to launch their own marketplace
Free tour-operator website templates
Annual strategy review (Plus+), priority support (Premium)
whatWorks: High review scores: ~4.7 stars on Capterra/G2 across 450+ reviews — praised as user-friendly once learned
Free entry tier and low headline booking fees (lowest in category at 1–1.5%) lower the barrier to start
Unbeatable distribution: native, frictionless pipe into Viator/TripAdvisor demand — the #1 reason operators stay
Strong channel manager keeps availability synced across many OTAs from one calendar
Genuinely embeds widgets on the operator's own site with their branding
Responsive support is praised by many reviewers
Operators can pass the booking fee onto the end customer
weaknesses: Repeated, unilateral pricing changes (3x in 2 years) destroyed trust — operators who joined because it was free/commission-free were force-migrated to monthly fee + per-booking fee
Free plan strips OTA/API connectivity, so for any operator relying on TripAdvisor it 'isn't actually a choice' — de facto forced upgrade (operator quote)
Booking fee is charged even on cancelled bookings per multiple reviews — deeply resented
À-la-carte gouging history: agent areas, iOS app, custom reports each billed separately (~$167/mo to regain prior functionality)
Slow/inconsistent support and availability-sync issues during peak season (r/touroperators, Capterra, G2)
Steep learning curve; UI called complex; getting LESS user-friendly with updates per reviewers
Cannot edit some bookings/invoices without cancel-and-rebook
Poor API documentation; recurring API connection problems
Payment-processing/payout feature requests left unresolved for 1.5+ years (Trustpilot)
Owned by Viator — staying on Bokun defeats the goal of reducing OTA dependency; structural conflict of interest
profiles.dataOwnership: Mixed-to-platform-leaning. Bokun stores reservation/guest data and the operator can access/export it, but the model is built to route guests through the Viator/TripAdvisor marketplace — where the OTA, not the venue, owns the customer relationship and can re-market to that guest. The free tier blocks API access, limiting independent extraction. Because the parent is an OTA, there is inherent marketplace lock-in and a conflict of interest over who owns the demand and the guest.
profiles.whiteLabel: Two distinct things. (1) Direct-booking widgets DO embed on the operator's own website with the operator's branding ("your customers only see your brand"). (2) A separate paid "White Label" product lets DMCs/DMOs/agencies launch a branded booking hub/marketplace. BUT the underlying economics still flow into Viator's marketplace and reseller network, and operators are continually nudged toward OTA listings rather than a clean own-domain, own-data setup. Whether the white-label hub sits on the operator's own domain vs a Bokun-hosted page is not clearly disclosed; pricing is "enquire only".
profiles.targetSegment: Small independent tour, activity and experience operators (98% of reviewers are small businesses; ~68% tour operators; ~45% leisure/travel/tourism), plus DMCs/DMOs/travel agencies wanting a white-label marketplace. Heavily skewed to operators who already depend on, or want, TripAdvisor/Viator distribution.
profiles.threatToGuestOverflow: Moderate, and only partially overlapping. Bokun targets tours/activities, one of Guest Overflow's four segments, and it does offer brand-able widgets on the operator's own site — so it competes on the embedded-booking dimension. However it is the antithesis of Guest Overflow's pitch: it is OTA-owned (Viator), charges per-booking fees, pulls guests into a marketplace, and has a track record of unilateral price hikes. For an operator who specifically wants commission-free, own-your-data, flat-fee, marketplace-free booking, Bokun is the cautionary tale Guest Overflow can position directly against. It does NOT touch restaurants, hotels or spas. Net: a relevant comparison/foil more than a head-to-head threat for GO's core restaurant/hotel/spa base.
lessonsForUs: COPY the foil narrative: 'We will never become an OTA, never take per-booking fees, never re-market your guests, never raise prices out from under you' — Bokun's three-pricing-changes-in-two-years and forced migration off the free plan are GO's strongest sales ammunition.
COPY price stability as a feature: publicly commit to fee structure and grandfather existing customers; contrast with Bokun's unilateral changes and the no-questions exit GO already offers.
AVOID the cancelled-booking fee: never charge on cancellations — it is one of Bokun's most-hated practices and validates GO's flat-fee, no-per-cover model.
COPY transparency on data: explicitly guarantee full guest-data export and that the venue (not GO) owns the guest — directly answers the Viator-ownership conflict operators fear.
AVOID the free-tier bait-and-switch: don't cripple a tier (e.g. API/connectivity) to force upgrades; GO's honest flat tiers are a differentiator.
COPY low friction onboarding but go further: Bokun is praised when learned yet has a steep curve and complex UI — GO's concierge done-for-you setup directly neutralises this top complaint.
FIX what Bokun fails at: fast, weekend-inclusive support and reliable sync — make responsive human support a headline (concierge + quarterly review already aligns).
Match Bokun's brand-on-your-own-site widget claim but win on true own-domain + no marketplace in between — that is GO's core wedge.
Note Bokun has NO restaurant/hotel/spa product — GO's multi-vertical, white-label, commission-free flat-fee offering is genuinely differentiated for those segments.
profiles.confidence: High on pricing tiers/booking fees (verified on Bokun's own pricing page: Free/$49/$149/$499 with 1.5/1.25/1% fees) and on the pricing-change backlash and review complaints (multiple independent sources, including operator quotes). Medium on exact data-ownership mechanics and whether the white-label hub runs on the operator's own domain (Bokun does not fully disclose these; inferred from its Viator ownership and the white-label page). Medium on Viator's stacked commission (~20–30% is widely cited but varies by contract). Trustpilot direct page was 403-blocked; complaint details there come via search snippets and secondary sources.

profiles.name: SimplyBook.me
profiles.positioning: "The flexible, modular online booking system for any service business" — a horizontal, self-serve appointment scheduler covering 50+ verticals (salons, clinics, coaches, classes, escape rooms, and lightweight restaurant table booking), not a hospitality specialist.
profiles.businessModel: Flat-tier SaaS subscription (per account, not per seat) with a freemium entry point. Crucially, monetization is two-dimensional: (1) the base plan tier gates your monthly booking cap, number of providers and number of allowed "custom features"; and (2) a modular add-on marketplace of 60+ "custom features" (deposits, intake forms, coupons, gift cards, HIPAA, custom domain, branding removal, etc.) — some included in tier count, some paid one-time/recurring add-ons. No per-cover/per-booking commission and no transaction fee on payments. They also run a separate White Label Partner/reseller program for agencies to rebrand the whole platform.
profiles.pricing: All EUR, annual-billed (monthly billing ~17% higher). Free: EUR0 — 50 bookings/mo, 1 provider, 1 custom feature. Basic: EUR11.90/mo annual (~EUR13.90 monthly) — 100 bookings/mo, 5 providers, 3 custom features. Standard: EUR24.90/mo annual (~EUR29.90 monthly) — 500 bookings/mo, 15 providers, 8 custom features. Premium: EUR49.90/mo annual (~EUR59.90 monthly) — 2,000 bookings/mo, 30 providers, UNLIMITED custom features. Enterprise: custom quote (discovery call) for multi-location. NO setup fee, NO per-booking commission, NO payment transaction fee. SMS notifications are paid add-ons (~USD8 per 100 credits). Branding removal ("link removal") requires PREMIUM. Custom domain is a paid one-time custom feature. Reviewers report add-on/customization costs can stack to several times the base subscription.
keyFeatures: Modular 'custom feature' marketplace (60+): deposits, prepayments, coupons, gift cards, memberships, intake forms, HIPAA add-on
Embeddable booking widgets for your own website + standalone hosted booking page
Custom domain support (paid add-on) so the booking site runs on your domain
Branding/copyright removal on Premium (white-label footer)
Multi-provider / multi-service scheduling with calendar sync (Google etc.)
Automated email + paid SMS reminders and confirmations
Payment integrations (Stripe, PayPal, others) with no platform transaction fee
Client data export (CSV + structured JSON) and one-click client deletion for GDPR
Multi-language / international support; WordPress plugin
Lightweight restaurant table-booking and event/class booking templates
Reporting/analytics and bookings export
Separate White Label reseller program for agencies
whatWorks: Extremely high review volume and rating (~4.6/5 across 1,200+ Capterra reviews) — proven, mature, trusted
Genuine freemium entry (50 bookings/mo free, no card) lowers the trial barrier
No per-booking commission and no payment transaction fees — a real cost advantage vs marketplace tools

Customer support frequently praised in reviews
Self-serve onboarding works well for technically comfortable owners
GDPR posture is clear: documented DPA, customer is data controller, export + deletion tooling exists
weaknesses: Booking caps are a growth tax: hitting the monthly cap (50/100/500/2,000) HALTS new bookings or forces an upgrade — even when you don't want the higher tier's other features. A busy venue hits Basic's 100/mo fast.

Pricing complexity and 'hidden' add-on costs: reviewers report customization charges stacking to many times the base subscription; you can't buy a single extra custom feature past your tier limit — you must upgrade the whole plan
Intimidating setup / steep learning curve — too many toggles for non-technical owners
Dated booking-page UI vs modern competitors
Branding removal locked behind Premium (EUR49.90+/mo); true full white-label needs a chain of conditions (SMTP + custom domain + T&C edits + non-blank theme)
Slow support on lower plans
Timezone confusion, inability to change a booking timeframe after first set, widgets not saving color/Facebook widget bugs (review complaints)
Generic horizontal tool — not purpose-built for restaurant covers/no-shows/hotel nights; hospitality is a template, not a focus
DIY model: no done-for-you setup or ongoing concierge review
profiles.dataOwnership: Favorable and clearly documented. SimplyBook.me is the data PROCESSOR; the venue is the data CONTROLLER (per their DPA, SCC Module Two Controller-to-Processor). The venue can export all client/booking data (CSV and structured JSON via Settings → Export) and delete client records on request. No marketplace sits between venue and guest — bookings flow to the venue's own account. This is close to Guest Overflow's data-ownership promise, so it's NOT a clean differentiator on its own; the data lives on SimplyBook.me's hosted system rather than the venue's own infrastructure, and the venue still depends on the platform's tooling to get it out.
profiles.whiteLabel: Partial / pay-to-unlock white-label, not white-label by default. The default hosted booking page and widget carry SimplyBook.me branding/copyright. Removing it ("link removal") requires the Premium plan. A custom domain (so the booking site lives on the venue's domain) is a separately paid one-time custom feature. Achieving a FULLY white-label, T&C-free experience requires combining several settings (own SMTP + custom domain or website widget + edited Terms & a non-blank theme). It embeds on the venue's own site via widget (not a forced marketplace listing), but full brand-invisibility costs extra and takes configuration. They also offer a separate agency White Label reseller program to rebrand the platform wholesale.
profiles.targetSegment: Independent and small/mid service businesses globally that book by appointment: salons/spas, clinics and healthcare, coaches/consultants, classes, photographers, escape rooms, events. Skews toward technically comfortable, self-serve owners. Restaurants and hotels are supported only as light templates, not a primary focus.
profiles.threatToGuestOverflow: MODERATE. Overlap on the core promises (commission-free, no transaction fee, venue owns/exports data, embeddable on own site) means SimplyBook.me already occupies some of Guest Overflow's messaging ground at a much lower headline price (free–EUR60/mo vs EUR99–249/mo). A price-shopping independent owner could land on it first. BUT it is a generic, DIY, self-configure tool with booking caps, a steep learning curve, pay-to-remove branding, and no hospitality specialization or done-for-you service — it does not compete with Guest Overflow's concierge build + hospitality focus + true on-domain white-label. Threat is on price perception and feature breadth, not on positioning or service.
lessonsForUs: COPY: keep flat pricing with NO per-cover commission and NO transaction fee — but make 'unlimited bookings, no cap' an explicit headline. SimplyBook's biggest complaint is the booking cap that punishes growth; Guest Overflow should promise no booking ceiling on every tier.

AVOID: do not gate core trust features (branding removal, custom domain) behind the top tier. White-label on the venue's own domain must be standard in Guest Overflow's BASE plan — that is our wedge against their pay-to-unlock model.
AVOID: the modular add-on maze and 'hidden costs up to 10x the subscription.' Keep Guest Overflow pricing simple and all-in; advertise 'no surprise add-on fees.'

COPY: clear, documented data-ownership story (DPA, controller status, full CSV/JSON export, deletion) — but go further by stressing data lives on the VENUE's own site/domain with no platform in between, and offer a real one-click full export as a selling point.

LEAN INTO: hospitality specialization (covers, no-show protection/deposits, repeat-guest history, quarterly concierge review). SimplyBook treats restaurants/hotels as a generic template — Guest Overflow's purpose-built hospitality workflows + done-for-you setup directly answer their 'steep learning curve / intimidating setup / dated UI' complaints.
COPY: a low-friction entry to counter their free tier — consider a strong founding/trial offer or guided pilot so price-shoppers don't default to SimplyBook's free plan.
MESSAGING: position against 'DIY + booking caps + pay-per-feature' with 'done-for-you, unlimited bookings, your brand, one flat price.'
profiles.confidence: High. Pricing, plan limits, white-label/branding gating, and data-ownership/DPA details were verified directly against SimplyBook.me's own pricing/help/legal pages on 2026-06-16; complaints (booking-cap growth tax, hidden add-on costs, learning curve, dated UI, timezone/widget bugs) corroborated across Capterra (1,200+ reviews, ~4.6/5), G2, and multiple independent review sites. Minor uncertainty: exact monthly-vs-annual figures and per-feature add-on prices fluctuate by region/promotion, and a few one-time custom-feature prices were not individually itemized.

profiles.name: Square Appointments
profiles.positioning: "Free scheduling that pays for itself" — appointment booking bundled into Square's all-in-one POS/payments ecosystem, monetized through card processing rather than the calendar itself.
profiles.businessModel: Freemium SaaS subscription whose real revenue engine is payment processing. The booking software is free or cheap; Square makes its money on a per-transaction cut (% + fixed fee) of every payment that flows through it, plus tiered monthly fees for teams and SMS overages, hardware, and add-ons. It is a horizontal appointments tool (salons, spas, barbers, personal services) rather than a hospitality-specific reservation system.
profiles.pricing: USD, per location/month. After Square's October 2025 pricing restructure into unified tiers (Square One/Free, Plus, Premium, Pro): Free = $0/mo; Plus = ~$29/mo; Premium = ~$69/mo; Pro = custom (sales-led, aimed at businesses processing $250k+/yr). NOTE: pricing is in flux and sources conflict — several 2026 guides still list the older Plus $49 / Premium $149 figures, so treat exact monthly numbers as approximate and verify per-account. The dominant cost is PROCESSING FEES, not subscription: Free plan ~2.6% + 15c in-person, ~3.3% + 30c online, ~3.5% + 15c keyed; Plus/Premium ~2.5% + 15c in-person, ~2.9% + 30c online. Afterpay ~6% + 30c. SMS: Plus includes ~500 msgs then ~3c each; Premium ~2,500 msgs then ~1.5c each. Hardware extra (basic reader free; Terminal ~$299). 30-day free trial on paid tiers.
keyFeatures: Online booking page (hosted on Square: yourbusiness.square.site or squareup.com/appointments/book)
Embeddable booking widget/button for an existing website
Automated SMS/email reminders that demonstrably cut no-shows
Deposits, card-on-file, and no-show/cancellation fee charging

Native integration with Square POS, payments, payroll, marketing, loyalty, and gift cards
Staff/team calendars, multi-location, resource/room management

OpenTable integration path via Square for Restaurants for table reservations
whatWorks: Genuinely free entry tier removes adoption friction — huge for solo operators and micro-businesses
All-in-one: booking, payments, POS, payroll, marketing under one login is a strong lock-in and convenience story
Reminders reliably reduce no-shows, a top praised outcome in reviews
Very easy for clients to book and for owners to start (self-serve, no salesperson)
Trusted, ubiquitous brand with mature payments infrastructure
Deposits + no-show fees let venues protect revenue without a separate tool
Hardware ecosystem makes in-person + online a single stack
weaknesses: NOT white-label: booking lives on a Square-branded URL (square.site / squareup.com), guests see Square; no custom-domain booking flow and no agency white-label option
Limited customization of the booking widget — repeatedly requested by users, still not delivered; little CSS/branding control
Account deactivations and fund holds/reserves (commonly up to 90 days) with opaque, scripted support — a recurring fear/complaint for businesses dependent on cash flow
Processing fees are the hidden true cost; a busy venue pays far more in % cuts than any flat tool would charge
Price increases and bugs after the trial cited in reviews
App vs desktop experience differs and can be confusing on the business side; occasional glitches/slow startup
Built for personal-services appointments, not restaurant covers/floor plans — restaurants are pushed to OpenTable, adding another platform and another fee layer
SMS metered beyond included allotment adds per-message cost
profiles.dataOwnership: The venue can build and export its Customer Directory (CSV export of customers; appointment/notes export sometimes requires contacting Square), so guest data is accessible — but it lives inside Square's ecosystem, and Square's terms let it hold funds, place reserves, and deactivate accounts. The greater ownership risk is financial, not just data: because payments run through Square, Square sits between the venue and its money and can freeze it. Practically, the platform mediates the relationship more than the venue does.
profiles.whiteLabel: No true white-label. The booking page is Square-branded and hosted on a Square domain; venues can embed a widget/button on their own site but cannot run a fully branded, custom-domain booking flow, and there is no agency/reseller white-label tier. Not a marketplace in the OpenTable sense (no consumer discovery network for Appointments), but the customer-facing booking surface is Square's, not the venue's.
profiles.targetSegment: Solo professionals and small teams in personal/appointment-based services — salons, barbers, spas, wellness, beauty, fitness, and similar — especially US-centric businesses already in or open to the Square payments ecosystem. Not purpose-built for restaurants, boutique hotels, or tour/activity operators.
profiles.threatToGuestOverflow: Moderate, and mostly indirect. Square overlaps with Guest Overflow on spas/wellness and generic appointments, and its free tier + brand make it the default many small venues reach for first. But it diverges sharply on Guest Overflow's core wedges: Square is NOT white-label (Square-branded URL), is NOT commission-free (the whole model is a per-transaction cut), is self-serve rather than concierge/done-for-you, and is weak for restaurants (defers to OpenTable). For an EU/Portugal venue wanting bookings embedded on its own domain under its own brand with flat pricing and no per-cover cut, Square is a different category. Threat is highest where a prospect just wants "free booking + take payments" and is brand-indifferent.
lessonsForUs: Lead hard on the contrast Square structurally cannot match: truly white-label on the venue's OWN domain and brand (guests never see a third party) — make this the headline.

Hammer the commission-free, flat-fee message against Square's per-transaction %: build a simple calculator showing that a venue doing X covers/month pays far more in Square processing % than Guest Overflow's flat EUR99/249 — turn their 'free' into our 'cheaper at any real volume'.

Turn Square's fund-hold/deactivation horror stories into a trust wedge: emphasize that the venue owns its data, its guest relationships, and is not at risk of a platform freezing its money; offer full no-lock-in export.
Match the no-show protection Square advertises (deposits, card guarantees) — already in Premium — and market it as parity-plus-ownership.
Copy what works: free/cheap entry friction-removal, reminders that cut no-shows, easy self-serve booking UX — but pair with our concierge done-for-you setup as the differentiator Square has zero of.
Avoid Square's pitfalls: deliver real branding/CSS customization (a chronic, unmet Square user request) and a consistent owner experience.

Exploit the restaurant gap: Square punts restaurants to OpenTable (another platform, another fee, another marketplace) — position Guest Overflow as the single, commission-free, on-your-domain solution for independent restaurants.
Keep concierge + quarterly reviews front and center — Square offers no human, done-for-you service; their support is a frequent complaint.
profiles.confidence: Medium-high. High confidence on the qualitative structure (freemium-funded-by-processing model, no white-label/custom-domain, Square-branded booking page, processing fees as the real cost, fund-hold/deactivation and customization complaints, restaurant gap deferring to OpenTable). Medium confidence on exact subscription numbers: Square restructured pricing in October 2025 and 2026 third-party sources conflict (Plus $29 vs $49, Premium $69 vs $149), and the official pricing page renders prices via JS placeholders I could not read directly — exact monthly figures should be re-verified per region/account, and EUR pricing for Portugal/EU not separately confirmed (figures cited are USD/US-market).

Caveat: bookings acquired via the 'Channels' module (Google, TripAdvisor, social) come through third parties; and post-Zenchef merger the data now sits inside a much larger group's ecosystem.
profiles.whiteLabel: White-labelled / venue-branded. The booking engine embeds directly on the venue's own website (plus its Google Business Profile, Facebook, Instagram); guests book on the venue's site, not on a CoverManager marketplace/directory. CoverManager is NOT a consumer destination like TheFork — it is the back-end engine. This is the same white-label-on-own-domain model Guest Overflow uses.
