profiles.targetSegment: Independent mid-to-high-ticket restaurants (100+ reservations/month), fine-dining/Michelin venues, multi-location restaurant groups, nightlife, and hotel F&B operations — primarily Southern Europe (Spain) and Latin America, with Zenchef adding Northern Europe. Explicitly not a fit for sub-50-reservation small/price-sensitive venues.
profiles.threatToGuestOverflow: HIGH overlap on the core model — both are white-label, commission-free, venue-data-owning booking systems embedded on the venue's own site, both targeting independent restaurants in Europe. CoverManager is the category incumbent in Iberia with massive brand trust and (post-merger) huge scale, making it the default reference point Guest Overflow prospects already know. However, the threat is concentrated on mid/high-volume venues and groups; CoverManager is over-spec'd, expensive, opaquely priced, has a dated UI, no free trial, and explicitly does NOT serve small/price-sensitive operators or include a website — leaving Guest Overflow's exact niche (small independents, spas, hotels, tours; flat transparent price; website + GBP included; done-for-you concierge) under-served. Direct threat to GO's larger-restaurant deals; low threat to GO's SMB/multi-vertical base.
lessonsForUs: COPY: keep 'commission-free on YOUR site, you own the guest data, exportable' as the central wedge — it's proven to win venues off marketplaces, and CoverManager's own marketing validates it.

BEAT THEM ON PRICE TRANSPARENCY: publish flat EUR99/EUR249 numbers openly. CoverManager's quote-only opacity and 'costs not mentioned in the demo' are a top complaint — transparent pricing is an instant differentiator.
BEAT THEM ON TOTAL COST: CoverManager excludes the website (extra EUR1,500-3,500) and adds setup/training fees. GO already bundles website builds + GBP + concierge setup — lead with all-in cost.
EXPLOIT THE 'TOO BIG / over-spec'd' GAP: CoverManager itself admits it's not for sub-50-cover venues. Own the small-independent / spa / hotel / tours segment they ignore.
BEAT THE DATED UI + mobile gap: ship a modern, mobile-first widget and dashboard; reviewers say CoverManager has been 'overtaken'.
OFFER A REAL TRIAL / low-risk entry: CoverManager has no free trial. GO's 'founding offer: 3 months half price + no-questions exit' directly counters this — keep it prominent.

CONCIERGE AS A MOAT: CoverManager support is loved but is ticket-based and 'shifts between departments'. GO's founder-led, done-for-you setup + quarterly reviews is a sharper version of the thing customers already value.
AVOID their commission asterisk: don't replicate channel commissions; market truly flat pricing with zero per-cover/per-channel fees as cleaner.
WATCH the Zenchef merger: position GO as the independent, founder-run alternative for owners wary of platform consolidation/lock-in — some operators are already shopping for alternatives.
DON'T over-build floor-ops complexity early: CoverManager's depth creates a steep learning curve. Keep GO simple for non-technical small-venue owners.
profiles.confidence: MEDIUM. HIGH confidence on model (white-label embed, commission-free direct bookings, venue data ownership/export, target segment, Zenchef merger, real complaints — all corroborated across the vendor site + multiple independent review/comparison sources). LOW confidence on exact pricing: CoverManager publishes no official numbers and is quote-gated, so figures (EUR99 vs EUR150-400 vs EUR79/169/349 + ~EUR1.50/reservation) are third-party estimates that conflict and should be verified via a direct demo/quote. One non-fatal data hiccup: deru.es failed to load (ECONNREFUSED), but its key numbers were captured via the Bouzon source.

profiles.name: Bouzon Bookings (by Bouzon Digital)
profiles.positioning: "The commission-free CoverManager alternative: a restaurant website with a 0%-per-cover booking widget built in, from EUR49/mo." Founder-run agency for independent Spanish restaurants that want to own their channel instead of paying TheFork/CoverManager per cover.
profiles.businessModel: Flat monthly subscription (SaaS + done-for-you web agency hybrid), NOT a marketplace and NOT commission-per-cover. Core is a custom restaurant website (EUR49/mo) with the bookings module as a EUR9.99/mo add-on. Additional flat-fee modules: online shop (+EUR19.99), pro email (+EUR4.99), Cloud POS (EUR29.99), POS+Stock (EUR49.98). Also offers one-time website buyouts (EUR699 / EUR999). Revenue = stacked module subscriptions + buyout fees; explicitly 0% per reservation. Deposits/no-show protection run through the venue's own Stripe Connect (Bouzon takes no cut of those, only standard Stripe fees apply).
profiles.pricing: Currency EUR. Website base plan EUR49/mo (custom bilingual ES/EN site, EU hosting, domain, SSL, SEO, WhatsApp+email support Mon-Fri 09:00-18:00 CET). Bookings module +EUR9.99/mo at 0% per-cover commission -> realistic entry price for website+bookings is ~EUR58.99/mo (marketing headline rounds to "from EUR49/mo"). Other add-ons: online shop +EUR19.99/mo, pro email +EUR4.99/mo (up to 5 accounts), Cloud POS EUR29.99/mo, POS+Stock EUR49.98/mo. One-time website buyouts: Base EUR699 (1 mo support), Complete EUR999 (incl. reservations + shop + interactive menu, 3 mo support); 50% subscription credit toward buyout (max 30% discount). No setup fees, no lock-in contract, cancel anytime. Positioned at ~half of CoverManager (~EUR99/mo, website not included) and cheaper than TheFork (~EUR39/mo + EUR2-5 per cover). No public free trial found.
keyFeatures: Custom restaurant website included in base plan (bilingual ES/EN, EU hosting, domain, SSL, local SEO)
Booking widget embedded directly on the venue's own site (date/time/party-size, instant confirmation, no redirect off-site)
Drag-and-drop floor plan with table allocation by capacity
Shift and block/blackout control
Optional no-show deposits / card capture via the venue's own Stripe Connect
Automatic confirmation + reminder notifications
Admin app with push notifications
Customer data 100% owned by the venue, CSV export
Native multi-tenant / multi-location support (claims up to 10+ locations)
Verifactu Spanish tax-compliance built in
Direct founder support; onboarding in 1-2 weeks
Adjacent modules: online shop, Cloud POS, stock & recipe/margin costing
whatWorks: 0% per-cover commission with a flat fee is the headline that wins price-sensitive independents tired of TheFork/CoverManager per-cover fees
Bundling the website INTO the booking price ('half the price of CoverManager AND you get a site') is a strong differentiator vs pure booking SaaS that assumes you already have a site
Explicit data-ownership promise ('the email and reservation history belong to you, not the platform') resonates against marketplace lock-in
Concrete, transparent, published pricing with no setup fee and no lock-in lowers the decision friction
Modular flat-fee stack (web -> bookings -> shop -> POS -> stock) lets a venue expand spend without ever hitting a commission
Local credibility: EU/Spain hosting, Spanish Verifactu compliance, bilingual, founder-accessible support
Fast, done-for-you onboarding (1-2 weeks) suits non-technical restaurant owners
weaknesses: No third-party review footprint at all (no Trustpilot/G2/Capterra/Reddit presence found) - unproven, hard to trust, likely very early-stage with few live customers (one visible case study: Bar de Carnes, Benalmadena, migrated 2025)
Founder-run solo operation (Pablo Bouzon) raises bus-factor / support-scaling / continuity concerns for a venue betting its booking flow on it
Support is business-hours only (Mon-Fri 09:00-18:00 CET) - restaurants take bookings nights and weekends, exactly when support is unavailable
Bookings is positioned as an add-on to a website rather than standalone, so a venue that already has a good website pays for a site it may not want

No discovery/marketplace = zero inbound demand generation; the venue must drive all its own traffic (Bouzon openly concedes 'clients arrive via your web or Google'), which is fine for established venues but useless for filling empty covers
Deposits depend on Stripe Connect only - no broader payment flexibility
Multi-location '10+' claim appears aspirational, not evidenced
Heavily Spain-centric (Verifactu, Malaga, ES/EN only) - limited credibility outside Spain
profiles.dataOwnership: Venue owns its guest data. Bouzon explicitly states "Customer data 100% owned by the restaurant (exportable)" and "the email and reservation history belong to you, not the platform," with CSV export. No marketplace sits between the venue and its guests, so no marketplace lock-in of the customer relationship. (Same posture as Guest Overflow.)
profiles.whiteLabel: Branded to the VENUE, not to Bouzon. The booking widget is embedded on the restaurant's own custom website (built by Bouzon) and guests book without leaving the site - not a listing on a Bouzon-branded marketplace. Functionally white-label from the guest's perspective. Note: unlike Guest Overflow which embeds into the venue's EXISTING site, Bouzon's model assumes Bouzon also builds/hosts the website (the booking module is an add-on to their web plan).
profiles.targetSegment: Independent restaurants and bars in Spain doing roughly 20-60 covers/day (~50-100 covers), non-technical owners, plus small multi-location groups. Explicitly framed as the cost-effective option for established venues with steady direct demand that resent per-cover marketplace fees. Spain-first (Verifactu, ES/EN, EU/Malaga hosting).
profiles.threatToGuestOverflow: HIGH conceptual overlap, MEDIUM-LOW immediate threat. Bouzon is almost a one-to-one mirror of Guest Overflow's thesis: white-label, commission-free, flat monthly, founder-run, EU-based, data owned by the venue, done-for-you. They validate the model and the messaging. Direct competitive collision is limited today because (a) Bouzon is Spain-focused (Verifactu/ES) while Guest Overflow is Portugal/EU bilingual EN/PT, and (b) Bouzon is very early with no review/social proof. The real threat is that Bouzon proves the playbook cheaper (EUR58.99/mo all-in incl. a website vs Guest Overflow Essential EUR99/mo) and could expand into Portugal/EU. Guest Overflow's defensible edge: embeds into the venue's EXISTING site (no forced website rebuild), concierge quarterly reviews of no-shows/repeat guests, and broader vertical coverage (hotels/spas/tours), which Bouzon does not address.
lessonsForUs: COPY the bundled-website angle as an OPTION: Bouzon's 'you also get a website' at ~half CoverManager is very compelling; Guest Overflow already sells custom website builds + GBP optimization, so package a clear 'website + bookings' all-in tier to compete on the same headline price.

PRICE PRESSURE: Bouzon's all-in entry is ~EUR58.99/mo vs Guest Overflow Essential EUR99/mo. Either justify the EUR99 with concrete concierge value (the quarterly no-show/repeat-guest reviews) prominently, or introduce a leaner entry tier so we are not undercut on the first quote.
COPY the explicit, published, transparent pricing with 'no setup fee, no lock-in, cancel anytime' - it removes friction; make sure Guest Overflow states this as plainly.
BEAT them on support: their Mon-Fri 09:00-18:00 support is a real gap for a hospitality product. Advertise extended/evening/weekend support as a differentiator.

BEAT them on trust: Bouzon has zero third-party reviews and a single case study. Guest Overflow should aggressively publish named case studies, Google/Trustpilot reviews, and concrete no-show-reduction metrics - social proof is the cheapest win available against them.

AVOID making bookings a mere add-on to a forced website rebuild. Guest Overflow's 'embed into your EXISTING site' is a genuine advantage - lead with it, since many established venues do NOT want to migrate their website.

COPY the data-ownership + CSV-export language verbatim in spirit; it clearly resonates. Then go further: emphasize full history export AND the concierge analysis of that data, which Bouzon does not offer.
EXPAND where they are narrow: Bouzon is restaurant-only and Spain-only. Guest Overflow's hotels/spas/tours coverage and EN/PT + EU breadth is a moat - market it.

WATCH their adjacent modules (POS, stock/recipe costing, online shop). They are building a flat-fee operations stack to increase ARPU without commission; consider a roadmap of complementary flat-fee modules to grow account value the same way.
profiles.confidence: Medium-high. Pricing, features, data-ownership and white-label posture are taken directly from Bouzon's own EN site and pricing page (high confidence on those exact numbers: EUR49 web + EUR9.99 bookings, 0% commission, EUR699/EUR999 buyouts, modules). Lower confidence on real-world customer satisfaction and scale: NO independent reviews exist on Trustpilot/G2/Capterra/Reddit and only one case study (Bar de Carnes) was found, so the 'weaknesses' are inferred from the model/support hours/company stage rather than from voiced customer complaints. Company appears very new and founder-run (Pablo Bouzon, Malaga), so figures may change quickly.

profiles.name: Octotable
profiles.positioning: "Commission-free reservation system that lives on your own website" — Italian self-serve booking + digital menu SaaS for restaurants, with an upsell path into the parent company's hotel/spa PMS.
profiles.businessModel: Flat monthly/annual SaaS subscription, freemium funnel. Zero per-cover commission and no activation fee (this is their headline differentiator vs OpenTable/TheFork). Free tier (capped at 30 reservations/mo) feeds paid tiers. Add-ons: a hosted website (~$17-19/mo) and an AI voice/WhatsApp assistant. Octotable is operated by Octorate Srl (Rome), a hotel channel-manager/PMS company — so restaurants are also a top-of-funnel for the bigger Octorate hotel platform. Self-serve onboarding (paste a code snippet / WordPress plugin); NO done-for-you concierge setup.
profiles.pricing: All prices ex-VAT, USD on the EN site (annual = ~20% off; monthly figures shown are the discounted-annual rate). FREE: $0/mo, capped at 30 online reservations/month, reservation page + Reserve with Google + QR digital menu. DIGIMENU: $23/mo (or ~$18.40/mo annual) — unlimited reservations, customizable digital menu, interactive table map, external API, embeddable reservation widget. PREMIUM: $35/mo (~$28 annual) — adds WhatsApp link, automated email + SMS. EVO: $59/mo (~$47.20 annual) — everything: waitlist, customer database, deposits/credit-card guarantee (no-show protection), widget customization, marketing campaigns, automatic WhatsApp replies. Add-on website "from $17/mo." NO activation/setup fee, NO per-cover commission. AI voice assistant is promoted but its price is NOT published anywhere I could find (likely a quote/add-on). Refund clause: subscription credit is "non-refundable, even in case of total or partial non-use." Cancel anytime, no contractual lock-in.
keyFeatures: Embeddable booking widget for the venue's own site (code snippet + WordPress plugin), light/dark theme to match the site
Reserve with Google / Google Maps & Business Profile integration (commission-free)
Real-time table-plan / floor-map management and shift organization
QR-code digital menu + at-table ordering
Waitlist management
Customer database with guest preferences/history
No-show protection: deposits and credit-card guarantees (EVO tier)
Automated comms: email, SMS, WhatsApp reminders & review requests
AI voice assistant answering calls 24/7 in 5 languages (FR/EN/DE/ES/IT), handling bookings/changes/cancellations and sending WhatsApp confirmations
Marketing campaigns (SMS/email)
Integration path into Octorate hotel PMS/channel manager/booking engine for hotels, B&Bs, agriturismi with a restaurant or spa
whatWorks: Genuinely commission-free + no activation fee — the same core promise as Guest Overflow, and they hammer it relentlessly as the wedge against OpenTable/TheFork
Widget is white-labelable (can show the venue's logo instead of Octotable's) and embeds on the venue's own domain so guests never leave
Free forever tier (30 res/mo) lowers the barrier for tiny venues to try it with zero risk
Very low price points ($23-$59/mo) — far cheaper than Guest Overflow's EUR99/EUR249 tiers
No contractual lock-in, cancel anytime
Strong Google ecosystem play (Reserve with Google) that drives free direct bookings
Bundled value: booking + digital menu + at-table ordering + AI phone agent in one cheap subscription
Clear GDPR posture: terms state the contract is only between the restaurant and the guest and that Octotable 'does not act as an intermediary'
Credible parent (Octorate) gives a real upsell into hotel/spa PMS, useful for boutique-hotel prospects
weaknesses: Almost no independent social proof: no Capterra/Trustpilot/G2 review presence found; the iOS app 'hasn't received enough ratings to display'; one aggregator showed a weak ~2.3/5 — buyers cannot validate it via reviews
iOS App Store version history is dominated by repeated 'Bug fixing' releases, hinting at stability churn
100% self-serve — no concierge/done-for-you setup or strategic reviews; a non-technical owner must paste code/install a plugin and configure flows themselves
Harsh refund clause: subscription credit 'non-refundable, even in case of total or partial non-use'
AI voice assistant heavily marketed but pricing is opaque/unpublished — likely a hidden upsell on top of the advertised tiers
Prices shown ex-VAT and in USD on a fundamentally Italian/EU product — actual EU cost is ~22% higher than the sticker
No-show protection (deposits/card guarantees) gated to the top EVO tier only
Restaurant-centric; spa/wellness & hotel use really require buying the separate, heavier Octorate platform
Brand/marketing feels SEO-content-heavy and thin on real customer stories; trust signals are weak
profiles.dataOwnership: Strongly venue-favorable on paper. Terms state the booking contract is only between the restaurant and the guest, that "Octotable does not act as an intermediary," and that the restaurant is responsible for its own data input. Octorate Srl is the data controller for GDPR purposes; users get full GDPR rights including data portability in a "structured, commonly used, machine-readable format" and right to erasure. No marketplace sits between venue and guest, and Octotable does not store payment-card data (uses third-party processors). Net: the venue effectively owns its guest list — comparable to Guest Overflow's promise, though Guest Overflow can claim a cleaner "you own it, period" story since Octotable is still technically the data controller/processor.
profiles.whiteLabel: Yes — white-label and embedded. The widget installs on the venue's own website via a code snippet or WordPress plugin, supports the venue's logo (instead of Octotable's) and light/dark theming, and keeps guests on the venue's domain without redirecting to a marketplace. There is NO consumer marketplace/discovery directory (unlike OpenTable/TheFork). Distribution is via the venue's site + social + Google (Reserve with Google), all syncing to one dashboard. Branding control is real but slightly weaker than Guest Overflow: it's a configurable widget you self-install, not a bespoke founder-built install fully wearing the venue's brand end-to-end.
profiles.targetSegment: Independent restaurants, bars, pizzerias, pubs — primarily Italy/EU — that want direct, commission-free bookings on their own site. Secondary: boutique hotels, agriturismi, B&Bs and venues with a spa/restaurant, funneled toward the parent Octorate hospitality platform. Skews toward price-sensitive, self-serve owners comfortable pasting a code snippet.
profiles.threatToGuestOverflow: HIGH on overlap, MEDIUM as a head-to-head threat. The core pitch is nearly identical to Guest Overflow — commission-free, white-label, embedded on the venue's own site, venue owns the data — and Octotable delivers it at roughly a quarter of the price ($23-$59/mo vs EUR99-EUR249/mo) with a free tier. That directly undercuts Guest Overflow on price and proves the model works. BUT Octotable is pure self-serve SaaS with weak social proof, no concierge setup, no PT-language localization focus, and harsh refund terms. In Portugal/EU it has limited brand presence and no done-for-you positioning. So it threatens Guest Overflow most on price-sensitive DIY restaurants, and least on the boutique/concierge segment Guest Overflow actually targets.
lessonsForUs: COPY the relentless, specific anti-commission messaging ('no per-cover fee, no activation fee, you keep 100% of revenue') — it is clearly the wedge that converts OpenTable/TheFork refugees.

COPY a free/low-friction trial path (e.g. a capped free tier or a longer free pilot) to lower the barrier vs our EUR99 entry; our 'half price for 3 months' is good but a true risk-free trial converts cautious owners.

EXPLOIT their biggest weakness — they are pure self-serve with weak social proof. Lead hard with concierge done-for-you setup + quarterly reviews + named founder; that is exactly what a non-technical restaurateur fears about Octotable.

BEAT them on trust: publish real named customer case studies, before/after no-show numbers, and visible reviews. Octotable has almost no third-party reviews — owning that space is cheap differentiation.

JUSTIFY our higher price explicitly: frame Octotable's $23-$59 as 'software you install and run yourself' vs Guest Overflow as 'a partner who builds, installs, and optimizes it for you.' Make the price gap a feature, not a liability.
AVOID their harsh 'non-refundable even if unused' clause — our no-questions exit is a marketing weapon; make it loud.
AVOID hidden-priced add-ons (their AI assistant pricing is opaque). Keep our pricing fully transparent including any AI/no-show features.
MATCH their Google 'Reserve with Google' integration — it's table stakes for free direct bookings and they use it well.
DON'T gate no-show protection (deposits/card guarantees) to a top tier only the way they gate it to EVO; bundling it earlier is a differentiator.

LEAN into PT/EN bilingual + EU/Portugal local presence and GDPR clarity — Octotable is Italy-centric and shows prices ex-VAT in USD, which feels off to an EU buyer; a clean EUR, VAT-inclusive, localized offer reads more trustworthy.
WATCH the Octorate upsell model: their restaurant tool funnels into a hotel PMS. If we want boutique-hotel/spa clients we should similarly have a credible path beyond a single booking widget.
profiles.confidence: Medium-high on pricing, features, white-label and data-ownership claims (taken directly from Octotable's own pricing/terms pages, June 2026). LOW on real customer complaints: Octotable has almost no independent review footprint — no Capterra/Trustpilot/G2 pages found, the iOS app lacks enough ratings to display, only a weak ~2.3/5 aggregator signal and a pattern of 'bug fixing' app updates. So the 'weaknesses' are partly inferred from structural/terms evidence rather than verbatim user reviews. Confidence that the pricing numbers are current: high; confidence on AI-assistant pricing: none (unpublished).

profiles.name: resOS
profiles.positioning: "Affordable, commission-free restaurant booking system — from free to cheap." A self-serve, EU-built (Denmark) reservation/table-management SaaS for independent restaurants that want OpenTable-grade features without per-cover fees.
profiles.businessModel: Flat monthly SaaS subscription tiered by booking VOLUME (not per cover), with a genuine permanent free tier as the acquisition funnel and a menu of cheap paid add-ons (deposits, white-label design, SMS, API, etc.) layered on top. Explicitly zero commission and zero cover fees — they monetize purely on subscription + add-ons. Payment processing (deposits/no-show fees) runs through the venue's own Stripe.
profiles.pricing: Currency shown in USD (also EUR/GBP). Four tiers, all commission-free: Free = $0 (25 bookings/mo, all core features). Basic = $47/mo (promo $24/mo) ~350 bookings/mo. Plus = $98/mo (promo $49/mo) ~750 bookings/mo. Unlimited = $149/mo (promo $75/mo) unlimited bookings. Annual billing = ~20% discount (one month free). 90-day money-back guarantee, no contract, pause/cancel anytime, no setup fee. Paid add-ons run roughly EUR 3.99–11.99/mo each and include: Deposits & No-show fees (via Stripe), Customisable design / brand-match (white-label), SMS confirmations, Meta Pixel, Google Analytics, custom booking-form fields, full API access, online waitlist, Reserve with Facebook. NOTE: some third-party sources still cite an older $35/$65/$95 tier set — the current site shows $47/$98/$149.
keyFeatures: Embeddable online booking form/widget for the venue's own website
Visual table/floor planner and table management
Guest CRM with profiles, preferences and history
Reserve with Google integration (no extra fee for Google bookings)
No-show protection: deposits per guest/booking, automated no-show fee charging, credit-card guarantees for large parties, repeat-no-show flagging (paid add-on via Stripe)
Automated SMS + email confirmations and reminders
Waitlist management
Takeaway/order management module
Customisable widget design (300+ colours, fonts, layouts, logo) — sold as a paid add-on
Analytics: booking volume/trends, no-show & cancellation rates, peak hours, guest feedback
20+ language multilingual support
Full API access + Zapier (1,000+ apps), MailChimp integration
GDPR compliant with export/import of booking data
Smart review solicitation / Google Reviews tools
whatWorks: Genuine never-expiring free tier (25 bookings/mo) — frictionless acquisition that converts small venues
Cheapest serious commission-free option — paid plans from $47/mo vs OpenTable/Resy at $149-$249/mo; their own comparison table leans hard on this
Truly flat, predictable pricing with NO per-cover commission — the central, repeated selling point
Strong, fast, free human support repeatedly praised in Trustpilot/Tekpon reviews (4.6/5 on Tekpon across ~395 reviews)
Highly customizable widget (300+ colours, fonts, layouts, own logo) — venues can make it look on-brand
Reserve with Google bundled at no extra commission — captures high-intent search traffic
Self-serve setup in ~10-30 minutes; no contract, 90-day money-back, pause anytime lowers risk
Built-in deposits / no-show protection with real proof points (deposit policies cut no-shows)
EU-built, GDPR-compliant, 20+ languages — good fit for European independents
weaknesses: Powerful customization makes it 'a bit confusing to set up' — multiple reviewers cite a learning curve
Guest-facing booking flow described as clunky/confusing in some reviews — completing a reservation isn't as smooth as it should be
Analytics and booking-interface customization called shallow / 'lacking depth' by reviewers wanting more
Brand-matching/white-label DESIGN is a PAID add-on, not included — base widget can feel generic unless you pay extra
Nickel-and-diming risk: core is cheap but deposits, SMS, API, custom design, Meta Pixel, analytics extras are all separate add-ons that stack up
Small diner network vs marketplaces (OpenTable/TheFork) — no demand-generation, you must bring your own traffic
Newer brand with low review volume on the big directories (0 reviews on Capterra, only ~34 on Trustpilot) — less social proof
Fewer enterprise/legacy POS integrations than incumbents
At least one Trustpilot complaint about a 'Reserve a Table' button appearing on a venue's Google listing that the owner felt they hadn't authorized
Self-serve only — no done-for-you setup or ongoing concierge/strategy review; the venue does the work
profiles.dataOwnership: Venue-favourable. resOS is GDPR-compliant, gives a Guest CRM and explicitly advertises 'Export your data anytime' plus import/export of booking data on every plan, so the venue retains and can extract guest emails/history. Because it is not a consumer marketplace, there is no diner network siphoning the relationship the way OpenTable/TheFork do — though pushing 'Reserve with Google' routes some bookings through Google. Net: low marketplace lock-in, venue effectively owns its guest data.
profiles.whiteLabel: Partial white-label. Booking is an embeddable widget that lives on the venue's OWN website (not primarily a marketplace listing), and design can be matched to the brand (logo, 300+ colours, fonts, layouts) — BUT that brand-matching/custom design is sold as a PAID ADD-ON rather than included by default, so the out-of-the-box widget carries more of a generic/resOS feel until you pay. It also actively promotes Reserve with Google as a booking channel. So: own-site embed yes, true zero-resOS-branding white-label only if you pay for it.
profiles.targetSegment: Independent and small-to-medium restaurants, cafes and fine-dining venues, primarily in Europe (Denmark/EU origin, 20+ languages, GDPR), that want OpenTable-style functionality without commission and are comfortable with self-serve setup. Also serves takeaway-ordering venues. Spans free-tier micro-venues up to multi-area restaurants.
profiles.threatToGuestOverflow: HIGH / direct. resOS is the closest mainstream analogue to Guest Overflow's core promise: commission-free, flat monthly, embeddable-on-your-own-site, venue owns its data, EU/GDPR, multilingual EN/PT-friendly. It is also dramatically cheaper at the entry point (free tier; $47/mo Basic vs GO Essential EUR99/mo) and far more feature-mature. Where GO differs and can defend: (1) resOS is self-serve with a noted setup learning curve and no concierge — GO's done-for-you build + quarterly review is a genuine moat; (2) resOS makes brand-match/white-label and several essentials (deposits, SMS, analytics) paid add-ons that stack up — GO can bundle these into flat tiers and lead with 'fully white-labelled, nothing extra'; (3) resOS targets restaurants mainly — GO's spa/hotel/tours coverage + website builds + Google Business optimization is broader. Pricing pressure is the biggest threat: GO must justify the premium with service, not features.
lessonsForUs: COPY the permanent free tier (or a true free trial with no card) as a top-of-funnel — resOS's free plan is its single biggest acquisition advantage and GO currently has nothing comparable
LEAD relentlessly with the side-by-side commission comparison vs OpenTable/Resy/TheFork ($1-1.50/cover, $149-249/mo) — resOS's own pricing page does this and it converts

BUNDLE what resOS charges extra for: include white-label/brand-match design, deposits/no-show protection, SMS, and analytics in the flat price. Make 'no add-on surprises' an explicit selling point against resOS's stacking add-ons
AVOID resOS's setup learning curve and clunky guest flow — GO's concierge done-for-you build directly neutralizes the #1 complaint; market it as 'we build it for you, guests check out in 2 taps'
Emphasize TRUE white-label by default (resOS charges for brand-match) — guests never see Guest Overflow, on the venue's own domain
Reuse resOS's proof points: 76% of UK venues hit by no-shows (avg loss ~£3,621/yr), deposits cut no-shows, ~30% repeat-customer uplift — strong copy for the no-show-protection pitch

Address the pricing gap head-on: GO's EUR99/249 must be framed as service + done-for-you + quarterly strategy, NOT software — because on pure software price resOS wins. Consider a lower entry tier to compete on the funnel
Match resOS strengths: explicit 'export your data anytime', GDPR, bilingual EN/PT, Reserve with Google integration, Stripe-based deposits — these are now table stakes
Differentiate on vertical breadth (spas, hotels, tours) and on the agency offer (custom websites, Google Business optimization) where resOS is restaurant-only and self-serve
profiles.confidence: High on pricing structure, commission-free model, add-on model, embeddable-widget/data-export and target market (verified directly on resOS's live site). Medium on exact booking-limit numbers per tier (JS-rendered; corroborated via third-party comparison) and on review-based complaints (Capterra has 0 reviews, Trustpilot only ~34; relied on Tekpon's 4.6/395 aggregate and comparison sites, with some prices still citing an older $35/$65/$95 set that the current site contradicts).

profiles.name: Tableo
profiles.positioning: "Affordable restaurant reservation & table management" — a Malta/EU SaaS that bundles a branded booking widget on your own site with multi-channel distribution (Google, TripAdvisor, Michelin, social) in one dashboard, free up to 100 covers/month.
profiles.businessModel: Flat monthly/annual SaaS subscription (freemium → tiered), NOT a per-cover marketplace commission like OpenTable. No commission on website/widget bookings. Revenue add-ons: 1% + Stripe fees on deposits/prepayments, metered SMS/WhatsApp ("transport fees"), and email-marketing overage blocks (~EUR19.99/mo per 10k emails). Channel integrations (Reserve with Google, TripAdvisor, Michelin) are activatable add-ons rather than a commission-driven listing marketplace.
profiles.pricing: EU pricing (annual billing, ~17% off vs monthly): Freemium EUR0 (up to 100 covers/month, unlimited users, no credit card, no time limit); Organizar (Organise) ~EUR49.17/mo — basic reservation mgmt, Google sync, interactive floor map, phone support; Crescer (Grow, "Popular") ~EUR82.50/mo — payments/deposits, SMS notifications, event mgmt, advanced customization, Reserve with Google; Expandir (Expand) ~EUR140.83/mo — chains/hotel restaurants, advanced reporting, AI messaging, dedicated account manager. UK figure cited around GBP59/mo for the Organise tier; USA paid plans cited at roughly USD99–149/mo. Variable fees: deposits 1% + Stripe fees; SMS/WhatsApp metered; email campaigns 1,000/mo included, +EUR19.99/mo per 10k. Free setup, training and support included; 2 months free on annual. No setup fee, no per-cover commission on direct bookings.
keyFeatures: Fully branded/customizable booking widget embedded on the venue's own website (fonts, colours, content)
Centralized dashboard aggregating bookings from website, Google, social and travel channels
Interactive floor map / table management with timing & seating parameters
Guest database/CRM: visit history, preferences, notes, frequency segmentation
Deposits, prepayments and card-block no-show protection via Stripe
Automated email + SMS/WhatsApp reminders and reconfirmations
Channel integrations: Reserve with Google, TripAdvisor, Michelin Guide, Facebook/Instagram, Bookia
Waiting list, blacklist, event management, restaurant-chain/multi-location support
Reporting & analytics (higher tiers); AI Assistant currently free
Free onboarding: setup, training and support included on all plans
whatWorks: Genuine freemium (100 covers/mo, no credit card, no time limit) lowers adoption friction for small venues — a strong wedge
Exceptional review scores: ~4.9/5 on Capterra; reviewers repeatedly praise after-sales service and hands-on customer care
Flat-fee, commission-free on direct bookings is the explicit anti-OpenTable selling point
Free setup + training removes the onboarding hurdle independents fear
White-label widget keeps the booking on the venue's brand/site
Multi-channel distribution (Google/TripAdvisor/Michelin) gives discovery upside without a forced marketplace
Company is responsive — ships features users request (e.g., real-time booking pop-ups)
weaknesses: Cancellation alerts historically email-only, not seen during service hours (reviewer complaint)
Too many transactional emails per payment step annoyed guests
Reported bugs in system and widget; no-show fee charging fails on some debit cards
Mobile interface weak for editing/modifying bookings
Pricing felt high once extras (TripAdvisor, Google bookings) stacked on
Card authorization prompts initially deterred some diners from completing bookings
Self-serve setup/configuration depth not a 'done-for-you' concierge — onboarding is assisted but tool-driven
Variable/metered costs (SMS, email overage, 1% deposit fee) make the 'flat price' less flat in practice
Small review base (~15-16 Capterra reviews) — limited independent validation at scale
profiles.dataOwnership: Restaurant-favourable but platform-mediated. The venue gets a full guest database/CRM (history, preferences, notes, segmentation) and is the GDPR data controller; Tableo positions itself as processor and forwards GDPR/data requests to the restaurant. However, the data lives in Tableo's SaaS — there is NO clearly advertised one-click full export (unlike OpenTable's documented CSV guestbook export), so practical portability/lock-in is ambiguous and undocumented on the public site. This is an exploitable gap for Guest Overflow's "you own and can export everything" promise.
profiles.whiteLabel: Hybrid. The booking form is a fully branded white-label widget embedded on the venue's own website (venue's fonts/colours, guests don't leave the site). But Tableo also pushes bookings through external channels (Reserve with Google, TripAdvisor, Michelin, social) where the venue is a listing inside those third-party platforms — so it's white-label on-site plus marketplace-style distribution off-site, not a single Tableo-branded consumer marketplace.
profiles.targetSegment: Independent and small-to-mid restaurants in the EU/UK (Malta HQ), expanding to US; from single sites on the free tier up to hotel restaurants and small chains on the Expand tier. Strong fit for venues that want commission-free direct bookings plus Google/TripAdvisor discovery.
profiles.threatToGuestOverflow: High overlap, direct competitor. Same core pitch — white-label, commission-free booking embedded on the venue's own site, flat monthly pricing, EU-based, free/assisted setup. Tableo undercuts Guest Overflow on entry price (free up to 100 covers; Organise ~EUR49 vs Essential EUR99) and has proven 4.9-star traction and multi-channel distribution Guest Overflow doesn't advertise. Restaurant-only, though — it does not cover hotels/spas/tours/activities, and it is software-led rather than concierge done-for-you, which is where Guest Overflow differentiates.
lessonsForUs: Add a real free or trial tier (e.g., free up to N covers/bookings, no card) — Tableo's freemium is its sharpest acquisition wedge and our EUR99 floor looks steep beside EUR0/EUR49
Lean hard on the data-ownership gap: advertise explicit one-click full CSV/JSON export and a written 'you own your data, no lock-in' guarantee — Tableo does not document this
Double down on concierge done-for-you setup + quarterly reviews as the differentiator; Tableo's onboarding is assisted-but-self-serve, not founder-built
Expand beyond restaurants (hotels, spas, tours) — Tableo is restaurant-only, leaving Guest Overflow's multi-vertical positioning open
Fix the friction Tableo's reviewers hate: consolidate transactional emails into one, surface cancellations as in-app/push/SMS not email-only, and make mobile booking edits first-class
Be honest/transparent about variable costs (SMS, deposits) where Tableo's 'flat' price quietly meters — make truly flat pricing a headline
Consider optional discovery channels (Reserve with Google) as an add-on so we don't cede the discovery upside while staying commission-free
Match their service reputation: responsive support and shipping requested features is what earns Tableo its 4.9 stars — make concierge support a defensible moat
profiles.confidence: Medium-high. Pricing tiers, freemium terms, commission-free model, white-label widget, channel integrations and the specific review complaints are confirmed against Tableo's own pages and Capterra. Lower confidence on: exact USD figures (cited secondhand at ~USD99-149, not confirmed on a loaded US pricing table — the US page geo-served EU pricing), precise per-add-on channel fees (not published), and data-export specifics (Trustpilot blocked by 403; export not documented publicly).

profiles.name: Formitable (now Formitable by Zenchef)
profiles.positioning: "Commission-free online reservations on your own website" — a brand-first booking widget for guest-experience-led restaurants that want to own their bookings and data rather than rent traffic from a marketplace.
profiles.businessModel: Flat monthly SaaS subscription per location (tiered), with zero per-cover/per-booking commission on normal reservations. Revenue add-ons: a per-transaction fee only on online payments (prepayments, deposits, tickets/events, gift vouchers), plus the payment processor's own fees (Mollie/Stripe). Now sold as part of the broader Zenchef "OS" suite (CRM, marketing, reviews, POS integrations) which is the upsell path. Direct-to-restaurant sales (demo + custom quote), not self-serve.
profiles.pricing: Currency EUR. Legacy Formitable plans were three tiers — roughly Basic, Standard, Pro — commonly cited in the ~€100–€250/mo range per location. Transaction fee on online payments differed by tier: Basic €2.99, Standard €0.99, Pro €0 per transaction (flat, regardless of party size or amount), applied only to prepaid reservations, deposits, tickets and vouchers; ordinary no-payment reservations are always free. Payment processor fees (Mollie/Stripe) are charged separately on top. Post-merger Zenchef pricing is not published — quote-only — with third parties citing starting points from ~€49/location/mo (basic) up to ~€109+/mo, plus extra modules for analytics/marketing; reviewers also report undisclosed/"unexplainable" startup (setup) fees. No public free trial. Confidence: medium — tier names/exact monthly figures are from aggregators and help-center references; the per-transaction fee numbers are well-corroborated.
keyFeatures: White-label booking widget embedded on the venue's own website and social channels (guests stay on the restaurant's site)
Commission-free covers on standard reservations
Guest CRM with full guest profiles, history and tags; exportable guest data
Prepayments, deposits and credit-card guarantees for no-show protection (via Mollie/Stripe)
Tickets/ticketed events and gift vouchers (paid flows)
Table & floor-plan management, waitlist
Automated confirmation/reminder messaging (email/SMS/WhatsApp via Zenchef)
REST API and developer widget for custom integration
Post-merger Zenchef additions: review management, marketing campaigns (email/WhatsApp), auto-tagging, multi-site/group management, POS integrations, post-dining surveys, partnership/discovery network
whatWorks: Strong brand-ownership story resonates with independent and fine-dining venues — became the largest NL reservation platform (~30% of restaurants) on exactly this 'your brand, your site, your data' message
Genuinely commission-free on normal covers, a clear differentiator vs marketplace models (TheFork/OpenTable) that charge per cover
Embedded on the venue's own domain so the venue captures the booking relationship and SEO/traffic value
Venue owns and can export all guest data (positioned heavily in case studies)
No-show mitigation via deposits/prepayments/card guarantees is well-developed and a top reason restaurants adopt it
Backed by credible fintech founder (Adriaan Mol / Mollie) and now resources of the larger Zenchef group; broad feature suite post-merger
weaknesses: Slow/poor support is the dominant complaint: reviewers report ~1 hour for simple answers and up to ~3-week response times from account managers
Undisclosed or 'unexplainable' startup/setup fees surfacing on invoices — billing transparency complaints

Post-merger forced migration: legacy Formitable widget retired, customers auto-moved to ZenchefOS; some report disruption (e.g. deleted historical photos dating to 2012) and feeling pushed onto a new product
Reports of suspicious/spam bookings (notably from France) with support responding by upselling rather than fixing
Long-time paying customers report no loyalty discounts or compensation when problems occur
Pricing opacity — no public pricing, quote-only, hard for small venues to evaluate; transaction fees on paid flows add up
Trustpilot only 'Average' at 3.7/5 (242 reviews) — service reputation is a real liability
Mixed/escalating cost as venues are pushed up-tier into the fuller (more expensive) Zenchef suite
profiles.dataOwnership: Strongly venue-favorable: the restaurant owns its guest and reservation data, can export personal/guest data in full, and is positioned as the legal data controller (GDPR). Marketed explicitly as 'full ownership over restaurant data' with no marketplace intermediary skimming the relationship. On migration to ZenchefOS, guest/booking data is transferred to the venue's new account. This is the closest competitor parallel to Guest Overflow's data-ownership promise.
profiles.whiteLabel: White-label / venue-branded. The booking widget is embedded directly on the venue's own website and social channels; guests book on the restaurant's site rather than on a Formitable/Zenchef marketplace. (Zenchef has since added a 'discovery'/partnership network, but the core model remains own-site, branded booking — not a directory listing.)
profiles.targetSegment: Independent and brand-conscious restaurants, especially mid/high-end and fine-dining venues in the Netherlands/Benelux and broader EU that prioritize guest experience and direct ownership of bookings; expanding via Zenchef to multi-site groups and larger operators across Europe.
profiles.threatToGuestOverflow: High and direct. This is the single closest competitor to Guest Overflow's core thesis: white-label widget on the venue's own domain, commission-free covers, venue owns/exports guest data, plus no-show deposits/card guarantees. They beat us on brand recognition, scale (~30% NL, 20k+ restaurants via Zenchef), funding, and feature breadth. However, they are restaurant-only (no spa/hotel/tours), increasingly enterprise/group-focused, quote-only and opaque on price, self-serve onboarding (no concierge done-for-you setup), and carry a weak support reputation post-merger — all of which are exploitable gaps.
lessonsForUs: COPY the core promise — they validated that 'commission-free, on your own site, you own your data' is a winning wedge against marketplaces; keep this front and center and make it sharper/simpler than their opaque messaging

COPY no-show protection done well (deposits, card guarantees, configurable refund windows) — it's a top adoption driver; bundle it without a per-transaction surcharge to undercut their €0.99–€2.99 paid-flow fees
WIN on price transparency — they are quote-only with surprise setup fees; publish our flat €99/€249 pricing openly and make 'no hidden setup fees' an explicit headline (their #1 billing complaint)
WIN on support — their biggest weakness is slow support (1hr–3wk responses); lean into founder-led concierge setup + quarterly reviews as the antidote, and guarantee fast response times in writing
WIN on no lock-in — counter their forced migration and lack of loyalty goodwill with our 'no-questions exit' and easy full data export front-loaded in the pitch
EXPAND beyond their lane — they are restaurant-only; our spa/hotel/tours coverage is a differentiator, target verticals they ignore
AVOID their merger trap — don't force breaking platform migrations on existing customers; treat data continuity and historical content (photos/history) as sacred
AVOID feature-bloat upsell pressure — keep tiers honest so customers don't feel funneled into ever-pricier suites
Use against marketplaces too — position the commission-free + concierge combo against TheFork/OpenTable as well, since Formitable's success proves the demand exists
profiles.confidence: Medium-high. Business model, white-label/own-site model, commission-free positioning, data ownership, no-show features, NL ~30% share, the Zenchef merger, and the per-transaction fee tiers (€0/€0.99/€2.99) are well-corroborated across the company's own help center, the merger article, and multiple aggregators. Confidence is lower on exact current monthly EUR figures because Formitable's pricing page is gone and Zenchef pricing is quote-only — the €100–€250/mo and €49–€109/mo figures come from third-party aggregators, not a primary published price list. Complaint themes are drawn from Trustpilot (3.7/5, 242 reviews) and review-site summaries rather than a large independently audited sample.

proven.title: Commission-free flat pricing is the single most-proven adoption wedge
proven.detail: Nearly every commission-free competitor leads with 'flat fee, zero per-cover commission' as the #1 reason customers switch, and it is repeatedly cited as what wins deals. Resy's flat fee is called 'the #1 reason restaurants pick Resy over OpenTable.' Zenchef quotes ~EUR600/mo saved in marketplace commissions. ResDiary's commission-free flat fee is 'the #1 reason venues switch from OpenTable/TheFork.' The pain it inverts is concrete and quantifiable: OpenTable cover fees alone run $1,500-$2,250/mo at 1,500 covers; TheFork costs EUR8k-35k/yr and charges on REPEAT guests ('your most valuable guests become your most expensive acquisition channel'). Guest Overflow must hammer this with a side-by-side cost calculator and make 'no per-cover commission, ever' a contractual promise, not a slogan. Critically, GO's flat EUR99/249 reads as expensive at LOW volume but cheaper at scale, so the message must be total-cost-of-ownership at realistic volume.
proven.evidence: Resy, Zenchef, ResDiary, Eat App, Tablein, Superb, Formitable, resOS, Tableo, Octotable, CoverManager, Bouzon (all flat/commission-free); TheFork & OpenTable (the quantified pain being inverted)

proven.title: 'You own your guest data' is proven messaging — but customers now demand a contractual export guarantee
proven.detail: Data ownership is the second universal selling point across commission-free players (SevenRooms' 'historically the #1 selling point vs OpenTable'; Resy, Zenchef, Eat App, resOS, Formitable all market it). What makes it a WINNING (not just table-stakes) play is OpenTable literally amending its contract to block data export to rivals, and the Fresha/Quandoo cases where card data can't be exported or the platform owns the diner outright. The proven differentiator is not just claiming ownership but guaranteeing frictionless full export: resOS advertises 'export your data anytime' on every plan; Eat App offers 'full unrestricted export.' Several competitors are weak here precisely because export terms aren't documented (Tableo has no one-click export advertised; Tablein's history has a 'time delay'). GO should make one-click full CSV/JSON export a written, contractual promise — this is a cheap, defensible wedge against both marketplaces and even commission-free rivals who under-document it.
proven.evidence: SevenRooms, Resy, Zenchef, Eat App, resOS, Formitable (claim it); OpenTable (contract block), Fresha (non-exportable card data), Quandoo (platform owns diner) as the cautionary foils

proven.title: No-show protection (deposits + card guarantees) is non-negotiable table stakes
proven.detail: Every serious competitor offers deposits, card-on-file guarantees, and prepayment, and it is consistently cited as a top reason upscale/high-value venues pay. Tock's entire business is built on prepaid/ticketing slashing no-shows (French Laundry/Alinea heritage). Zenchef claims partner no-shows under 1%; CoverManager claims under 2%. resOS cites that 76% of UK venues are hit by no-shows (avg loss ~GBP3,621/yr) and that deposits demonstrably cut them. The proven best-practice MODEL is Stripe-based tokenization that is free until a card is actually charged (ResDiary, Bouzon, resOS run deposits through the venue's own Stripe with no platform cut). GO's Premium already includes this — but the lesson is to (a) implement transparent, fast refund logic to avoid the no-show-fee BACKLASH that damaged Resy, Treatwell (~92% negative on cancellation handling), Fresha, and Booksy, and (b) not gate it only to the top tier the way Octotable (EVO-only) and Eat App (paid add-on) do.
proven.evidence: Tock, Zenchef, CoverManager, resOS, ResDiary, Bouzon, TheFork, Tablein, Superb, Formitable (all offer it); Resy, Treatwell, Fresha, Booksy (no-show-fee backlash to avoid)

proven.title: Done-for-you / concierge onboarding is a proven loyalty driver AND the antidote to the #1 complaint
proven.detail: The single most consistent NEGATIVE across self-serve competitors is painful onboarding and a steep learning curve: SevenRooms ('steep onboarding,' 'overwhelming features'), ResDiary ('badly designed Excel,' inadequate training), CoverManager (steep learning curve), Eat App ('support almost non-existent during setup'), Mews/Cloudbeds (8-week onboarding, demo-vs-reality gap), SimplyBook.me/resOS ('confusing to set up'). Conversely, the competitors that offer done-for-you setup get praised for it and use it to lower switching friction: FareHarbor's free concierge account-manager build is its most-loved feature alongside support; Superb migrates reservations + guest data + installs the widget + trains staff; TheFork and OpenTable provide dedicated account managers and free onboarding. This validates GO's founder-led concierge model as a genuine moat. GO should go further than all of them with the QUARTERLY review (no-shows/repeat guests) — Eat App reserves quarterly business reviews for Enterprise only, and most rivals offer one-time onboarding, not ongoing advisory.
proven.evidence: FareHarbor, Superb, TheFork, OpenTable (concierge as strength); SevenRooms, ResDiary, CoverManager, Eat App, Mews, Cloudbeds, resOS, SimplyBook.me (onboarding pain validating the wedge)

proven.title: True white-label embed on the venue's own domain is a structurally defensible winning play
proven.detail: The competitors closest to GO's thesis embed a branded widget on the venue's own site so guests never hit a marketplace (SevenRooms, Tablein, Formitable, resOS, Octotable, Bouzon, CoverManager). This is proven to resonate with brand-conscious independents and is something marketplaces STRUCTURALLY cannot match: Resy/OpenTable/Tock/Treatwell/Fresha/Booksy/Quandoo all force the platform brand on the guest (Tock 'literally won't let venues remove its logo'; Fresha forces a Fresha account; OpenTable's confirmation emails wear OpenTable branding). The decisive lesson: many 'white-label' rivals charge EXTRA or gate it for true brand removal — resOS sells brand-match as a paid add-on, SimplyBook.me locks branding removal behind its top Premium tier, Eat App charges $19/mo for a customizable widget and reviewers can't even upload a logo, Zenchef/ResDiary still surface their own marketplace (Dish Cult / Zenchef app). GO should make fully invisible, on-domain white-label STANDARD in the base tier and contrast 'guests never see a third party, every email wears your brand.'
proven.evidence: SevenRooms, Tablein, Formitable, resOS, Octotable, Bouzon, CoverManager (on-domain embed); Tock, Fresha, OpenTable, Treatwell, Booksy, Quandoo (marketplace branding); resOS, SimplyBook.me, Eat App (pay-to-unlock branding to avoid)

proven.title: Pricing transparency (publish the number, no sales call) is a proven trust/conversion advantage
proven.detail: A striking pattern: most incumbents HIDE pricing behind a demo/quote (TheFork 'ask for pricing,' OpenTable, Resy, SevenRooms, Zenchef, ResDiary, CoverManager, Mews, Cloudbeds, Little Hotelier premium tier, Superb's 'Customize' calculator, Formitable). This opacity is repeatedly cited as a friction point and trust-eroder, and surprise/creeping costs are among the loudest complaints (ResDiary '$69 advertised vs GBP154 billed,' Cloudbeds/Mews mid-contract hikes, Formitable 'unexplainable startup fees,' CoverManager 'costs not mentioned in the demo'). The competitors that DO publish — resOS, Tablein, Eat App, Tableo, Octotable, SimplyBook.me, Booksy, Bouzon — are the lean challengers winning price-shoppers. GO already publishes EUR99/249; it should weaponize 'see our price, no sales call required' and 'no setup fee, no surprise add-ons' as explicit headlines.
proven.evidence: Transparent: resOS, Tablein, Eat App, Tableo, Octotable, Bouzon, Booksy, SimplyBook.me; Opaque (the pain): TheFork, OpenTable, Resy, SevenRooms, Zenchef, ResDiary, CoverManager, Mews, Cloudbeds, Formitable, Superb

proven.title: No lock-in / easy exit is proven demand — long contracts and cancellation traps drive churn
proven.detail: Contract lock-in and cancellation friction generate some of the most damaging complaints in the dataset: Mews 2-year lock-in is its '#1 churn driver'; OpenTable's 1-year auto-renew; Zenchef's annual auto-renew with 'strict cancellation deadlines' forcing full-year payment; Little Hotelier billed a user 14 months (~USD1,900) after a termination attempt; Mindbody/Cloudbeds refused cancellations and charged after cancel requests; Quandoo's 3-month-notice rolling contracts and 'continues to direct debit after you quit'; ResDiary's mandatory 3-month minimum. Conversely, no-contract/cancel-anytime is a marketed strength for Zappy, Superb (0-month), Booksy, Tablein, resOS (90-day money-back), Rezdy, Bokun, SimplyBook.me, Octotable, Bouzon. GO's 'no-questions exit' founding offer directly matches the proven winning posture — it should be made loud and placed next to competitors' cancellation horror stories.
proven.evidence: Lock-in pain: Mews, OpenTable, Zenchef, Little Hotelier, Mindbody, Cloudbeds, Quandoo, ResDiary; No-lock-in as a sell: Zappy, Superb, Booksy, Tablein, resOS, Rezdy, Bokun, SimplyBook.me

proven.title: Reserve with Google integration is now table-stakes free-direct-booking distribution
proven.detail: A large majority of booking competitors integrate Reserve with Google as a commission-free channel that captures high-intent search traffic without a marketplace: Zenchef, ResDiary, Resy, SevenRooms, Cloudbeds, resOS, Tablein, Octotable, Tableo, CoverManager, Quandoo, Little Hotelier all surface it. resOS bundles it 'at no extra commission'; Octotable uses it as a core free-bookings driver. Because GO does NOT generate marketplace discovery demand, Reserve with Google (plus Instagram/Facebook 'Reserve' buttons, also widely supported) is the proven, on-brand way to give venues a discovery channel without surrendering data — and pairs with GO's Google Business Profile optimization upsell. This should be standard, not an add-on.
proven.evidence: Zenchef, ResDiary, Resy, SevenRooms, Cloudbeds, resOS, Tablein, Octotable, Tableo, CoverManager, Little Hotelier, Quandoo (all integrate Reserve with Google)

proven.title: Fast, human support is the most-praised attribute and a repeatable moat
proven.detail: Where competitors are LOVED, it is overwhelmingly for responsive human support: Tablein's fast human support is 'the single most-praised attribute' (5.0/5 Capterra); FareHarbor's 24/7 support is its #1 praised feature (4.7/5, 1,100+ reviews); CoverManager's 'close, family-like' Spanish support; Eat App, resOS, Superb, Zenchef (when reached by phone) all praised. Where competitors are HATED, slow/scripted/email-only support dominates the complaints: Mindbody (3+ days), Mews, Cloudbeds ('ticket black holes, no phone number'), Little Hotelier, Tock (email-only), Rezdy ('no phone, no live chat'), Treatwell ('48-72h, no weekends'), Booksy, Quandoo. GO's founder-led concierge is a natural fit — but the lesson is to provide a NAMED human who answers fast (and ideally evening/weekend coverage, which Bouzon's Mon-Fri 9-6 notably lacks for a hospitality product).
proven.evidence: Praised: Tablein, FareHarbor, CoverManager, Eat App, resOS, Superb; Hated: Mindbody, Mews, Cloudbeds, Little Hotelier, Tock, Rezdy, Treatwell, Booksy, Quandoo

proven.title: A free or genuinely risk-free entry tier is the proven top-of-funnel for independents
proven.detail: The lean challengers winning small independents almost all use a free/freemium or no-card trial as the acquisition wedge: resOS (free 25 bookings/mo forever — 'its single biggest acquisition advantage'), Tableo (free up to 100 covers/mo, no card, no time limit), Octotable (free 30 res/mo), SimplyBook.me (free 50 bookings/mo), Eat App (free tier + 14-day no-card trial), Booksy ($29.99, 14-day no-card trial), Tablein (14-day trial), Zappy (14-day trial), Fresha/Booksy/Mindbody historically free to seed density. GO's EUR99 floor looks steep beside EUR0-49 rivals; its 'half-price for 3 months + no-questions exit' helps but is not a true no-risk trial. GO should consider a capped free tier or guided free pilot so price-shoppers don't default to resOS/Tableo/Octotable, and must justify the premium explicitly as service (concierge + quarterly review), not software.
proven.evidence: resOS, Tableo, Octotable, SimplyBook.me, Eat App, Booksy, Tablein, Zappy (free/no-card-trial funnels); contrasted against GO's EUR99 entry floor

proven.title: All-in-one bundling (booking + floor plan + CRM + reminders + analytics) that justifies the subscription
proven.detail: Customers reward platforms that replace multiple tools in one subscription, and reviewers rate operator tooling highly even when they resent fees: OpenTable's software scores 4.4-4.7 (floor plan, CRM, no-show/repeat-guest reporting); SevenRooms is praised for best-in-class CRM + marketing automation; Zappy bundles agenda+clients+packs+invoicing+payments+marketing; Zenchef/CoverManager/Eat App/Superb sell all-in-one breadth as a reason to consolidate. The PROVEN baseline feature set GO must match to be credible: branded booking widget, visual floor/table management, guest CRM with history/tags/preferences, automated email/SMS reminders (which 'demonstrably reduce no-shows' across Square, Tablein, resOS), waitlist, and analytics on covers/no-shows/repeat guests. The flip-side lesson is to AVOID the modular nickel-and-diming that rivals are hated for (resOS, ResDiary, Eat App, SimplyBook.me, Zappy, Mindbody all stack paid add-ons) — bundle the obvious essentials into the flat tier.
proven.evidence: OpenTable, SevenRooms, Zappy, Zenchef, CoverManager, Eat App, Superb (loved breadth); reminders cut no-shows: Square, Tablein, resOS; add-on nickel-and-diming to avoid: ResDiary, Eat App, SimplyBook.me, Zappy, Mindbody

proven.title: Own loyalty / repeat-guest tooling beats cross-venue marketplace loyalty
proven.detail: Marketplaces use loyalty programs that drive repeat business to the PLATFORM/competitors, not the venue — and this is a noted weakness: TheFork's Yums 'steers diners to competitor restaurants'; OpenTable Dining Rewards and Resy/Amex Global Dining Access build the marketplace's audience; Mindbody/Fresha/Booksy actively surface YOUR clients to competitors when you're full. Meanwhile venue-owned CRM + segmentation + marketing campaigns are praised where present (SevenRooms, CoverManager 'loyalize without sharing data with competitors,' Eat App loyalty suite, Zenchef). The proven play for GO: give the venue its OWN repeat-guest loyalty/marketing tooling that benefits only them, framed as the antidote to cross-venue programs — reinforcing the 'your guests are yours' thesis.
proven.evidence: TheFork (Yums), OpenTable (Dining Rewards), Resy (Amex), Mindbody/Fresha/Booksy (route clients to competitors) as the anti-pattern; SevenRooms, CoverManager, Eat App, Zenchef (venue-owned CRM/loyalty as the proven alternative)

proven.title: Multi-vertical breadth (hotels/spas/tours) and EN/PT localization is open, under-contested ground
proven.detail: Almost every restaurant competitor is restaurant-ONLY (Tablein, resOS, Tableo, Octotable, Bouzon, Zenchef, CoverManager, Formitable, ResDiary, Superb, Eat App), and the hotel (Cloudbeds, Mews, Little Hotelier), spa (Zappy, Fresha, Treatwell, Mindbody, Booksy) and tours (FareHarbor, Rezdy, Bokun) verticals are served by separate, siloed players that don't cross over. No single competitor credibly spans restaurants + boutique hotels + spas + tours with one white-label commission-free model. Additionally, deep PT/EU localization is a proven moat where it exists (Zappy's certified PT invoicing, MB WAY/Multibanco, Citizen Card, PT-language support is 'hard for foreign tools to match'); CoverManager owns Iberia; most US-centric tools (OpenTable, Resy, Tock, Square, Mindbody, Eat App/MENA) are weak in EU/Portugal. GO's bilingual EN/PT, EU/GDPR-native, multi-vertical positioning is genuinely differentiated — but matching PT payment/compliance table-stakes (MB WAY, certified invoicing) is required to win Portuguese venues.
proven.evidence: Restaurant-only: Tablein, resOS, Tableo, Octotable, Bouzon, Zenchef, CoverManager, Formitable, ResDiary, Superb, Eat App; siloed verticals: Cloudbeds/Mews/Little Hotelier (hotel), Zappy/Fresha/Treatwell/Mindbody/Booksy (spa), FareHarbor/Rezdy/Bokun (tours); PT localization moat: Zappy, CoverManager

proven.title: Be honest about discovery, then pair white-label with Google Business Profile as the demand answer
proven.detail: The one capability GO structurally cannot replicate is marketplace demand generation — and across the dataset this is exactly why venues TOLERATE the players they otherwise resent: OpenTable's network 'is the one thing GO can't replicate'; Fresha/Treatwell/Mindbody/Booksy/TheFork/Quandoo are kept specifically because they 'bring NEW customers' (Fresha's marketplace, Treatwell's 500k daily searchers, Amex Global Dining Access). The proven, repeated lesson for commission-free tools is to NOT pretend to replace discovery, but to position for venues that already have demand (direct/repeat/walk-in) and want to stop paying a toll on it — and to answer the discovery gap with Reserve with Google + the venue's own SEO + GO's Google Business Profile optimization and website-build services. This honest framing is recommended in nearly every commission-free competitor's lessons.
proven.evidence: OpenTable, TheFork (irreplaceable demand network); Fresha, Treatwell, Mindbody, Booksy, Quandoo (kept for new-customer discovery); recurring 'be honest about discovery + pair with GBP' lesson across Resy/Zenchef/resOS/FareHarbor profiles

gaps.title: Per-cover commission on YOUR OWN repeat guests is the single most-hated, most-recurring pain
gaps.detail: Marketplace/hybrid players charge per seated diner even on regulars the venue already owns. TheFork: 40-60% of its bookings are repeat clients yet still billed ~EUR2-6/cover, so 'your most valuable guests become your most expensive acquisition channel' (illustrative EUR8.6k-35k/yr). OpenTable: $1.50/network + $0.25/website cover, a busy Friday costs more than a slow Tuesday, total ~$21.6k-31.8k/yr at volume. Fresha (20%, min ~$6) and Treatwell (35%+VAT first booking) and Mindbody (20%, cap ~$30) and Booksy (30% first visit) all skim acquisition. FareHarbor (~6% guest-paid, can't be absorbed) and Rezdy (3%/booking) and Bokun (1-1.5%, charged even on cancellations) carry it in tours. Guest Overflow's flat EUR99/249 with ZERO per-cover, ever, is the sharpest wedge — build a side-by-side cost-at-volume calculator.
gaps.evidence: TheFork, OpenTable, Fresha, Treatwell, Mindbody, Booksy, FareHarbor, Rezdy, Bokun

gaps.title: Opaque, quote-gated pricing is near-universal and actively resented
gaps.detail: The majority hide prices behind a sales demo: SevenRooms (~$499/mo/feature, 'more costly than anticipated'), Resy ($249-899 sales-gated), Zenchef ('see our price, no sales call' is an open attack), CoverManager (estimates range EUR99 to EUR400-700, 'costs not mentioned in the demo'), Mews, Cloudbeds, Little Hotelier (payment % undisclosed), Superb ('Customize' tool), Mindbody, ResDiary ('$69 advertised vs GBP154 billed'), Tock, Formitable/Zenchef ('unexplainable startup fees'). Guest Overflow already publishes EUR99/EUR249 — make 'see our exact price, no sales call required, no setup fee' a loud, contractual trust signal.
gaps.evidence: SevenRooms, Resy, Zenchef, CoverManager, Mews, Cloudbeds, Little Hotelier, Superb, Mindbody, ResDiary, Tock, Formitable

gaps.title: Add-on / module nickel-and-diming makes 'commission-free' or low headline price a lie at the real total
gaps.detail: Flat-fee competitors recover margin via stacked paid modules so the quoted price is never the real price. Zappy: invoicing EUR6.50-12, GDPR EUR4, extra location EUR10, storage EUR4, branded app EUR29 all separate. ResDiary: SMS GBP10, gift vouchers GBP35, Order&Pay GBP35, even POS/PMS/CRM integrations GBP10 each. Eat App: widget $19, waitlist $19, CRM $39, payments $19, POS $59 add-ons (Pro ~$389 before extras). resOS: deposits, white-label DESIGN, SMS, analytics, API all paid add-ons. SimplyBook.me: 60+ paid 'custom features', costs reported up to 10x base. Mindbody/Cloudbeds/Mews stack payment take-rates on top. Guest Overflow should bundle the obvious essentials (deposits/no-show, white-label branding, SMS, analytics) into flat tiers and promise 'no surprise add-on fees'.
gaps.evidence: Zappy, ResDiary, Eat App, resOS, SimplyBook.me, Mindbody, Cloudbeds, Mews

gaps.title: Poor / slow / scripted support after the sale — the most consistent operational complaint
gaps.detail: Support quality collapses post-onboarding across nearly every player: Cloudbeds ('attentive during setup, then shouting into a void', no phone number, ticket black hole), Mindbody (3+ business days, work orders open 8 months), Little Hotelier (slow, scripted, phone lines fail), Mews (calls days out, accountability complaints), SevenRooms/Resy (inconsistent, slow), Zenchef/Formitable (bot-like, 1hr-3wk responses, billing-aggressive), Booksy & Treatwell (slow/dismissive, chat-only 48-72h, no weekends), Tock (email-only, 'irrelevant' answers), Rezdy (no phone, no live chat). Guest Overflow's founder-led concierge + quarterly reviews + a named human who answers is a directly defensible moat — guarantee response times in writing.
gaps.evidence: Cloudbeds, Mindbody, Little Hotelier, Mews, SevenRooms, Resy, Zenchef, Formitable, Booksy, Treatwell, Tock, Rezdy

gaps.title: Contract lock-in, auto-renewal traps and surprise post-cancellation billing breed deep distrust
gaps.detail: Hated cancellation/lock-in mechanics recur: Zenchef (annual auto-renew, users forced to pay a full year for missing a notice window), OpenTable (1-year auto-renew), Mews (2-year lock-in, its #1 churn driver), Mindbody (12-month, BBB/Trustpilot refused-cancellation complaints), Little Hotelier (billed 14 months ~$1,900 after trying to cancel), ResDiary (3-month minimum), Quandoo (3-month notice, 'continues to direct debit after you quit'), Cloudbeds (refused refunds on early cancel), Octotable ('non-refundable even if unused'). Guest Overflow's no-questions exit / month-to-month should be made loud and contractual, positioned directly against these horror stories.
gaps.evidence: Zenchef, OpenTable, Mews, Mindbody, Little Hotelier, ResDiary, Quandoo, Cloudbeds, Octotable

gaps.title: Data lock-in & marketplace ownership of the guest relationship — the structural villain
gaps.detail: Marketplaces own or restrict the guest data they should not: OpenTable amended its contract (2019) to BLOCK exporting diner data to rivals and requires being 'system of record'; CEO framed blocking rivals as a moral/legal duty. Fresha cannot export encrypted card-on-file data and forces clients into a Fresha account. Quandoo's terms literally make the diner Quandoo's customer and reviews its property — catastrophic now that it is shutting down. Mindbody routes YOUR clients to competitors when you're full and its privacy policy permits selling data for targeted ads. Booksy/Treatwell mediate the relationship ('rented back to you'). DoorDash (SevenRooms) and Amex (Resy/Tock) and Booking Holdings (FareHarbor/Bokun) raise independence fears. Guest Overflow's 'you own your data, full one-click export, no marketplace in between' should be a written contractual promise, weaponized against each.
gaps.evidence: OpenTable, Fresha, Quandoo, Mindbody, Booksy, Treatwell, SevenRooms (DoorDash), Resy/Tock (Amex), FareHarbor/Bokun (Booking Holdings)

gaps.title: Most 'white-label' competitors are not truly invisible — branding leaks via marketplace, app, or paid upsell
gaps.detail: True guests-never-see-the-vendor white-label is rarer than claimed. Tock literally won't let you remove its logo. Zappy's free experience is the shared 'My Zappy' app (true branded app is +EUR29). Square/Fresha/Booksy/Treatwell/Quandoo book on vendor-branded URLs or marketplaces showing competitors. Zenchef/ResDiary/Eat App show their brand via app, emails, Dish Cult microsite, or a paid 'customizable widget' ($19). resOS and SimplyBook.me charge extra to remove branding / add custom domain. Guest Overflow's promise — lives on the venue's own EXISTING domain, guests never see the platform name, branding standard not an add-on — is a genuine, defensible wedge. Say it explicitly vs each named competitor.
gaps.evidence: Tock, Zappy, Square, Fresha, Booksy, Treatwell, Quandoo, Zenchef, ResDiary, Eat App, resOS, SimplyBook.me

gaps.title: Booking caps and per-seat pricing punish growth — a hidden 'success tax' on flat-fee tools
gaps.detail: Several flat-fee tools reintroduce cost as you grow. SimplyBook.me HALTS new bookings at the tier cap (50/100/500/2,000), forcing a whole-plan upgrade for booking volume you don't otherwise need. Tablein adds EUR0.37-0.67 per-reservation overage on lower tiers (a soft per-cover fee). Booksy/Zappy charge per provider/seat (a 5-chair Booksy shop = ~$110/mo before fees; Zappy tiers by provider count). Mindbody charges full base rate per location. Eat App tiers by cover volume. Guest Overflow should make 'unlimited bookings, no cap, flat per-venue price that doesn't punish growth' an explicit headline.
gaps.evidence: SimplyBook.me, Tablein, Booksy, Zappy, Mindbody, Eat App

gaps.title: Clunky, dated, or buggy UX and unreliable booking flows — a recurring product-quality gap
gaps.detail: Operator and guest UX failures repeat: Resy ('clunky/outdated', slow table drag), SevenRooms & Mews & Little Hotelier (mobile app 'impossible to use', laggy, double-booking bugs), ResDiary ('badly designed Excel', 'maximum covers exceeded' errors), Rezdy ('Fred Flintstone' UI, weak mobile app), CoverManager ('overtaken', dated UI, no mobile parity), Zenchef/Formitable (widget resets date on card failure, accidental wrong-date bookings, no 'next available slot' filter), FareHarbor ('14-second spinning wheel' dropped sales ~25%), Tablein (no live update, slow page), OpenTable & Booksy (phantom availability / double-booking). Guest Overflow should ship a fast, mobile-first, live-updating widget with a 'next available slot' search and reliability as a marketing asset.
gaps.evidence: Resy, SevenRooms, Mews, Little Hotelier, ResDiary, Rezdy, CoverManager, Zenchef, Formitable, FareHarbor, Tablein, OpenTable, Booksy

gaps.title: Hidden payment-processing take-rate is the real (undisclosed) cost behind 'commission-free' / 'free' tools
gaps.detail: Many 'flat' or 'free' tools actually monetize via an undisclosed payment cut, so the headline understates cost. Superb (0.99-1.29% + must adopt their POS/payments), Cloudbeds (~1.4%+ + 1% FX), Mews (undisclosed contractual %, held reserves, ~6-day payouts), Little Hotelier (Pay % undisclosed), Square (whole model is 2.5-3.6% processing + fund holds up to 90 days / account deactivations), Mindbody (2.75-3.6%), Fresha/Booksy (processing margin), Zenchef/Formitable (2-4% card + per-SMS on top of 'commission-free'). Guest Overflow can promise pure flat fee with NO payment take-rate and let venues keep their own processor — 'no commission AND no payment cut, keep your existing setup, get your money fast'.
gaps.evidence: Superb, Cloudbeds, Mews, Little Hotelier, Square, Mindbody, Fresha, Booksy, Zenchef, Formitable

gaps.title: Self-serve onboarding pain and steep learning curves leave non-technical owners stranded
gaps.detail: Almost all competitors are DIY/self-serve with heavy setup, the opposite of done-for-you. SevenRooms (steep onboarding, 'overwhelming features'), Mindbody (up to 8-week onboarding), Eat App ('support almost non-existent at setup'), ResDiary & SimplyBook.me & resOS & CoverManager (complex config, learning curve), Rezdy ('3-day setup' taking weeks/months), Square/Octotable/Tablein/Bouzon (paste-a-snippet DIY). Even concierge-ish FareHarbor doesn't review performance over time. Guest Overflow's founder-builds-and-installs-it model plus quarterly review of no-shows/repeat guests is a clear, hard-to-replicate moat — market it as 'we build it for you, you never touch a settings menu'.
gaps.evidence: SevenRooms, Mindbody, Eat App, ResDiary, SimplyBook.me, resOS, CoverManager, Rezdy, Square, Octotable, Tablein, Bouzon, FareHarbor

gaps.title: Bait-and-switch: killing free tiers and unilateral pricing changes destroyed incumbent trust
gaps.detail: Trust-shattering moves create a pool of burned, migrating prospects. Fresha ended its 'free forever' plan in 2025 (permanent reputation damage). Bokun changed pricing ~3x in 2 years and force-migrated free/commission-free users onto monthly + per-booking fees, stripping OTA access from the free tier. Cloudbeds/Mews reported mid-contract hikes ('+30%', 'doubled'). FareHarbor reportedly changes operators' prices/titles/T&Cs without asking. Rezdy 'bait-and-switch' (flat turning into %). Guest Overflow should publicly commit to fee stability, grandfather existing customers, and never introduce a surprise commission — and run targeted 'they changed the deal on you' switch campaigns.
gaps.evidence: Fresha, Bokun, Cloudbeds, Mews, FareHarbor, Rezdy

gaps.title: Time-boxed Quandoo shutdown = a goldmine of stranded, frustrated restaurants to migrate NOW
gaps.detail: Quandoo (Recruit Holdings) is in a confirmed global wind-down: new bookings end 30 Sept 2026, full shutdown 31 Dec 2026, with no buyer. Its terms make the diner Quandoo's customer and reviews its property, so restaurants do not walk away with a portable owned guest database — they lose the channel entirely. This is both the perfect cautionary tale validating Guest Overflow's whole thesis (a data-owning marketplace can vanish overnight) and a live, dated lead source. Run a 'Quandoo is closing — keep your bookings and your guest data' migration campaign with free done-for-you export/import before Dec 2026.

gaps.title: No competitor combines multi-vertical reach (restaurants + hotels + spas + tours) — every rival is siloed
gaps.detail: The market is sharply segmented and most leaders serve only one vertical, leaving white space for a single bilingual EU provider. Restaurant-only: TheFork, Zenchef, ResDiary, Eat App, Tablein, CoverManager, Superb, Formitable, Octotable, Bouzon, resOS, Tock, Resy. Hotel-only: Cloudbeds, Mews, Little Hotelier (and they're overkill/poor fit for restaurants/spas/tours). Spa/wellness-only: Zappy, Fresha, Treatwell, Mindbody, Booksy. Tours-only: FareHarbor, Rezdy, Bokun. A boutique hotel with a restaurant and spa, or an operator running mixed venues, must stitch multiple vendors. Guest Overflow's one commission-free white-label widget across all four verticals (plus GBP optimization + website builds) is genuinely differentiated — lead with it for non-restaurant prospects.
gaps.evidence: All competitors (vertical silos): TheFork/Zenchef/ResDiary/etc. (restaurant), Cloudbeds/Mews/Little Hotelier (hotel), Zappy/Fresha/Treatwell/Mindbody/Booksy (spa), FareHarbor/Rezdy/Bokun (tours)

gaps.title: The honest gap GO must address: no competitor-killing weakness on discovery — marketplaces generate demand GO cannot
