gaps.detail: The one thing GO structurally cannot match is net-new demand generation, and it is precisely why incumbents retain venues despite hated fees: OpenTable's ~60k-restaurant network fills off-peak nights, TheFork + Tripadvisor + Yums drive incremental diners, Resy/Tock via Amex Global Dining Access send affluent cardholders, Fresha/Treatwell/Mindbody/Booksy marketplaces bring first-time bookers, FareHarbor/Rezdy/Bokun pipe into Viator/GetYourGuide. GO should NOT pretend to replace this — instead position for venues that already have demand (own audience, walk-ins, repeat guests) and pair the white-label widget with Google Business Profile optimization + website builds as the legitimate, commission-free 'get found' answer. Honesty here is a credibility wedge, not a weakness.
gaps.evidence: OpenTable, TheFork, Resy, Tock, Fresha, Treatwell, Mindbody, Booksy, FareHarbor, Rezdy, Bokun

gaps.title: Direct same-thesis EU competitors (resOS, Tablein, Octotable, Bouzon) undercut GO on price — service must justify the premium
gaps.detail: The closest analogues already occupy GO's exact positioning (commission-free, white-label, own-site, data-export, EU/GDPR) at a LOWER headline: resOS (free tier; $47/mo Basic vs Essential EUR99), Tablein (free to 100 covers; ~EUR49 Organise), Octotable ($23-59/mo, free 30 res/mo), Bouzon (~EUR58.99/mo all-in INCLUDING a website). On pure software price GO loses. But these are self-serve with weak/no social proof (Octotable has almost no reviews; Bouzon has zero third-party reviews and one case study; resOS/Tablein small review bases), business-hours-only support (Bouzon Mon-Fri 9-6), and no concierge/quarterly review. GO must (a) consider a leaner entry tier or true free trial to compete on the funnel, (b) frame EUR99/249 as 'a partner who builds, installs and optimizes' not software, and (c) win on published case studies, no-show metrics, and extended/weekend support these rivals lack.

product.title: Truly invisible white-label by default (own domain, no platform brand anywhere) — make it the base-tier hero feature
product.detail: Build the booking widget so it renders 100% under the venue's brand on the venue's OWN domain, with zero Guest Overflow branding in the widget, confirmation emails, SMS, or the guest payment step — included in BOTH tiers, never as a paid add-on. This is the single sharpest structural wedge because almost every competitor fails it: Tock literally won't let venues remove its logo (CSS hacks only); Resy/OpenTable/TheFork/Quandoo/Fresha/Treatwell/Booksy/Mindbody surface their own brand or are full marketplaces where guests see the platform and browse competitors; Eat App and SimplyBook.me gate logo/branding removal and custom domain behind paid add-ons/Premium; resOS sells brand-match design as a paid add-on; Square has no custom-domain booking flow at all (Square-branded square.site URL); Tableo/Octotable/Zenchef still show platform brand in places (app, emails, or discovery network). Concretely verify branding is stripped from: widget chrome, the checkout/payment page, all transactional email/SMS, and any hosted fallback page. This is a build priority because it is the one claim no well-funded incumbent can match and it underpins the entire positioning.
product.evidence: Tock (logo non-removable), OpenTable/TheFork/Quandoo/Fresha/Treatwell/Booksy/Mindbody (marketplace-branded), Eat App + SimplyBook.me (white-label is paid add-on), resOS (brand-match is paid add-on), Square (no custom domain), Zenchef/Tableo/Octotable (partial)

product.title: Built-in no-show protection: deposits + card guarantees via the venue's OWN Stripe, with transparent fast-refund rules — bundle in Premium, never as a per-transaction surcharge
product.detail: Ship deposits, full prepayment, and card-on-file guarantees with configurable rules by day/time/party size, automated charging on no-show/late-cancel, repeat-no-show flagging, and a transparent, fast, clearly-communicated refund flow. Route payments through the venue's own Stripe Connect so Guest Overflow takes NO cut and adds NO per-transaction fee (contrast Formitable/Zenchef's EUR0.99–2.99 per paid-booking surcharge stacked on top of Mollie). This is table-stakes that customers demonstrably pay for (Tock built its whole business on prepaid; Zenchef claims <1% no-shows; CoverManager <2%), but it must be done WITHOUT the diner-facing abuse that damages venue reputation: Resy guests charged $25–50 even when cancelling in-window with slow refunds; Treatwell's cancellation handling is ~92% negative on Capterra; Booksy/Fresha charge even after cancel/re-book; OpenTable surprise no-show charges drive 1.5/5 Trustpilot. So the differentiator is not just having deposits — it's transparent rules + venue-controlled policy + clean/fast refunds. Zappy notably lacks any clear deposit/card-guarantee flow, an easy win in spa/wellness.
product.evidence: Tock/Zenchef/CoverManager (proof no-show tooling sells), Resy/Treatwell/Booksy/Fresha/OpenTable (refund/charge abuse to avoid), Formitable/Zenchef (per-transaction surcharge to avoid), Zappy (lacks deposits)

product.title: One-click full guest-data export (CSV + structured JSON) as a contractual, in-product promise
product.detail: Build a self-service, one-click full export of the entire guest CRM + reservation/booking history in both CSV and machine-readable JSON, available on every tier, plus easy IMPORT from competitors (TheFork/OpenTable/Fresha/CoverManager) to win switchers. Make 'you own it, export anytime, leave freely' a contractual guarantee surfaced in the UI. This directly weaponizes the biggest incumbent weaknesses: OpenTable amended its 2019 contract to BLOCK exporting guest data to rivals; Fresha cannot export encrypted card-on-file data (real migration friction); Tableo has NO documented one-click export (gap they admit); Treatwell makes export awkward post-termination; Quandoo's terms make the diner THEIR customer and the data vanishes at shutdown. Even friendly competitors (resOS, SimplyBook.me, FareHarbor, Octotable, Eat App) offer export but few make it one-click + import-to-switch. Pair the import path with the time-boxed Quandoo wind-down (shutting by Dec 2026) as a concrete migration-campaign feature.
product.evidence: OpenTable (contractual export block), Fresha (card data non-exportable), Tableo (no documented export), Treatwell/Quandoo (export friction/loss), ResDiary/FareHarbor (import-from-OpenTable proves switch demand)

product.title: Genuinely flat all-inclusive pricing engine: no booking caps, no per-cover/overage fees, no paid feature add-ons for essentials
product.detail: Architect the product so the published EUR99/EUR249 is the real price: NO monthly booking cap, NO per-reservation overage, and the obvious essentials (SMS/email reminders, deposits, white-label/custom domain, CRM, analytics, waitlist) bundled into the flat tiers rather than sold à-la-carte. This attacks two distinct, pervasive competitor failure modes. (1) Booking caps as a growth tax: SimplyBook.me HALTS bookings at 50/100/500/2,000 caps; resOS/Tablein/Octotable/Tableo/Eat App tier by cover volume; Tablein charges EUR0.67/reservation overage on Starter (a soft per-cover fee). (2) Add-on nickel-and-diming: Zappy (invoicing/GDPR/storage/extra location each extra), ResDiary (SMS/vouchers/POS each ~GBP10–35/mo), Eat App (widget/waitlist/CRM/payments all extra), SimplyBook.me (60+ paid 'custom features', costs reportedly 10x base), Mindbody/SevenRooms (per-module/per-location stacking). Make 'unlimited bookings, everything included, no surprise add-ons' an explicit, buildable product guarantee.
product.evidence: SimplyBook.me (hard booking caps), Tablein (EUR0.67/res overage), resOS/Tableo/Octotable/Eat App (volume-capped tiers), Zappy/ResDiary/Eat App/Mindbody/SevenRooms (add-on stacking)

product.title: Multi-vertical booking engine: first-class spa/wellness, boutique-hotel room-nights, and tours/activities — not just restaurant covers
product.detail: Build configurable booking primitives so one product genuinely handles restaurant covers/floor plans, spa/wellness appointments + treatment packages with session counting, boutique-hotel room-night availability, and tour/activity capacity (manifests, party-size, time slots) — the segments incumbents abandon. Restaurant-only players cannot follow: Tablein, resOS, Tableo, Octotable, Bouzon, CoverManager, Zenchef/Formitable, Superb, Eat App, ResDiary are all restaurant-only; hotel players (Cloudbeds, Mews, Little Hotelier) ignore restaurants/spas/tours; tour players (FareHarbor, Rezdy, Bokun) ignore restaurants/hotels/spas; spa players (Zappy, Fresha, Treatwell, Mindbody, Booksy) are commission/marketplace models weak on EN/international and boutique-hotel/tours fit. This breadth is GuestOverflow's structural moat — but it must be real in the product (vertical-specific flows: treatment forms/medical history for spa like Zappy; resource/manifest management for tours like FareHarbor/Rezdy; deposits for hotels). Prioritize spa + tours templates next, since those verticals are served only by commission-taking marketplaces.
product.evidence: Restaurant-only: Tablein/resOS/Tableo/Octotable/Bouzon/CoverManager/Zenchef/Superb/Eat App/ResDiary; hotel-only: Cloudbeds/Mews/Little Hotelier; tours-only: FareHarbor/Rezdy/Bokun; spa marketplaces: Zappy/Fresha/Treatwell/Mindbody/Booksy

product.title: Fast, lightweight, mobile-first guest booking flow with guest self-service (cancel/reschedule) and a 'next available slot' search
product.detail: Make widget load speed and checkout smoothness a measured, marketed product SLA, and let guests self-cancel/reschedule without phoning the venue, with no forced account creation and no 'find your venue in our app' step. Competitor failures make this a concrete win-list: FareHarbor's '14-second spinning wheel' dropped a venue's sales ~25%; Tablein has page-speed/load complaints and non-live data; Zappy's client app (3.3/5) won't let guests self-cancel/modify (forces phone calls) and guests can't even find their venue; Fresha/Booksy force a platform account; Formitable/Zenchef silently offer wrong-date alternatives causing accidental bookings and lack a 'next available 8pm' filter; Resy UI is 'clunky/outdated'; SevenRooms mobile is 'impossible to use'; CoverManager/ResDiary UIs called dated/'badly designed Excel'. Build: sub-second widget, 2-tap checkout, explicit 'next available slot' search, opt-in (not silent) alternative-date suggestions, and guest-facing cancel/reschedule links in every confirmation.
product.evidence: FareHarbor (slow checkout -25% sales), Zappy (no self-cancel, can't find venue), Formitable/Zenchef (silent wrong-date), Resy/SevenRooms/CoverManager/ResDiary (clunky/dated UI), Fresha/Booksy (forced account)

product.title: Floor/table + resource management and guest CRM with tags, history, notes, and segmentation — match the operator tooling incumbents are loved for
product.detail: Ship a clean, fast floor-plan/table view (smooth drag, pacing/capacity controls, shifts, waitlist) plus a guest CRM with profiles, visit history, spend, preferences/allergies, tags, and segmentation — because this is the software bar operators rate highly even when they hate the fees. OpenTable scores 4.4–4.7 on operator tooling; SevenRooms 4.7 on CRM/marketing; CoverManager 4.6–4.8 on floor ops. Without credible operator tooling, GuestOverflow reads as 'just a booking form' (the explicit weakness flagged against Zenchef and the gap in lightweight tools). Specifically include reservation-SOURCE tracking (website/Google/IG/PPC) and table-count-based limits — both were explicit unmet user requests in Eat App that 'win deals', and Tablein/Octotable also lack source attribution. Build the floor view smooth (Resy's drag is slow) and avoid 'maximum covers exceeded' phantom-capacity bugs reported in ResDiary, CoverManager, Booksy, and Little Hotelier.
product.evidence: OpenTable/SevenRooms/CoverManager (loved operator tooling = the bar), Eat App (source-tracking + table-count limits are unmet wins), ResDiary/CoverManager/Booksy/Little Hotelier (phantom-capacity bugs to avoid)

product.title: Reserve with Google integration (commission-free) and a venue-owned repeat-guest loyalty layer — the legitimate discovery answer without a marketplace
product.detail: Build native Reserve with Google integration (free direct bookings on high-intent search) plus a simple venue-OWNED loyalty/repeat-guest feature that benefits only that venue. This addresses the one thing GuestOverflow structurally lacks vs marketplaces — discovery demand — without betraying the no-marketplace promise. Reserve with Google is now table-stakes among the commission-free set (resOS, Tablein, Octotable, Tableo, Zenchef, CoverManager, Eat App, Little Hotelier, Square all use it). The loyalty layer counters cross-venue programs that actively steer YOUR guests to competitors: TheFork's Yums, OpenTable Dining Rewards, Resy/Amex Global Dining Access, Mindbody/Booksy/Fresha marketplaces that surface competitors to your own clients when you're full. Pair this in-product with the existing Google Business Profile optimization upsell so ex-marketplace venues don't feel they lost their discovery channel.
product.evidence: resOS/Tablein/Octotable/Tableo/Zenchef/CoverManager/Eat App/Square (Reserve with Google = table stakes), TheFork Yums / OpenTable Rewards / Resy-Amex / Mindbody-Booksy-Fresha (cross-venue programs that poach your guests)

product.title: In-product TheFork/OpenTable commission savings calculator and quarterly automated no-show/repeat-guest report
product.detail: Build two concrete product surfaces that operationalize the concierge positioning. (1) A savings calculator (covers/mo x avg ticket) showing marketplace commission spend vs GuestOverflow flat fee — TheFork costs ~EUR2–6/cover and EUR8k–35k/yr, OpenTable network covers $1,500–2,250/mo in fees alone, Fresha 20%/Treatwell 35% finder fees, FareHarbor/Rezdy/Bokun per-booking %. This is repeatedly cited as 'the single sharpest wedge'. (2) An automated quarterly performance report (no-show rate, repeat-guest %, peak times, revenue protected) that powers the founder-led quarterly review — a differentiator NO competitor offers in standard tiers (Eat App/Cloudbeds reserve quarterly reviews for Enterprise only; Superb does one-time onboarding only; everyone else is self-serve). The report data also feeds retention and proves ROI.
product.evidence: TheFork/OpenTable/Fresha/Treatwell/FareHarbor/Rezdy/Bokun (commission pain to quantify), Eat App/Cloudbeds (quarterly review is Enterprise-gated), Superb (one-time onboarding only)

product.title: Reliability + clean multi-tenant data isolation as an engineered, marketed feature; avoid POS/payment-processor lock-in
product.detail: Treat uptime, live-updating reservation data, and strict tenant data isolation as first-class engineering priorities, and stay payment-processor- and POS-agnostic (drop-in on the venue's existing stack, no rip-and-replace). Reliability failures are brand-killers across the set: Cloudbeds booking calendar offline 8+ weeks and imported an unrelated hotel's contacts; Mindbody lost client data on import; Little Hotelier double-booking bugs + 'slow, laggy, buggy'; Quandoo confirmed-but-not-received bookings; Tablein non-live data needing manual refresh; SevenRooms wrongly flags overbooked tables; Booksy phantom availability/double-booking. Lock-in via payments is the other trap to avoid: Superb/Cloudbeds/Mews/Little Hotelier subsidize cheap subscriptions with a payment take-rate and force their processor; Fresha/Square hold funds. Message 'no payment cut, keep your existing POS/processor, your money is never held' — a clean promise enabled by routing deposits through the venue's own Stripe.
product.evidence: Cloudbeds (8-wk outage + data-mix), Mindbody (data loss on import), Little Hotelier/Booksy/SevenRooms (double-booking/phantom capacity), Superb/Cloudbeds/Mews/Square/Fresha (payment lock-in/fund holds to avoid)

product.title: Consider an AI phone/WhatsApp booking assistant as a Premium/Custom differentiator
product.detail: Evaluate building (or integrating) a 24/7 AI voice + WhatsApp assistant that answers phone reservations, handles changes/cancellations in EN/PT, and sends confirmations — capturing phone demand independents otherwise miss. This is an emerging, genuinely-differentiating capability: CoverManager's Bot.ai is called 'a genuine differentiator', Octotable markets a 5-language AI voice assistant, Zenchef has 'AI Call', and Zappy/Eat App are adding AI/MCP layers. It fits the concierge/done-for-you ethos and the bilingual EN/PT positioning. Lower priority than the core white-label/no-show/data/pricing build, but a strong Premium-tier or Custom hook that keeps GuestOverflow from looking thin against the rapidly-AI-enabling incumbents — and notably, competitors gate or hide its pricing (Octotable's AI price is unpublished), so transparent inclusion would itself be a differentiator.
product.evidence: CoverManager Bot.ai (cited differentiator), Octotable + Zenchef (AI voice/call), Zappy/Eat App (emerging AI/MCP layers)

pricing.title: Keep flat commission-free as the core wedge — the ROI math wins decisively above ~50-80 covers/mo, so lead with a cover-volume break-even calculator
pricing.detail: Every marketplace competitor charges per-cover and that is their single most-hated trait. TheFork: ~EUR1.50-2.50/cover at EUR25 ticket, EUR2.50-4 at EUR45, up to ~EUR6 at EUR80, charged on EVERY cover including repeat guests (40-60% of bookings are repeats); illustrative bills EUR8,600-35,000/yr. OpenTable: $1.00-1.50/network cover + subscription, real bills $21.6k-31.8k/yr at ~2,500 covers/mo, plus a new 2% transaction fee. Our own page cites EUR2-3/cover as the marketplace cost. At EUR2.50/cover, Essential EUR99 breaks even at ~40 covers/mo and Premium EUR249 at ~100 covers/mo — trivially low for any real restaurant. The action: do NOT reprice the core flat model; instead build and front-load a side-by-side calculator (covers/mo x avg ticket x marketplace %) showing the flat line vs the rising commission line. Frame the sharpest wedge exactly as TheFork's pain: 'stop paying EUR2-6 every time YOUR regular comes back.' Quantify the no-show/repeat-guest tax that flat pricing eliminates.
pricing.evidence: TheFork, OpenTable, Resy (validates flat-no-commission), Quandoo (cautionary tale), Fresha (20% new-client fee), Treatwell (35%+VAT first booking), Mindbody (20% capped marketplace commission), Booksy (30% first visit), FareHarbor (~6% guest-paid), Rezdy (3%/booking), Bokun (1-1.5%)

pricing.title: Essential EUR99 is exposed at the entry point — multiple direct white-label, commission-free EU competitors undercut it heavily; add a lower/free funnel tier rather than dropping EUR99
pricing.detail: The closest direct analogues all sit well below EUR99 and several offer a true free tier, which is our biggest funnel gap. resOS: free 25 bookings/mo, Basic ~EUR47/mo (promo EUR24), same white-label/commission-free/EU/GDPR thesis. Tablein: floor EUR67/mo. Tableo: free up to 100 covers/mo, Organise ~EUR49/mo, 4.9 Capterra. Octotable: free 30 res/mo, paid EUR23-59/mo. Bouzon: ~EUR58.99/mo all-in INCLUDING a website. SimplyBook.me free + EUR11.90. A price-shopping independent lands on these first and our EUR99 reads as expensive on pure software. Recommendation: do NOT cut EUR99 (it is justified by concierge done-for-you build + quarterly reviews, which none of these offer). Instead (a) add a genuine free or no-card trial tier as top-of-funnel acquisition — this is the single most-replicated winning tactic among direct rivals — and (b) consider a 'Starter/self-serve' tier around EUR49-59 for venues that don't need full concierge, so the first quote is not lost on price. Reframe EUR99 explicitly as 'a partner who builds, installs and optimises it for you,' not software, because on software-only price we lose.
pricing.evidence: resOS, Tablein, Tableo, Octotable, Bouzon, SimplyBook.me, Zenchef/CoverManager (opaque but ~EUR99-109 starting)

pricing.title: Make all-in flat pricing genuinely all-in — bundle deposits/no-show, SMS, white-label branding and analytics, because 'commission-free' rivals nickel-and-dime via add-ons and that is a clean contrast
pricing.detail: The dominant hidden weakness across flat-fee competitors is add-on stacking that makes the headline price fiction. Zappy: invoicing, GDPR, extra location, storage, branded app (EUR29) all separate. ResDiary: SMS, gift vouchers, Order&Pay, even POS/PMS/CRM integrations each GBP10-35/mo. Eat App: widget EUR19, waitlist, CRM, payments, POS all extra. SimplyBook.me: branding removal + custom domain + SMS as paid 'custom features.' resOS: brand-match/white-label design, deposits, SMS, analytics all paid add-ons. Octotable/Tableo gate deposits/no-show to the top tier and meter SMS. Zenchef/Formitable add EUR0.99-2.99 per-transaction on prepaid flows on top of processor fees. Recommendation: keep Essential/Premium prices but guarantee 'no surprise add-on fees — the quoted price is the real price,' and ensure Premium genuinely bundles deposits + card guarantees + analytics + white-label + SMS allowance. Note deposits run via the venue's own Stripe (as Bouzon/resOS/Octotable do) so we take no payment cut — message 'no commission AND no payment take-rate,' which beats Superb/Mindbody/Square/Cloudbeds whose flat price is subsidised by a 0.99-3.6% processing margin.
pricing.evidence: Zappy, ResDiary, Eat App, SimplyBook.me, resOS, Octotable, Tableo, Zenchef, Formitable, Superb (0.99-1.29% payments), Mindbody (2.75-3.6%), Square (processing-funded), Cloudbeds (~1.4%+1% FX)

pricing.title: Publish prices openly — pricing opacity is the most common competitor friction and transparent EUR99/EUR249 is a conversion advantage worth defending
pricing.detail: Quote-only/sales-gated pricing is a near-universal complaint and trust killer: TheFork ('ask for pricing' + ~EUR400 setup), OpenTable (plans page gated), Resy (sales-gated, ~$249 floor), SevenRooms (~$499/feature, hidden), Zenchef ('most frustrating for small operators'), CoverManager (quote-only, 'costs not mentioned in demo'), ResDiary ($69 advertised vs GBP154 billed), Mindbody (hidden, creeping), Cloudbeds/Mews/Little Hotelier (all quote-only), Superb ('Customize' calculator), Tock/FareHarbor/Bokun (negotiated). Our published EUR99/EUR249 is already a differentiator — keep it loud ('see our price, no sales call required') and pair with 'no setup fee' to counter TheFork's ~EUR400, OpenTable's historical install fee, CoverManager's EUR200-500 onboarding, and Formitable/Zenchef 'unexplainable startup fees.'
pricing.evidence: TheFork, OpenTable, Resy, SevenRooms, Zenchef, CoverManager, ResDiary, Mindbody, Cloudbeds, Mews, Little Hotelier, Superb, Tock, FareHarbor, Bokun

pricing.title: Reposition the EUR99/EUR249 gap and add a Custom/multi-location tier with explicit, transparent pricing — the upper tier is well-placed but the multi-location story is underspecified
pricing.detail: Premium EUR249 sits competitively for what it bundles: it undercuts Resy (~$249 floor, US-only, not white-label), SevenRooms (~$499/feature), Tock ($199-699), Eat App Pro (~EUR360 before add-ons), and is roughly level with Zenchef/CoverManager mid-tiers while including concierge they lack. So EUR249 needs no cut. The gap to address is the top end: SevenRooms/Mindbody/Cloudbeds/Mews win growing/multi-location venues on breadth, and per-location pricing (Mindbody, Zenchef, Little Hotelier, Cloudbeds) 'punishes growth' — a complaint we can exploit. Recommendation: keep Custom tier but publish a clear starting price and a flat-per-venue (not per-cover, not per-location-penalty) structure for 2nd+ locations, mirroring Tablein's multi-location discount and beating Mindbody's full-base-rate-per-location. This retains growing accounts that would otherwise be SevenRooms upsell targets.
pricing.evidence: Resy, SevenRooms, Tock, Eat App, Zenchef, CoverManager, Mindbody, Cloudbeds, Mews, Little Hotelier, Tablein

pricing.title: Hold the no-lock-in / no-questions-exit + founding offer — contract lock-in and exit friction are top competitor churn drivers and cost nothing to keep as a pricing-trust lever
pricing.detail: Lock-in horror stories are pervasive and directly validate our model: OpenTable 1-yr auto-renew; Resy/Mews 2-yr contracts; Tock high-pressure sales; Zenchef/Formitable annual auto-renew with strict cancellation traps ('forced to pay a full year'); ResDiary 3-month minimum; Quandoo rolling annual + 3-month notice + 'debits after you quit'; Mindbody 12-mo lock-in with refused cancellations + fund holds; Little Hotelier reported 14-month surprise bill (~$1,900); Cloudbeds mid-contract +30% hikes; Bokun 3 unilateral price changes in 2 years. Our 'no-questions exit' + 3-months-half-price founding offer is a concrete pricing-trust weapon — keep it prominent and add a written 'we never raise your rate out from under you / grandfather existing customers' commitment to counter Cloudbeds/Bokun price-hike anger. Also add a risk-free trial (no card) to match the freemium funnel rivals (resOS, Tableo, Octotable, SimplyBook.me, Fresha/Booksy historically) use to win cautious owners.
pricing.evidence: OpenTable, Resy, Mews, Tock, Zenchef, Formitable, ResDiary, Quandoo, Mindbody, Little Hotelier, Cloudbeds, Bokun, resOS, Tableo, Octotable

pricing.title: For spa/wellness, do NOT chase Zappy's EUR16-20 entry price head-on — justify the premium with concierge + no-show protection + true on-domain white-label
pricing.detail: In the PT spa/wellness segment the incumbent Zappy massively undercuts us (STAR EUR16-20/mo for a solo provider, seat-based to EUR119), and Booksy ($29.99 + $20/seat), Fresha (~$15-20 + 20% new-client), Mindbody (per-location, marketplace 20%), Treatwell (EUR35 + 35% finder fee) all anchor low or marketplace-funded. Competing on headline seat price is a losing game and seat-based pricing is not our model. Recommendation: keep flat per-venue pricing (a genuine advantage vs per-seat that 'punishes a 5-chair shop' — Booksy ~$110/mo, Zappy/Mindbody scaling) and justify EUR99+ with done-for-you setup, deposits/card-guarantee no-show protection (Zappy's booking flow lacks it; Fresha/Treatwell/Booksy have resented punitive versions), guest self-service (Zappy app 3.3/5, can't self-cancel), and true on-domain white-label (vs Zappy's shared 'My Zappy' app / EUR29 branded upsell). Target restaurants/hotels/tours where these spa incumbents are absent before fighting Zappy on price.

pricing.title: Counter the one honest gap — flat pricing generates zero discovery demand — by bundling/upselling Google Business Profile optimization so the ROI story isn't undercut by 'where do new guests come from'
pricing.detail: Marketplaces (TheFork, OpenTable, Fresha, Treatwell, Quandoo, FareHarbor/Bokun via Viator) justify their per-cover fees with real net-new discovery demand we cannot replicate. The flat-vs-commission ROI pitch only fully closes for venues that already have their own traffic/walk-ins/repeats. Recommendation: in pricing/packaging, explicitly position GO as 'own your direct + repeat traffic commission-free' and attach our existing Google Business Profile optimization + website builds as the legitimate discovery answer (mirroring Bouzon's bundled-website-at-half-CoverManager angle and Octotable/Cloudbeds' Reserve-with-Google). Consider a 'website + bookings' all-in package to compete on Bouzon's ~EUR59 headline and to neutralise the 'no marketplace = no leads' objection during the sale. Optionally add commission-free Reserve-with-Google as a standard channel so the flat plan still captures high-intent search traffic.
pricing.evidence: TheFork, OpenTable, Fresha, Treatwell, Quandoo, FareHarbor, Bokun, Resy/Amex, Bouzon, Octotable, Cloudbeds
